rating 2.0. Disappointing price/deal but fun Horde (even w/poor skill implementation) Disappointing overall, I give it 2 stars for the average player and 3 for the fan of story and/or horde (vs, I leave to the hardcore vs people).

Season pass was/remains a joke, they put map limits in to attempt to justify its existence. The $$$ price and just throwing card packs at the game owner = LAME.

Enjoying it with my wife since GoW3, it is very unfriendly to 2 or more people same xbox. Preorder bonus only sole owner, so I could play Marcus but no Ana for wife. All cards only for owner too - why not ANY core skins and abilities, bounties shared, just dumping it on the profile installed? Those preorder ones can't be made meaning you will generally never have any other player having access to them. GoW3 and even GoW:J were so much more giving with shared content and unlockable in game skins and content on same Xbox.

If you're still a Horde or Story fan I think you might get a 3 out of it, even tho former short and latter makes you play a RNG slot game they'd rather get $$$ from you for. With bad odds and a lot of duplicates and the fact you cannot create a card you haven't yet received (something the Dev doesn't talk about even in their mini tutorial) it is a challenge and a haul to play how you want. No mutators here - grind and pray to RNGesus.

After you big initial purchase (up to $100 premium) you feel like you're playing a freemium with all the for sale only options - halloween, esports, and 'run the jewels' whomever they are (sorry, shrug) all using real $$ and no in game, AND first 2 maps we payed for being GoW3 remakes, there has been a lot of backlash. The 10th anniversay was a good event and supposedly christmas event will have the ability to purch with credits, we'll see.

So there remains some goofy things (eg private horde stays online and will afk kick, private vs cant get xp but that's the only way to use your bought maps? wtf...) and some unfortunate design choices (bizarro horde progression, no unlockables through gameplay at all).

I'm also still a bit surprised bounties are something you buy in game/real $, just have quests/bounties available like every other game I've enjoyed. Oh the lameness...

Still if you like the old school.. well you'd probably be better served just playing those games. That tagline of 'best since GoW1' is such a joke, sorry. I'd give them best since GoW:J.. lol.. or maybe those two go head to head. It'd be close honestly, so far I'd give it to GoW:J.

Such potential but money grab moves really gutted this one. See how it develops but holiday buyer beware. I already saw a month after release the price on the ultimate edition drop to the regular edition, that tells you something. At least you'll get a better deal than us saps who wanted to support the new game and TC and gave them the benefit of the doubt. Oops. We know now.

rating 5.0. A solid edition and new start to Gears Well, I admit that I was once a really hardcore fan of the Halo series. Then Microsoft gave 343 full reign of it and it's jumped the shark just about every time, or run straight into its teeth. I'm very pleased to say, Gears of War 4 does a DRAMATICALLY better job in this regard.

Now, to be fair, it's very definitely an introduction to the new series and management, for better and worse. Certain elements are a little... forced, but that should be nothing new to Gears fans: there's an unusual tendency for architects to have designed a lot of strangely waist-high little structures in their buildings, somehow deep holes can be plugged by grenades, and everything can be chainsawed except the things that should be.

But, on the other hand, the characters are solid, the dialogue is fantastic in purest Gears tradition, and they actually manage to convey some emotional impact without being heavy-handed on relying on expanded universe lore that most players will never know (looking at YOU, 343...). The multiplayer is still Gears, which I've never been a fan of, but plenty of people enjoy it. I came back more for the Horde mode, and it's been worth it so far, although I miss some of the more absurd enemies and panic that came in Gears 3.

The single biggest gripe I have about it is that you only have two-player co-op in campaign. I understand, having completed it, why they chose to do that, but they easily could have allowed for more players, at least most of the time. Ultimately though, I'd say I prefer having NPCs doing more menial tasks (such as holding down a switch) that would have otherwise been done by a player.

Overall, if you're already a Gears fan, you're probably going to love this game. If you're not, I don't think there's anything here that will draw you in.

current_product_reviews:
rating 1.0. Shameless. Thanks, Coalition... DO NOT BUY THIS EDITION. So yeah, the cover shooting is fine/fun, but this is one shameless cash-grab... With somehow WORSE graphics than Ultimate.

Be wary, some spoilers below so read on at your own discretion.

The campaign is a pretty big disappoint to say the least. Yes, the combat is solid (as Gears has always been) and it thankfully went back to the old control scheme instead of Judgment's butchery of it. However, while the action is once again entertaining, plot-wise, it's a travesty of storytelling with large amounts of holes, questions that the developers don't even bother answering (let alone attempting to give hints on) and imperative plot points for understanding the new Sera, new characters and The Swarm that are completely swept under the rug. Of course, the reasoning behind this is that The Coalition doesn't want to tell a great narrative now; they want to franchise it for as long as possible and come up with resolutions as they're allowed to make sequels. Guys, a constant slew of unaddressed questions is not a story and a cliffhanger is not an ending (it should only be part of). And speaking of the "ending"; it's terrible. It feels like the game is just getting started (especially when you consider where the finale takes place) and then, after a 2-3 minute cutscene, it cuts to black with next to nothing having been accomplished and learned. There's not even a main villain or anything (the guy it sets up to be a primary foe is dispatched earlier on and doesn't even have a name in the game) What's even more shameless is that, to get information on the narrative (beyond, you know, the damn game), you have to go online to something like Wiki as the game doesn't even do its job of informing you. For anything more, you have to wait another decade (for two more games plus DLC) to just understand base information that they should have been telling you now like:

-Why does JD hate Marcus and why did he seemingly abandon him? (of course, we could infer, but tell us what happened!)
-Why does JD hate Baird?
-What happened to Anya and how did she die?
-What is Jinn's motive and why does she just disappear during the last act?
-Why do JD and the outsiders hate Jinn and their seemingly peaceful society, but still want to loot and attack their settlements?
-Why in the world would Reyna's mom lock she, JD and Del in a shed when The Swarm attack AFTER she just had you save the ENTIRE village from a DeeBee attack? These are your best proven fighters! (Okay, so that was just really bad writing/a plot hole, but still).
-How can the Swarm hack robots with quills? And if they can, why aren't they hacking the DeeBees later in the game instead of fighting them? Seems like a waste of a randomly-generated enemy trait.

These are just a minimal amount of major plot threads that are just shrugged off in favor of further franchising, like Halo 5. And no, to everyone gibbering away that they're just setting up for the DLC and sequel and yadd-yadda-yadda----NO. This is flat-out bad writing and bad storytelling. Give us something!

The Swarm are also kinda just dumb. They don't look interesting, hardly have any diversity and the "Scions" just look nonthreatening with their too-dimwitted/human faces. There's potential for them, but the game gives no depth to them or their purpose other than harvesting.

The characters are also grating. JD is alright, but the "humorous" dialogue is near non-stop and most of it is the trio not taking anything seriously, making jokes and speaking solely in sarcasm. Yes, the original games had this, but in this one, it's so incessant and so cringe-worthy that it's just obnoxious. There's simply a time and place for everything and this one has these infants copping jokes around a mutilated body they just discovered. That and they act like brats and treat Marcus and Baird like crap. Reyna even has the audacity to speak down to Marcus at one scene where all he's trying to do is talk sense.

Multiplayer is alright but it doesn't 'feel' like Gears of War combat. It feels like a Call of Duty interpretation. Again, it does Gears' gameplay, but in a Call of Duty-esque way. Now, everything's about loadouts, temporary cards you can equip and "zombie" characters (like COD) that look pretty stupid. I really liked unlocking the character models in old Gears games and the ones in this are just lazy (color-splattered? Seriously? Gimme a Theron, please). Worse, multiplayer is utterly mutilated with micro-transactions. There are seriously 3 forms of currency and getting credits isn't a grind, it's an absolute slog. Worse, when you actually get enough credits to buy one of the packs, it's completely random whether or not you'll receive anything worthwhile. 99% of the time, you receive the lamest gun skins or experience cards--and even doubles at that in the SAME PACK! Even worse (yup, it does get worse) I now have doubles (and even one TRIPLE) of cards that the game won't let me "scrap" for currency to buy another skin instead. Now, I have decks of cards that are literally unusable. It's revolting, it's disgusting and it's even gambling. They literally force you to gamble on stupid slots. Funny, considering all those gamers who got busted on COD for gambling because they were actually EARNING money by playing--and the ol' government came down on them for. BUT NO, it's totally okay for AAA game companies to extort and pocket money if they're the ones forcing gamers to gamble. Insane. Also, the season pass was utterly worthless as it doesn't come with ANYTHING that would/should come in a season pass. Oh, the new characters/Run the Jewels box or the MLG loot box??? NOPE! Gotta pay extra even though you already shelled out $100 (not that I care for the character models, but still, it's principle). Of the loot I got with the $100 edition, I got nothing worthwhile but several "pine" skins for guns that look as stupid as they sound. So what do you get with this edition? Kinda-sorta early access (one week before they're released for free) to multiplayer maps that can only be played on the developer mode.

As for guns, the DeeBee weapons are actually the most fun (the Overkill and Embar). However, most of the time, I just used the original lineup of guns as I feel they're more effective. The Dropshot is annoying as it sometimes works really well and sometimes explodes directly next to someone and does... Nothing. The Buzzkill is stupid fun, but like the Dropshot I never bothered to use it beyond its introductory use (as it's slow to start firing and needs to be used from a static position). The Markza is okay, but when you already have a sniper and lancer/hammerburst, it's kinda void.

Music is also severely lacking when it should be used during cutscenes. One "emotional" moment is rendered "meh" by the complete lack of a sympathetic song (think The Coalition trying to replicate the Maria scene from Gears 2 but without memorable music and/or emotional investment from the player--as again--it's taken no time to explain why we should care about these new characters).

As a fanatic of all things Gears (except that crap one Judgment), I find Gears 4 to be decent mindless fun, but a slap in the face as well for those who have supported the series. It's clear the people involved (and still are from previous games) needed Cliffy B. to guide them and really don't understand their fan base nor what makes a game great. It's a AAA title all the way in that it's a microtransaction-filled cash-grab made for the general public first and fans secondly.
other_product_reviews:
rating 2.0. It's like Star Fox... If You Removed Everything Good About It As most should know, this game looks PHENOMENAL. The background graphics (especially the first level) truly showcase the graphical power of the GBA. That, and the music is cheesy, 8-bit-sounding fun. I love shooters akin to R-Type, Gradius, Iridion 2 (which I oddly played prior to this one), but Iridon 3-D truly falls shot due to its unforgiving difficulty and poor hit detection.

Enemies take WAY too many hits to kill (if you can manage to at all) and none of the weapon upgrades do anything (at least I couldn’t find any difference between them other than the way they looked) to improve your ship and the bosses are another issue altogether. Most of them require you to hit a weak point. The problem? The only way to hit said weak point is position your ship directly in front of oncoming fire. Thus, you’re taking the same amount of damage you’re dealing. And no, there are no saves. You acquire a password after each beaten level, however, you start with the same lives you had at the time of that password acquisition. So if you beat the second level with one life to spare, that’s all you get for the rest of the game. Good luck.

It’s also difficult to dodge oncoming fire because of how tiny the enemy blasts are and due to the fact that the hit detection is just terrible. Even when you think you’ve dodged something you haven’t. Worse is that there’s simply too much going on in some levels to even decipher between environment, enemy, or both (the comet level is the worst).

The only fun encounter I had was the final boss fight that actually requires attention to movement and dodging (practically everything else just fills the screen with blasts).

Iridion 2 fixed practically every issue with Iridion 3-D, so I recommend playing that one over this one.

rating 4.0. "I was dodging bullets like raindrops"... Having played the previous iterations of the Max Payne saga and having watched the abhorrent film adaptation, I decided to bust out my GBA and give this handheld version a go. I was pleasantly surprised by this little variant’s ability to capture the best elements of Max Payne’s dark world into an on-the-run adventure.

Honestly, the gameplay is one thing, but the technicalities herein are truly outstanding for a GBA title. Sound design and top-notch voice-work (forgiving some of the over-eager actors, of course) accompany fantastic 3-D animations around surprisingly interactive isometric environments. That, and bullet-time has been thankfully incorporated to a near-perfect degree. The gunplay itself is quite fun (if extremely simple) for what it is and splattering a baddie against the wall with a sawed-off never gets old. Be advised though, despite the mature rating, much of Max’s disturbing content has been removed (the baby’s death and bloody crib, practically all of the course language and even the grisly dream sequences). I’m not sure why they did this as—even on a GBA—it’s an M-rated game and children shouldn’t be playing this. Censoring it just seems a bit pointless when you still have the blood, violence and drug use (painkillers).

The only real negative is that, especially toward the end, enemies become far more infuriating due to the fact that they can shoot you from off-screen (before you even know that they’re there. The other problem is that they never have to reload, thus, enemies never stop firing. The main problem with this is during the final level in which you must run up a staircase. Near the top is a single, dual-wielding enemy who shoots down at you and literally kills you within half-a-second (that’s not hyperbole). I’m not sure if it’s a glitch, but the game WILL NOT allow you to shoot at him from below and he CAN ONLY be killed if you run directly at him and shoot on the same step that he’s on (he's a normal bad-guy not a boss) Even bullet-dodging doesn’t work as again, due to a programming error, he can ONLY be hit if you’re standing on the same step as he is. Before this point, you could kill any enemy on a staircase with ease; here however, the unfairness is just staggering. For anyone else that gets stuck right here, immediately activate bullet-time when you enter the staircase and run up the stairs while simultaneously pressing the “heal” button and “fire” button. None of the bullets will hit him, but they will detonate a proximity mine behind the enemy (which doesn’t hurt him, but will kill you) and the constant healing will ensure you can make it to his step and where you can finally kill him with a sliver of health left (as he will literally be draining your health while you sprint toward him). This is provided you have enough painkillers and bullet-time stored. Otherwise, I don’t know how you’d best him.

Beyond that single enemy, the other issue is that there’s no in-game assistance available, so many a time, after clearing a level of villains, I was wondering around what to do only to find that if I backtracked far enough, there was one door (of many) or one button (of many) that—while inactive before—were now active/unlocked for no discernible reason. Some sort of map/identification for objectives such as this would’ve been helpful and eliminated a lot of unnecessary confusion.

Beyond those two gripes, I was extremely pleased with just how polished and ambitious this little recreation was and unlike many a port can actually recommend this to fellow GBA players.

rating 1.0. Not even close! Slippery, chap plastic. Don't buy The tools are the only thing worth getting in this package. I bought Xbox one thumb sticks for my DS4 sometime last year, and they were amazing. I tried to buy them again, but they were discontinued, and these are the closest model.

rating 5.0. Great thumbsticks for Xbox One controller The product was delivered in a nice and tidy box. Professional presentation so I made sure to put a picture of it as well. Some people complained it was the wrong product or packaged poorly. They either are reviewing someone else or this company listened to the complaints. I was really impressed. Obviously, it's a cheaper product so the parts are just plastic moldings that aren't gonna last you 10 uses. But I was able to easily get through my two controls without the parts breaking on me. rating 1.0. Just plastic These might look like the original but these feel hard plastic all around rather than the rubber texture the official ones use.

rating 1.0. 5 Star to 1 Star Review in 1 Week! I play FIFA competitively and the original Sony PS4 thumbstick material tends to degrade to peeling within a few weeks. I play so much my left thumb is like sandpaper and quickly breaks down the soft rubber on all of the different thumbsticks I’ve used as replacements.  These are made of a tougher, gritty plastic and there is no bonded rubber that can break down and peel. It’s a solid piece of plastic that has a sandpaper-like texture from the bottom side of the thumbstick head all the way around to the top, leaving a slightly concave top that is great for minuscule movements. I’ve purchased countless replacements and none are as rugged as these. The stick also sits slightly higher than default and, therefore, appears to have a slightly increased turning radius. FPS and racing game enthusiasts may notice an improvement in movement with this design as it is generally offered in expensive custom joysticks. If this thumbstick somehow degrades, then I will edit this review. UPDATE: Since installing these thumbsticks on 4 PS4 controllers a week ago, I now have 2 with sticky or nearly nonfunctional joysticks. I only install these replacements on the left side because that’s the only side that degrades with heavy use. I believe there is a design flaw that causes these thumbsticks to bind/stick within the controller housing or the extra turning radius, as mentioned above, puts too much strain on the analog stick mechanism. After replacing 2 of these new thumbsticks, I have found one controller now much more functional in all movement directions and 1 controller has broken internal parts, probably due to the extra movement these thumbsticks provide. What initially seemed like a perfect design turned out to be a very bad one. The rugged material IS perfect, but the design needs to be modified to more closely match the engineering specifications of the original Sony controller. I do not recommend.

rating 5.0. Great replacement thumbsticks for Xbox One The thumbsticks on my Xbox One S controller have worn out over time, and have recently even begun to tear and rip on the edges. Instead of going out and spending $60 on a new controller, I decided to purchase these replacement thumbsticks.

Installation was very easy, the most difficult part was removing the plastic plates where your hands grip the controller (the very first step of disassembly) though that wasn't very difficult because I had opened up the controller ahead of time just to make sure I could actually take it apart. Once I got inside the controller, I decided to clean everything up (might as well, right?) and then I put on the new thumbsticks. The new thumbsticks are very grippy and nice. At first, they might feel like hard plastic, but that is just because they are so very new that the rubber hasn't had time to soften up yet.

I highly recommend these Tomsin replacement thumbsticks as an easy and cheap way to pump out a few more years of life from your aging game controllers. Perhaps the money you save can be put towards an Elite Controller in the future, that is what I am doing.

input:
rating 5.0. Love it Final Fantasy Type-0 was so much better than I expected.
While it wasn’t like older Final Fantasy’s, it still had great Final Fantasy elements. I have been in love with Final Fantasy games my whole life, starting with VII. I honestly stopped liking them after X sadly. I just couldn’t get on board with the new fighting style and it made it way less fun for me. Type 0 was a great blend of new and older methods. I actually really loved the fighting in this one. I loved the FF methods of the kupos and the chocobos. The missions was different along with the time limit before them, but I didn’t mind it.
I liked having all the different characters, though it did make it harder to level all of them up and it was harder to have many more choices in characters. I just took them all through training to see which ones I enjoyed fighting with the most. It worried me that I was missing storylines if I didn’t go to all the different places, but I think so far I’m managing just fine.
All in all, I do recommend this game. It has been really fun so far and it’s not too hard at all. It’s a pretty good game and really addicting. My niece and nephew love watching it with me and they enjoy playing it as well, though they aren’t too great at it.
I chose one day shipping to get it on my day off but I think they didn’t take care of it in shipping since they were rushing because when it came to me the case had been completely smashed. It cut my finger when I opened it. The front had a crack all through it, the corner had broken off, and the back was split as well. Miraculously the disk survived and didn’t have one scratch on it so I was still able to play right away. So the packaging wasn’t the best. The customer service was great though and gave me a 30% refund right away.

rating 4.0. A very different type of Final Fantasy. FF Type-0 HD used to be a PSP game which was only released in Japan in 2011. There was hope to it being released in the US, however this never came to be. Fortunately we got the game in the form of a remaster. First things first, this game is bloody. It's got the M rating for a reason and is a much darker Final Fantasy game. The stables of the series such as magic, chocobos, summons(eidolons), character classes, music, etc all return.

One of the standouts for me, and is usually a big draw to the FF series, is the outstanding music. From the intro, I got the song We Have Arrived stuck in my head. It plays out during a variety of missions and other areas of the games and is one of the most enjoyable themes in a game.

The next plus which is obviously the biggest - combat. The combat system to the game is fast paced, fluid, and contains a large variety. You get 14 members that you can interchange during missions(of if a member dies you rotate them out) Each member has their own type of weapon/skill set which resemble traditional FF jobs. You map commands to the X, Square, and Triangle buttons. X being attack while the other being magic or skill attacks. O being your guard/defense ability such as Curaga/Block/Wall/etc. You mix these with your character while attacking to gain the edge on your opponents in real time. The ability to change between your 3 active party members on the fly also adds to combat. There is so much variety and challenge that I rarely found myself trying to avoid a conflict.

The opening scenes were some of the most dramatic and powerful that I have seen in an FF to date. I don't want to spoil it but something happens that really tugs at a diehard FF fans heartstrings! All of the cinematics are done quite well, unfortunately during in-game engine scenes some NPC models do not appear to have been given as much attention. The overall world itself gives a new take to the FF mythos and it is a joy to learn more about it and the characters you are playing.

Touching on it a little already, the only drawback is the remastering itself. Some character models are detailed well enough that they feel like at least a PS3/PS4 game, while others definitely feel PS1/PS2 area as they weren't touch on much from their PSP version. This can be a bit jarring. Another factor is the heavy motion blur. I didn't have a problem with it but when I showed the game to a few friends they commented on it that it was making them feel a little disoriented. In addition, the dub is very hit or miss. Some scenes/actors pull it off quite well, when others are very typical of an English dub to where they are over the top when it is not needed.

Overall, this game has been a lot of fun(which is at the end of the day what games are about!), if you are an FF fan or like action RPGs this is definitely one to try out if you can get past some minor graphical missteps and suffer through the motion blur.

PRO:

- Amazing Soundtrack
- Really fun and engaging Combat
- Highly Detailed Cutscenes
- Interesting World/Characters

CON:

- Some character models and textures are really rough(due to it being originally a PSP game)
- Bland environments in some areas(again due to original nature of sofware)
- Voice Acting sometime is cringeworthy(English Dub)
- Motion Blur could affect some.
rating 1.0. One Star This game really isn't that much fun.  Maybe just not my style though.

rating 2.0. The FF15 demo is better than the Type-0 HD game Meh.

I've been a huge fan of the Final Fantasy series since last century. Even the worst FF game has been very good.

That streak has come to an end.

As with most games, the first thing I did was turn down the music/soundtrack so I could better hear the dialog and sound effects. Even so, there is non-stop screaming and bombs in the background even when you're not in a battle.

Past Final Fantasy games have allowed you to explore, and when it's time for a battle, you could use strategy. Not here. Here you just mash buttons as fast as you can, then move to the next area--no faster! Now mash buttons again. Oh, you died.

The saving grace for this game is the playable demo for FF 15. While I didn't ever figure out the battle system, the graphics were amazing, the world layout non-linear, the dialog interesting, and the menus intuitive. I'm very much looking forward to the full FF15 game.

rating 2.0. R.I.P to the Best Franchise So where do I begin?? They have taken away the classic item looting, level grinding, skill learning game that we all loved. The battle system is one where you can just smash buttons until the victory screen appears. I literally have been playing Diablo III on my computer at the same time since I can just hit random buttons without even having to avoid attacks. My biggest complaint over the last few years, as a whole, is the battle system. Being able to sync characters together by casting separate skills, spells, or attacks at once was what truly propelled this series into the strategic combat RPG that won the hearts of its fans. I'm sad to say, unless they remake the original games, this will probably be the last Final Fantasy that I buy...

What does this game do well?
The cinematic screen and overall graphics are amazing. That's literally about it. The FF series has moved away from making games that are fun to play, and absorb you into the world, into making games that are pretty and "revolutionary". I understand the need to change games in fear of them becoming stale or extinct, but, the battle system MUST go back to the way it was. The system evolved from FF - FF X and the game has not been as clever or good since then.

But hey, don't let my love of the classic franchise hurt your opinions in any way. I'm sure some people love this new game series

rating 1.0. Boring game, poor controls, feels like work, and I love most Final Fantasy titles. Keep in mind I'm writing this from the point of view of someone that owns almost every Final Fantasy game. I've played every single one all the way through and really enjoyed almost all of them. Those that I weren't thrilled by (Final Fantasy XIII Lightning Returns etc.) I still played all the way through. This game is terrible. I was hoping for a Final Fantasy Tactics type experience (given the description of the game), but I really think it's bad all around.

Negatives:

- Graphics are poor, yes it's a PSP remake, but knowing this going in I still was disappointed.

- Adjusting the Camera Angles are a nightmare, poor controls in general, I cringe when I open menu's because of how painful the controls are. Clearly something is wrong when something like Final Fantasy I controls are a dream compared to this.

-Story Line: Most people say the story starts off strong and then gets weaker and complain about random jumps in the storyline without much explanation. I thought the story started off bad and just got worse as I went through it.

Gameplay: This is why I stopped playing the game after 15-20 hours or so. I wanted to enjoy it, I really did. I've never given up on a Final Fantasy game before, but navigating the menus just felt like work, and I wasn't getting paid to play this game. You have controls of 14 people i think it is. This should be fun getting to give different skills and items to 14 different people, but it isn't. I don't know how to describe why exactly except to say that it feels overwhelming and the menus are clunky. I'm the type of person that has spent numerous hours on strategy games like "Ogre Battle" etc, so I usually like controlling lots of characters.

Conclusion: Stay far away unless you're so desperate for a new Final Fantasy game that you want it to hurt.

rating 2.0. Unless you're okay with spending $60 dollars for the demo of an actual good game, this probably isn't worth your money Originally reviewed by me at somnambulant-gamer.com:

Normally I'm the kind of person who, if I don't have anything positive to say about a game, just doesn't say anything. It's not really worth my time to write about a game I don't care about, right? But Final Fantasy Type-0 is terrible, and I really feel it's only fair to let you know. This game was actually so bad, I had to wait a day to write about it, because yesterday afternoon I actually wanted to title this post "Type 0: a crappy game from crappy people who want you to waste your money on crappy things". My grumpiness was compounded somewhat by the fact that all the big review sites like IGN and Gamespot were giving this game glowing reviews that it frankly didn't deserve, which led to me wasting money. Okay, enough venting, let me get into this.

The game starts out with a cinematic that was probably only 15 to 20 minutes long, but felt like 3 hours. It was atrocious, disjointed, and fully 3/4 of the cinematic is spent on the tragic death of a character I can only assume you're supposed to care about, because you have absolutely no idea who he is at this point. The graphics look to be polished versions of something you might expect to see on a hand-held device that's a generation or two out of date (because they are), which isn't too terrible, except in a few instances where you've got characters with highly detailed faces and wafting individual strands of hair who still have blocky shovel hands reminiscent of World of Warcraft characters prior to the Warlords of Draenor update. Apparently, the game is about 4 countries (that maybe have crystals?) that are represented by signature beasts: a white tiger, an azure dragon, a red phoenix, and a black tortoise. Fans of the franchise will recognize the references to (and overt appearances of) staples such as the Bahamut and Phoenix eidolons. The country with the tigers has magitek machines, and apparently they go to war against the country with the phoenix, which for reasons inadequately explained until many, many hours later leads them to attack the stronghold of the country with the azure dragon.

You're dropped into this war as three kids who just saw another kid (presumably one of their number based on the uniform and pretty much nothing else at this point) die after struggling to find them (with no other apparent purpose) and apparently being too wounded to be healed by magic despite the fact that it takes him fully ten freaking minutes of cinematics after the attempt to finally kick the bucket, at which point his chocobo (big two-legged riding bird, staple of the franchise), who's seemed dead for about eight of those ten minutes, gives a mournful cry in a blatantly manipulative attempt to evoke an emotion the game hasn't earned. Then you get to spend about an hour and some change running through the city being subjected to some of the most boring and uninspired combat and maps you've seen in the last decade. The combat itself is brutally disappointing, reminiscent of the repetitive slogs seen in games like Devil May Cry where you hack and slash your way through dozens of faceless mooks over and over, without the inspired combo systems and flashy design that made that game a success. There's lots of small issues interspersed through the combat system as well, including clunky targeting with a mind of its own, frequent (if fairly short) load times, and one of my personal pet peeves - camera angles that are a greater hindrance to your party's advancement than the actual enemies.

You get to control three (of an eventual fourteen) characters during this section, a character who uses magical cards to attack and perform other maneuvers (a cool and almost successful call-back to fan-favorites of the franchise like Setzer in Final Fantasy III / VI), one who uses a spear and a jump attack (the "dragoon"), and a character who fights with a chinese longsword. Each character has all of four combat options available at this point, a standard attack, their special trick (card shuffling, jumping, and an AoE aura effect), one spell of a given element, and their defensive option. The combat has been compared to Crisis Core by several sites, but I really feel that does a disservice to Crisis Core, which had a robust combat interface that flowed seamlessly between the combat and exploration phases and utilized a highly customizable materia system that was actually pretty advanced for its time.

I feel like I have to find some redeeming quality in the game at this point... Okay, there's two things. The first, is that the cinematics, while climactically disjointed and painfully, brutally boring, go to places that are out of the norm for the franchise, in a good way. You'll see Square-Enix referring to this game as a "more mature" entry. While the actual story-line is about as mature as a twelve year writing his first piece of fan fiction, what this is really referencing is the fact that in this war-time environment, we actually see spurts of blood when someone is shot or stabbed, soldiers ruthlessly gunning down or bayoneting enemy wizards and civilians, etc. If some real character and story development had been married to a cohesive plotline and a modern combat interface, this could have been an amazing step for the Final Fantasy franchise, whose original fans are now at an age where they have kids, or even grandkids, of their own.

The second redeeming quality is that the game comes with a playable demo of Final Fantasy XV, an actual next-gen game set in the same world. Final Fantasy XV seems to be set to offer many of the things this game lacks: strong character development, a cohesive story, true next-gen graphics, and a robust combat system. While obviously still in the developmental stage, this fledgling game helped to restore some of the faith and hope that the actual game it was packaged with sucked out of me like some kind of vampiric leech that feeds on joy and nostalgia. Unfortunately, at the end of the day Type-0 isn't really a game; it's an advertisement for the actual game that they expect you to pay $60 for.

rating 5.0. Final Fantasy XV Episode Duscae Review I'll be honest. I bought this solely to play Episode Duscae. So this review is for the included demo. No spoilers. On a whole I think XV very well has a chance of being the best FF title ever made. And I am one of those hardcore FFVII fans. So anyway, after playing through the demo twice now, here are my thoughts:

PROS

1) JAPANESE AUDIO WITH ENGLISH SUBTITLES

2) I LOVE the addition of the jump and dash comands.

3) The fighting style is absolutely amazing.

4) "Listening" in on conversations instead of having people speaking at all times. I like not missing things because I wasn't paying attention. Even if it is less "real".

5) The "return to rest point" menu option.

6) Status boosts

7) The fact that the status boost are always visible near your HP bar

8) The open world map is amazing. And the graphics! I don't care if this game comes out in the low resolution of the demo. Because I STILL loved it

9) THE PHANTOM SWORDS

10) I actually LOVE the exclusion of the "skill tree" system. I hated it in X and I still do. I assume we will get some sort of system when magic and techniques are given to us in the full game. But please, I'm here to play a game and be immersed in the story. Not to spend hours and hours staring at some skill chart. The constant "what if I had done that instead" "Oh shoot this next boss is weak against fire. I leveled up ice". Please, magic levels up upon using it! In real life if I want to get better at an instrument I don't go to the gym and suddenly get better at that too. You have to practice the instrument. So using fire magic should equal fire magic leveling up. Not "I won a battle so now I can do whatever I want with these points"

11) Getting EXP for completing missions. This to me felt more real. In real life you gain "experience" from not only doing physical activities but by doing tasks. Want to be a military pilot? You need to train at the gym, but flying a plane around would probably be a better "experience" to get. So having charaters gain EXP from fighting and missions is a smart move!

CONS

1) THERE ARE NO SUBS WHILE IN A BATTLE!

2) While camping the characters current and new HP/MP are not shown.

3) Again, while camping, you can't CHOOSE what to cook. I stocked up 10 of everything to check and make sure a lack of ingredients wasn't the problem.

4) Targeting is AWFUL. You have to press and hold R1 until it finally locks, I can't figure out how to switch targets, and the camera doesn't track the enemy.

5) It is too difficult to escape if surrounded. I have been completely surrounded and not been able to use potions or run (he limps while at 0 HP) I like the limping, but either the potions need to be quicker or your friends need to help more.

6) After several hours of playing I was only helped by my party members a handful of times.

7) While using the dash/run command if you are close to an enemy Noct decides "Hey dash and attack are the same button. SO ATTACK" NO I WANTED TO RUN AWAY. Please make these different buttons.

8) Finding the Subtitle option was horrible. The options in the title screen are "Language and Text". Ok so I think Subs are on. Start game. No subs. After you get through the opening, and the tutorial, if you pause and go to the help menu. Options are again listed. This time with a Subs hide option. Turning subs on I had to restart the game to see the opening. This is ridiculous! And this menu has no language or text options. Please make the title menu's options include subs. And the in-game options include languages.

9) This is not a big one. You can't speak to everyone, you can only eavesdrop on people having conversations.

10) Again, not huge. The clothes for the NPCs are so repetitive. I think there are 3 outfits per gender. Alternate colors. That's it.

MY WISHLIST

1) Changing Noct and the others clothes. Maybe some different Tees for sale. A new pair of shoes. Just thought it would be cool to give them a little customizability!

2) SWIMMING! Ever since Noct took a dive in the ocean with Leviathan in a teaser, I've been dying to take a dip in the FF world

3) After staring at the gas station and everything in it. I noticed snacks on the counter. Cheese crackers and nuts. It would be fun to be able to buy snacks and eat them while exploring. No need to camp. A quick snack and a short lasting status boost. That would be awesome.

rating 5.0. Worth it! I know a lot of Final Fantasy fans might cling to the old systems, but one thing that kept me liking the franchise was its willingness to change.  I was worried after XIII that that was in the toilet.  Both Type-0 and the accompanying XV demo proved me so wrong and I'm completely glad for the departure from turn based into action RPG.  Yes, there is some camera hopping and some irritation with animation, the English voices are horrible, (Cinque's eye was doing a really weird deformation in a cut scene but I found it hilarious) but I'm also the person that enjoyed the very buggy early stages of Skyrim.  I'm not one that thinks a few kinks ruin the immersion or ability to involve my imagination.  Clearly you needed a lot of imagination to get through some of the earlier games.  Don't get me wrong, the earlier entries had their charm but role-playing has far more potential in free choice with open world action.  Especially if you want to really get the most out of their always stellar graphics in erecommendation..  I knew going into this that the graphics were a few years old and originally for a handheld, but I love the strategy to be found in utilizing the right characters for the battles and not letting them drop like flies in missions because once gone, they're out until the mission is over.  I absolutely loved this concept.  I didn't find level grinding to be tedious and I appreciated the promotion of abilities as incentive.  Admittedly, I skipped through some tedious cut scenes, but I wanted to get back to the action and looked forward to each town's little mini sub quest as a breather between missions.  Five stars doesn't mean it was everything I wanted in a game.  My highest marks in gaming go for games I actually enjoy enough to finish and replay.  Final Fantasy XV is looking like true perfection, but this game is on my favorites list with Crisis Core and Kingdom Hearts 1 and 2 for just pure fun.  Skyrim is still the only game I bothered to play into completion in a long time but this one will at least get some serious play and my earnest recommendation. It's not for everyone, but I can say it's definitely a hit for me.

rating 4.0. A concise return to series roots I will start with the few flaws and get them out of the way. Type-0 was originally a mobile game, so its graphical shortcomings are forgivable. Gameplay-wise, you're left with 14 teens returning to their military academy after almost every mission. This is honestly a bit shallow to me, but similar to one of my other favorite Final Fantasy games, VIII. Tutorials (that appear even halfway through the game) are presented to you incessantly by a Moogle. There are some missions when you may hear "KUPOOO!!!!" literally every ten seconds.

What you're left with after that is a logical evolution of the series. Deep character relationships and arcs, Cactuars, Moogles (Honestly too many), Chocobo (riding, breeding, racing, capturing, theme song), a WORLD MAP, summons including Ifrit and Shiva, crystal-worshipping establishments, and of course a guy named Cid. Several classic themes by Nobuo Uematsu make an appearance for the first time in several games such as the aforementioned Chocobo theme, Victory Fanfare, Prelude, and more.

The combat is more timing-based than time-based, if that makes sense. Occasionally an icon will appear notifying you of a short enemy opening to deliver a critical or even one-shot-kill hit. You won't need to wait around as much as 7 or 13's battle systems. The only drawback of this being an HD port of a mobile game is the lack of abilities. You can have one or two weapon or magic-based abilities totalling 3, and a defensive spell (which includes DOING A BARREL ROLL)

The story is much more gritty than most FF games, dealing with teenagers drafted into the bloodiest war in Final Fantasy history, and at the same time is more casual, originally being a mobile game. All characters are available immediately following the first mission (And you can play as any of them if you get annoyed with one. I'm looking at you, Tidus from FF10) and since there are so many, they're not focused on quite as much as, say, 7 or 10. Still, they have their own personalities and skills, and can be interacted with between missions. Also between missions, you will have an amount of "time" that never expires in real-time, but is more of a sidequest currency. Leaving the school takes 6 hours. Conversations that earn you extra potions and such cost 2 hours. You can always skip ahead to the next story mission, but it's in your best interest to walk around school and see who will reward you with an expensive potion because you listened to them talk about their crush or, somehow more awkwardly, gave them an expensive potion.

Type-0 has all the trademarks of the franchise while still being as accessible and addictive as a mobile game. I'd recommend it to longtime nostalgia-ridden fans of the series and anyone who enjoys a good JRPG.

rating 4.0. The Good: +The story starts off engaging The Good:

+The story starts off engaging. In this aspect, it takes after FFXII
+Really fun and complex battle system
+Manageable difficulty, missions are engaging and diverse.
+Geared toward a more mature audience. In fact, this is one of the first of the series where you see people dying on-screen

The Bad:

-The story is rather confusing unless you actually invest a lot of time trying to understand. Will most likely require a 2nd playthrough
-Story has a good concept. Just bad execution
-Lack of character development
-The world map is rather bland
-Sidequests are gimmicky and rather boring
-Camera angling and lock-on feature is frustrating until you get used to it.

This is a game that's a part of the Fabula Nova Crystallis series developed by Square Enix, and thankfully it's quite the opposite of Final Fantasy XIII, with it's repetitive automatic battle system and it's theme of a group of L'cie who chose to defy their fate. In fact, it's battle system is really engaging despite its shortcomings with camera angling and harvesting phantosma - both vital parts to receiving a good score on missions. Oh, and you better get used to dodging often. The stages in this game is divided into missions that are executed by Class Zero. In most stages, you can play as 14 different characters in a party of three people. Your mission grade and the rewards you receive is based on the amount of time you took to complete the mission, the number of phantosma harvested, and the number of people who died.

If you already didn't know, this is a remastered version of Final Fantasy Agito, a PSP game that came out back in 2011. Despite the remastered label slapped on it, the graphics aren't suited for the PS4 system. However, I believe it's still a beautiful game and that it's a good place to start in terms of difficulty if you're trying to get more into action oriented JRPGs.

SPOILERS

This game takes more after Final Fantasy XII, with its heavy political atmosphere and dark plot. For the first time in the Final Fantasy series, we see people slowly dying on screen. As opposed to the theme of defying fate in FFXIII, the L'Cie and the people of the world of Orience are slaves to the four crystals who order their respective L'Cie and empire to conquer the whole continent and basically bring about the apocalypse known as Temper Finis so that the prophesied savior Agito is said to be born. This spiral of conquering the world, defeating the big bad empire, and everybody dying has been repeated for over six billion times for the sake of one single outcome whose decision rests on the shoulders of Class Zero.

rating 4.0. Good Game With Shallow Characters Final Fantasy Type-0 Review

I went into this game expecting a PSP game with upgraded graphics and that's exactly what I got. What I do however find annoying if the lack of depth the characters bring. There was a time when Final Fantasy stood out as having some of the most interesting likeable characters throughout all of gaming. Other then that the game is decent. Gameplay is fun enough and the music is excellent.

Final Fantasy XV Demo Review

I find the all male cast REALLY takes away from the game. Strolling the country side with a bunch of bros doesn't feel very Final Fantasy like to me. Gameplay is good but I do miss the turn-based combat that the series was known for. It's to early to speak on story or music as there isn't enough of either to review. After spending about three hours with the demo I have to say I like it and I'm ok with most of the changes they made for this ONE entry into the series. I still stand by my opinion that this is a series that would benefit more from returning to it's roots than to continue changing into something different. Square Enix can make different games with different play styles but they shouldn't call everything Final Fantasy.

rating 4.0. Part I | Final Fantasy Type-0 HD (From Zero to Hero) | Part II | Final Fantasy XV: Episode Duscae (The Prince Finally Arrives) Part I | Final Fantasy Type-0 HD | The 2011 Fantasy we never got, back with a Vengence!

Intro | Final Fantasy Type-0 HD is basically Final Fantasy Agito for those of you who still remember that PSP title that never saw daylight in NA or EU.

With that said, it wouldn't be justified to judge this game by console standards at all, let alone PS4 Next Gen (AKA Current Gen) standards since it was built around Late Timeline PSP tech as its foundation. With that little tid bit outta' the way lemme point out one more thing, we never got this game in any official form at all until now for north america, so for those who didn't order the japanese psp version back in 2011 or so, this is a brand new experience for most of us.

Story | The story so far reminds me greatly of a fusion between Valkyria Chronicles II, Vampire Knight and oddly enough Resonance of Fate. As I am stilling playing the game I cannot fully judge this portion of the game quite yet, so far it is interesting enough for me to actually WANT to know what happens next, but that could be mainly because the entire FFXIII series left me starving for at least a decent JRPG storyline from square enix for many years now. Oh by the way, for what its worth, the story of Final Fantasy Type-0 HD is far superior way and above what the entire XIII trilogy offered, although if you really think about it, that isn't saying much.

Side Note: I will admit the Story is interesting so far, otherwise I couldn't bother to play if the story and characters weren't at least somewhat likable, but I must mention this on the side, the Introduction video to this game is long and very convoluted. That is not a wise choice in my opinion, I don't know if its because I'm getting to old to process all this lore and information as quickly as I use to when I was a kid in the 90s or teen in the early to late 2000s but I found it very jarring and almost annoying that we as the player are being fed ALL this backround story and Lore all at once at the very start!

It nearly turned me away from the game there was so much to learn and understand, fortunately I gave the game another chance, and unlike bloodborne this game came out on top and so far seems worth it.

Story-Characters | This is a section I rarely use now an days when it comes to my in-depth game reviews but I feel this one deserves it since there is untapped potential here. I wanted to mention that the characters in the story of FFType-0 HD are actually for the most part likable, at least in comparison to the horrible XIII games. I would say the characters here are FFX tier of likable. Some are even relatable.

In terms of character development and story, Square Enix finally remembered how to do a Final Fantasy game justice here, Ace is very likable guy and Machina and Rem are ok as well, the rest of Class Zero are also worth noting.

Presentation | The way this game presents itself to you as a new player can be off-putting at first due to the long intro and tons of lore being tossed at you, but give it some time and your faith in square enix will finally be refreshed, if only some what after playing this game.

To describe this game and its world correctly, I would place it somewhere between Steampunk/Fantasy with some Sci-Fi sprinkled in, so think FFVII, FFVI and FFVIII in terms of its world and its state of the world.

You will notice a few types of mechs and wild creatures from games like VII, VI and VIII easily. I know I sure did and it was GREAT, truly a throw back to the PS1 era fantasies.

As far as presentation goes I am not fully done and haven't come up with an ideal way to describe how I feel about it as a whole yet, I'll touch on this topic again later.

Gameplay | I'll keep this bit brief, just a warning, to those who it may concern.... If you are an oldschool JRPG gamer stuck in the past and refuse to submit to the future, stop reading right now. Yer outdated tastes are not welcome here, go back to the PS1 era and play yer relics where you belong.

Type-0 HD is part of the new trend of Action Styled RPG genre, a sub-genre of RPG that has slowly been gaining favor and focus over the past 5 years or so, maybe even slightly longer than that, I haven't kept track in awhile.

And lemme tell you, as a huge fan of Action RPG gameplay/combat this game isn't a disappointment, its not just a generic mindless hack n' slash as a lot of the people whining would have you to believe. It takes a great amount of skill to truly test yerself in this game, it can even be harder than Bloodborne if you choose to take on Agito mode instead of Cadet or Officer modes. The folks complaining are those who cannot accept what is new to them, either that or they're not skilled enough to embrace the delightful change. At any rate, the gameplay in Type-0 HD is very satisfying. If you are looking for a thrill in an action rpg game, you've found it. And if you think the action gameplay is good in this game, wait til you get to the Final Fantasy XV: Episode Duscae demo, it blows Final Fantasy Type-0 HD out of the water in every way imaginable.

Side Note: The gameplay in Type-0 HD is much closer to that of a south korean made game called Vindictus in terms of shear, raw combat potential. In terms of Character Roster, Selection and Balance however, it surpasses Vindictus in that regard due to having 14 selectable characters with a balanced 1 to 1 gender ratio as opposed to Vins stupid unbalanced 1 to 2 gender ratio male to female.

It is yet another shining example of Square Enix doing something right for once, keeping things balanced, fair and equal. Although thats in terms of selectable genders.... the actual gameplay of each character is very different leading some characters to being more OP than others, in some cases and situations MUCH more OP than the others in Class Zero... I'm looking at you Ace.

Sound/Music | The OST is great, its been a long time since I've heard a Final Fantasy game with such a strong soundtrack, lots of choir and vocals along with violins and many other great songs, oddly enough though the guy who did this game and its soundtrack isn't the legendary Mr. Nobuo Uematsu or the equally as legendary Miss. Yoko Shimomura. Its a gentleman named Takeharu Ishimoto as the lead composer. He doesn't seem to be as skilled as the big legends however I see great potential in his future, the music he composed for Final Fnatasy Type-0 HD is truly a step in the right direction for his career. Basically, the lad knows what he is doing and he is doing it well. As a Freelance Music Producer myself, I can clearly say this man will become a legend maybe some day. Right now though I would consider him student class under his senpais Mr. Nobuo Uematsu and Miss. Yoko Shimomura.

The Voice Acting, Yes I hear the complaints, but to be fair its an overreaction. They actually have quite a few talented voice actors on board on this project of a game. Steve Blum is even present! THE LEGENDARY STEVE BLUM! AKA Spike from Cowboy bebop! As for class zero themselves, right away I noticed the black mighty morphin power ranger himself Johnny Yong Bosh or "JYB" for short, in the roster of talented voice actors, as well as the lovely lady Heather Hogan who I rarely hear in video games lately. To be absolutely fair here, yes there are a lot of newcomers in this game in the voice acting department, some from the anime world others not, I did not recognize most of them.

A lot are indeed new most likely, but even most of the newbies are doing an ok job in my book. There are literally only a select few characters whos english dub voices are completely emotionless and horrible and feel as if they're reading right off the script for the first time ever, all of them female characters who are terrible. Fortunately you mainly hear Ace in Class Zero talk the most, I don't think i've heard the guy who voices Ace before, but he plays the role well in my book.

Anyway I could go on and on about this bit as well, all I will say is people are being very harsh against the english cast in this game, I as an english speaking fan of JRPGs gladly accept it, now more than ever simply because MANY game publishers and developers have been skipping out on fully voice english dub casts in NA releases of japanese games. Koei and Sony being the worst offenders at the moment with the recent Dynasty Warriors Empires games, Samurai Warriors and on the Sony side Freedom Wars, all of which do not even HAVE an english voice option. So I am very grateful that Square Enix didn't leave their english speaking fans out like Koei and Sony has, SE did something right.

Conclusion | As of right now I will simply say I am satisfied, both as a true Final Fantasy fan and as a gamer. Final Fantasy Type-0 HD has restored my faith in Square Enix that they are indeed still capable of developing great games for this generation and beyond. Cheers!

Part II | Final Fantasy XV: Duscae | The Ultimate Fantasy we've been waiting for since the year 2006 has finally arrived! S-Sorta.....

Note: I already played through the highly anticipated Final Fantasy XV: Duscae for the first time a little while ago when I got my Type-0 HD order from Amazon.com. Needless to say it is truly out of this world, truly otherworldly yet somehow familiar. I am going to play through it one more time to do all the side quests and see if i can discover any other minor details that I missed in my first playthrough and add the details here later.

Note II: The only zone I filled out was the conclusion, because my opinion on Final Fantasy XV has an unshakable Foundation that has convinced me that Final Fantasy XV will finally once again return the Final Fantasy Name to its true glory at long last!

Intro | Coming Soon.....

Story | Coming Soon.....

Presentation | Coming Soon.....

Gameplay | Coming Soon.....

World | Coming Soon.....

Sound and Music | Coming Soon.....

Conclusion | 5 Stars, 10 out of 10, Grade S, Ultimate Status, Performance Unmatched. Call it what you want, THIS is the Final Fantasy game to rule them all. I would even go as far as to say, The Legendary most loved FF game of all time, Final Fantasy VII has finally met its match. Be prepared!

Other Thoughts | Coming Soon.....

rating 4.0. Fun, slightly repetitive, story is only ok, gameplay makes up for it imo Slightly repetitive, but the gameplay is actually pretty fun. You have a lot of characters, so you can find the ones that really fit your style. I normally wouldn't choose a character like 8, but he was so fun! Loved the ability to time the insta-kills and such. Some of the optional challenges were frustrating, and it's kind of annoying you have to play through multiple times to get all the levels, unless you're grinding for hours (which I did at one point). There is a cave outside the town to the left of the "home base" and I just farmed for xp and extracted phantoma to sell at the dealer in I forget which town.

Story was only ok, but I'm not into military storylines really. It did have a nice arc where the students went through experiencing war, but it was done in only an OK manner, at least to me.

rating 5.0. Well done Square! Keep it up! Ive been a final fantasy fan since VII. But to start this off I am reviewing this game at a PSP port standpoint.

I am a game designer so Ill try to not be biased but in terms of quality I can say this game has it. At first the game took me at a surprise with how the visuals looked they didn't look too bad for a PSP port but could be a ton better. Then I began to notice the great things about this game. This game has a combat system that is just superb. Its fun and tactical. Then the story just keeps getting better and better as the game progresses. Other than the story, you can also sink hours into side quests if you do them right. Lastly I will talk about the soundtrack. The soundtrack is amazing! I love it ALOT!

So enough said about this game. I can just say I would highly recommend it for any hardcore final fantasy fan. ( Buying the strategy guide for playthrough #2 is a good idea ). At first this game may seem like a let down for a next gen system but its truly an amazing piece of work. Good job square!

Graphics: 5/10 ( Not very good, terrible for next gen )
Plot/Story: 10/10 ( Amazing )
Combat: 9999999/10 ( Freaking awesome for a final fantasy game )
Controls: 7/10 ( Camera angles can make it difficult sometimes )
Sound: 9 ( Solid, Great soundtrack )

OVERALL: 9.5/10 ( Despite the bad visuals and controls its still GREAT! )

rating 4.0. A strange but ultimately rewarding entry to the Final Fantasy series. Decent looking port of the PSP title. Lovably bizarre subplots mixed in with a violent and dramatic story about the horrors of war. It has an actual world map, too.

If you're getting this for the FFXV Duscae demo, make sure to buy new. The demo is provided by an online voucher and is not on the disc. Also bear in mind that the voucher has apparently expired earlier this year (as I'm typing), but there have been reports of the product codes still working regardless.

rating 3.0. Game play wise the battle system is great and addictive Final Fantasy Type 0 can be enjoyable if you don't mind its story and graphics. Story wise, it has a strong start but as chapters unfold it starts to become stale and uninspiring. Even though they updated a few character models, plenty parts of the game still carries outdated visuals. Game play wise the battle system is great and addictive. One key issue with the battle system is the camera controls so it requires a little patience to get used to. There are plenty of quests and the over world is huge and packed with many dungeons to explore and loot to obtain. If you're a Final Fantasy fan looking for an action RPG then this is it!

Final Fantasy XV demo was a great addition to this game. It's packed with great visuals, voice acting and unique characters. I can't wait till FFXV, it's shaping up to be the best JRPG released by Square Enix stateside.

current_product_reviews:
rating 3.0. Decent Action RPG, Pretty Terrible Final Fantasy I have been a long-time Final Fantasy fan, so my review is probably biased in that regard. I tend to hold these titles to a higher standard, so when they are not particularly great, like this one, I will be more disappointed than most.

After playing through this title, I found it to be fairly superficial at its heart. The combat was not incredible engaging, the characters progression fell relatively flat, and the bosses were fairly uninspiring. The dialogue is extremely cringe worthy, and I found myself disliking half of the characters as they seem more like a caricature than a person. The story was okay, but it was so severely hampered by the dialogue, that it broke any kind of immersion I might have felt.

In general, this is a fairly average action RPG. I think it is worth playing through if you have extra time/money on your hands, but like the other FF games in its universe (I am looking at you FFXIII), it is not a great entry to the series.

rating 3.0. A little busy but an entertaining workout I got Just Dance 4 not long ago, and am loving it so much I wanted more. After reading the reviews for DC I expected it to be even better. Some of the defaults are really irritating, so I am modifying my review now that I have tweaked it a bit.

Initial issues that are fixed with settings:
1. It only played snippets instead of full songs.
2. It has this annoying Freestyle mode where it doesn't show you the moves anymore, but only shows you dancing by yourself onscreen as a lit up silhouette. If I only wanted to watch myself dance around to my own moves in front of a mirror, I wouldn't have needed an Xbox. It is seriously irritating.
3. It isn't really good cardio until you get into Hard difficulty level. That's kind of a pain because I would like to be able to ease into the choreography while still breaking a sweat. But the Hard level does provide a good workout even if you're just flailing around trying to follow the leader.

I do like that you can create playlists for fitness mode. And the choreography is fun.

This one definitely has a less family friendly playlist than Just Dance, which isn't a bad thing necessarily but FYI. The moves are a bit more sophisticated than JD.

What I still prefer about JD 4 is that the screen isn't as busy. DC has a little too much going on in my opinion, but that may be a plus for some people.

Overall much better than doing boring aerobics or the treadmill...

rating 5.0. Worth it, even if you have the previous two. The music they chose for the intro is my only complaint in this latest iteration of Dance Central. I like lots of dubstep, but the music they begin the game with is just terrible. However, if you can get past the opening menu screen, you'll find that everything has improved.

The best feature and the reason you should buy this over the previous two (or in addition to, in my case) is their new party mode system. I have had many parties centered around Dance Central 3 and its predecessors, and three does the best job of making people feel comfortable jumping in and playing.

The new pose mode is great for my gamer friends that aren't as thrilled about dancing. The new make-your-own dance mode is the source of endless hilarity (although they could do a little more to help people who fail it because they're too embarrassed to make something up). And the keep the beat game is a good breather of sorts, though none of us really get how you're supposed to play (do you copy the beat or your opponent's move to steal it? and what's to keep you from doing the same one dance for the entire song?).

Finally, as always, the thing that separates DC3 from all other games in the genre (and most games, really) is the attention to detail. One of the smartest things they did was to make entry into party mode trigger on a high-five between the participants. This is not only extremely functional, but it's a small gesture that contributes enormously to how people enter the game psychologically. Now, before starting, you have to look at your partner and do a gesture largely reserved for when something awesome is about to happen. I think this, in no small part, generates trust between the dancers and makes them both feel that whatever follows is going to be good. It's very different from jumping into DC2 or DC1 at a party.

rating 5.0. The only reason to own an Xbox Kinect - Fun party game and great for people who wanna learn to dance!! This game justifies the existence of the XBox Kinect sensor.

First of all, I really enjoyed Just Dance 3 (JD3) on Wii, but did not realize how much fun a serious dance game could be until I purchased Dance Central 3 (DC3). And when I say serious I'm not referring to the plot; what I'm referring to are all the options available in this game. The most useful option is being able to practice small sections of a dance as many times as you like until you have the movement down. This option is very useful especially when you are dancing to higher difficulty versions of a song, and unless you are an actual dancer/choreographer yourself you might find yourself needing to try, try, try again. Also, these songs are intricately choreographed, yet they don't feel like they belong solely on a music video. They are moves you could actually do on the dance floor, especially the line dances like electric slide or cupid shuffle. Speaking of which, the music selection in DC3 is more varied than in JD3, and includes more hip hop options. Finally, while multiplayer in DC3 is limited to 2 players, there are more gameplay options available, such as a battle mode where you try to outdo your opponent in terms of dance moves.

If your looking for an alternative to JD3 and want to seriously learn some actual dance moves, I highly recommend this game.
