rating 5.0. Awesome game Ever since Dance Central (1), I've been a totally enthusiastic fan of this series. Harmonix did an awesome job of coming up with great choreographies for all the songs on their list. I've bought all 3 of their Dance Central games and have never been disappointed. I like how in Dance Central 3 (henceforth DC3) there are more options (such as Party, in which players can just drop in and out of the game seamlessly) and even a Story mode. There are also many more songs added to the list ranging from the 70's to the current selection of songs.

Story - despite the cheesy plotline, I found myself enjoying this aspect of DC3. You are an agent working for the DCI (Dance Central Intelligence) and your mission is to stop the big bad guy (Dr Tan) from taking over the world by going on dance-offs throughout the eras. You start in the 70's and end in the present and have to dance a certain number of songs in order to defeat the evil Dr Tan. I like the Story mode just because it allows me to sample all the tracks available in the game. Otherwise, on my own I probably wouldn't play some of the song (I don't like some of the songs featured). The storyline is not really the point but it provides a great way to get into the groove of dancing.

Improved Interface - I like how the characters have new designs and costumes. I also like how the interface of the game looks more modern and better. The flash-cards also have better cues for the player and are very easy to understand even without rehearsing a song first. DC3 also has a better audio interface where players can just speak commands and the game will follow them.

Fitness - The estimated calories burned in the Fitness mode (you can either activate it or not) is a great motivation. It's nice to see how much estimated calories I've burned in a certain session. The best part is how much fun I'm having while I'm doing it. I don't feel like I'm working out at all! Some of the choreography can be really challenging, especially in the more difficult levels.

Difficulty - A new addition to the difficulty was added called "Beginner". I think it's awesome because it lowers the learning curve for really new players and allows them to join in the fun without feeling like they can't keep up. As someone who has been playing Dance Central since they came out, Beginner was very easy but I'm happy that they added it anyway as a means to allow new players to jump in!

Options - There are so many modes to choose from - from Party to Story to Rehearsal to Perform It, I can choose any mode I want. I like the wide range of modes. I also like that there is no longer the wacky interval in the middle of the song (like in Dance Central) where you can do things Freestyle.

Song List - Awesome selection of songs from the 70's to the 00's. Some of my favourites were features too like the smash hit from the 90's "Everybody" by Backstreet Boys. Harmonix added most of the hit songs from each era which was great!

Optional DLC - If the 46 songs that are already included isn't enough, there is also a store that you can access to purchase MORE songs! They are about 240 points a piece though which is a little too much in my opinion, but then again, I'm quite cheap.

Download Songs from DC1 and DC2 - Owners of DC1 and DC2 will be happy to know that for a small fee (I think it was 400 points for each transfer) they can import all the songs from DC1 and DC2 into DC3. So thanks to that forward compatibility, I now have 126 songs (plus 4 that I downloaded from the store). That's just amazing!

Overall - great game especially if you want lots of people to join in for party mode. Great motivation for losing weight (or at least being active). Great value for money for all the songs you get.

rating 5.0. Same Forumla, New Moves As an active player of the previous 2 Dance Central games, I knew I had to get this game. I bought it from Amazon for the preorder bonus songs (Alejandro and Paparazzi by Lady Gaga), but I haven't received the code for that yet, so I will be leaving them out of the review for now.

First of all, don't expect a whole new game. This game plays exactly like the previous Dance Central games, which I think is great. After all, if it's broke, don't fix it, right? As in all Dance Central games you follow your dancer, there are cue cards to the side, and if you're off at all it will show you in red on the dancer. So far I haven't had any problems with it reading me and accurately following my movements.

Though while it is the same general formula, it is also completely different. I was actually quite surprised. When I picked up Dance Central 2 I started playing in Medium Mode right away and was relatively good at the songs right off the bat. This time about 99% of the moves in the songs I have played seem to be brand new. I started playing in Medium again, but when I got further down the list as far as difficulty on the songs is concerned, I found myself tripping up and laughing as I missed moves. They are great moves and they are all super fun, so it's not a plus rather than a negative. It gives me goals to work for again, instead of just automatically being good at the songs.

There is a really good blend of songs across all different categories. I actually love the goofy yet fun additions of the Electric Slide and the Cupid Shuffle (one of my favorite ones to play so far because it is a 'goofy' song for me in comparison to the others). Also, I noticed that one of the songs is by a Korean band - 2NE1. I haven't played that song yet, but I do hope that said addition means we'll be seeing more 'foreign' songs in the future. Perhaps Gangnam Style as downloadable content? Pretty please?

I did check out the party mode and while it's a good idea, I don't see why I'd choose it over just having everyone free play. It also has problem reading my hand when I 'raise it' to join, which is frustrating.

Voice commands are much better than in previous games. They took the guess work out by using the 'black light mode' to highlight the voice commands. I'm more likely to use the voice commands when I'm playing by myself, though I still use the controller when playing with other people around so I'm not yelling at the TV.

I haven't tried the story mode yet, just the free play, so I cannot review the other modes at this time.

Overall the choreography is refreshing new with the game play you already know and love if you've played previous titles. I recommend it.

Update: Okay, so I have played both preorder tracks and they are fun. More importantly, however, I have beaten the storymode. So I am back to review that.

First of all, don't expect a serious story mode from a dance game. Ever. This story mode is toooootally cheesy. The gist of it is that you are trying to defeat Dr. Tan and his dance robots... by traveling through time via your boom box, 'Boomy' (yes, it has a name now), and mastering the 'crazes' of the era. You earn the 'craze' song by unlocking craze moves that are hidden in other songs. Secondly, you have to get enough star power for 'Boomy' to work and time travel you to the next era. Completely cheestastic story and silly 'keep the beat' mini game aside, the storymode is really fun. I had a great time with the 70s, the 00s (oh God, that's an 'era' now. I feel old, and I'm a 90s kid. Whoops.), and the 'Present' eras. Also, it's nice have an excuse to dance the Macarena again. Haha... yeah. Warning - it makes you dance a Beiber song. I'll admit, that was the hardest song for me, as I didn't know it at ALL and I still don't remember it. But I don't have to play it again, so it's cool. Overall, fun to play - cheesy story. Have fun and don't take it seriously!

rating 5.0. DC3 I have DC1 I don't have DC2 but this is a major jump from the first. The dance moves are better though I wouldn't say that they are that hard to learn nor the songs. I didn't really like all of the songs from DC1 but this one has some good ones, not to mention I love the disco stuff.

The characters have changed there are some new ones but some old ones. The graphics seem more crisper to me as well as the back grounds.

The set up is completely different from DC1 (I'm talking about the menu) I think this one compared to the first is totally different. But its a good different. I found this one to be easier were areas I found the other just plain. I did find out that all the DC's can be added to ANY current DC out there which is pretty cool. So basically you can add DC1 and DC2 to DC3 and have them all together on one game instead of using all three separate, I thought this was pretty darn neat.

The dancing I found was way better choreographed than the first, again I can't speak for the second but it was really fun and made you WANT to dance. I did want to point out that unlike with DC1, DC3 picks up my dance moves better. I'm not sure if my Kinect just doesn't like the first one or if I'm doing something wrong or if its the game.

Players- I like how this one you can have two players during every song. Not only that but if that player steps out then the "screen moves" automatically leave. The second or the first player can leave at any point during the song and step back in when there ready to by just holding their hand up. This is a really helpful feature if you have small children who want to come and go or if you want to jump out to catch a much needed breath.

A tip that I would like to leave you with is to wear more "fitted clothing" when playing, this will pick up your moves more accurate as opposed to baggy clothes. If I wear my big shirts and sweats it only picks up some hand motions and not the full body motions at all. And when I wear baggy pants/sweats then it misses most of my foot work even with the Kinect getting full view of my body. So PLEASE take this into consideration when playing, its not like when your dancing in the studio and can see your moves in a mirror.

rating 2.0. Skip this. Go buy an official Nintendo controller instead. First impression is that it looks really nice, very similar to the original Gamecube controller. Buttons feel nice, both L, R and Z's. The bad thing is that it is so light that it feels cheap and hollow. The left stick's spring is pretty weak too, and so it doesn't return to its central position quickly after a quick input. The controller works fine overall, you can get used to it. My main problem, and the main reason why I'm giving it such a low rating, is that since the first time I used it, brand new, out of the box, I've had an upward drift on my left stick, which is slight and doesn't interfere when playing a Smash Bros match, but outside of a match, it's pretty annoying when trying to navigate the menus and character select. There is no way to even try to calibrate it on the Switch settings as it just won't let me. And judging by a lot of other negative reviews I've seen, this is a pretty common issue with this controller in particular. In all honesty, I wouldn't recommend this controller to anyone. I find it unacceptable for a brand new controller to have this issue, and even more so that this issue is so common and PowerA is not doing a proper quality control on their products and lets this happen to so many people. Like I said, I can still use it to play Smash Bros when inside a match, and that's the only reason why I'm giving 2 stars instead of 1, just because it's ''usable''. But outside of that, it's pretty annoying and inconvenient. I can see this being a bigger problem if I ever want to try it on another game like Breath Of The Wild or Mario Odyssey for example, where the movement inputs need to be a lot more precise and consistent. Just skip this one and go look for an official Gamecube controller for Switch made by Nintendo. This is not worth it even at it's price.

rating 4.0. Paid $6.99 for this and it's great for that value. But not for $24.88 My overall rating is 5 stars because I am reviewing this as a general consumer.

Personally, I have two Gamecube adapters and 8 original Gamecube controllers that I use for Super Smash Bros, but for $6.99 at the time of purchase (December 17 2019, they were on sale), I couldn't pass them up for that price. I got them, tried them out, and they're okay. I don't like how the L and R triggers don't go down all the way like the original Gamecube controllers, and so they also don't have the two clicks where you can distinguish a half input and a full input. If they added this feature in it would have been significantly better.

The shell also is a bit more rugged, while the original Gamecube controller feels smoother and just better. In addition, the weight of the PowerA controller is really light. It doesn't feel right. The original Gamecube controller feels heavier and more solid to hold.

However, at $6.99, the value is there. It's a working controller that you can use for other games besides Super Smash Bros but with the Gamecube controller look. If you don't have Gamecube controllers and an adapter for them, these work. They take up one of your 3 available USB slots on your dock though.

If you're expecting Gamecube controller quality, it is not here. But I recently purchased replacement thumbstick caps for old Gamecube controllers, and the cost was slightly less than this PowerA controller.

All in all, not a great "Gamecube Style" controller, but still a good controller for its simple functions of being a controller.

rating 3.0. It’s made for children. Growing up, playing smash was always one of my favorite things to do after school. And what better way to play than on a GameCube controller?
Pros:
-Rubberized analog and C-stick
-Short travel distance for ZL and ZR
-Detachable Cable
-Great price
Cons:
-The biggest one I found, and it’s a pretty big one that seemed to be unmentioned, the controller’s overall size. It’s about 80% the size of an original or even a competitively priced PdP controller.
-Unusual spacing between the rather Large D-pad and C-stick
-What could be called “mushy” buttons but nothing that would make or break the purchase
-If you’re a sweaty palms kind of gamer, the finish is slick and can slide out of your hands

Overall review: I’m unhappy I spent my money on this controller but if you have kids or a friend with smaller hands this could potentially be a great buy. Just don’t settle for the first recommended controller.

rating 1.0. was this meant to be a satire of video games? I rue the day this title came out for Playstation. It was considered so original, it influenced many, many stealth-type games that followed it. Too many.

Where do I start with the things I hate about this game?

1. It's way too difficult. You get awfully sick of hearing "Snaaaaaaaaaaaaaake!" every 10 seconds. There should have been a way to ratchet down the difficulty even further for 'tards like me. "Easy" mode really isn't. Sheesh. I wonder what "Hard" mode was like.

2. Way too many cutscenes! I've never seen anything like it. There's one cut scene that goes on for like 30 minutes. This has got to be the talkiest video game I've ever played. And these dialogues are all so silly, and you're pretty much forced to watch them or you won't be able to understand what's going on. In fact, I would say that most of your time in this game will be spent watching cutscenes. It's not so much a video game as a movie in which you are occasionally allowed to press buttons.

3. I hate video games that won't let you pause at certain moments, as this won't. I wouldn't advise playing this if you have a wife or mother who's gonna occasionally ask you to help her in the kitchen: you'll find yourself saying, "Yeah, I'll help you after this scene finishes . . . in about half an hour! Not allowed to pause it for some reason!"

4. Even more excusably, there's not way to pause the cutscenes. You can't even pause the codec dialogues!

5. There are many scenes in which the game disables your weapons and accessories for no logical reason other than that if it didn't, the scene would be too easy.

6. There are far too many boss monsters. It's like every other encounter is a boss monster. The first part of the game (say, up until the first encounter with Revolver Ocelot) is pretty great, but that's not representative of the game: after that it's basically an unending series of boss monsters.

7. Frustratingly, you have to fight several boss monsters twice because they never seem to die. Having to "kill" the same boss monster over and over again makes you feel like you're going in circles.

8. The principals having these deep discussions about delicate emotional issues while an enemy is shooting at them or while a timer is ticking down. Was this written by a 14-year-old?

9. The system for flattening oneself against a wall is not ready for prime time: most of the time you find yourself flattened against the wall and you didn't mean to do this. This will get you killed several times as you are thus basically standing still, allowing your enemies to shoot you. It should have been running up to a wall in combination with pressing L1 or something.

10. The way your guy stands there uselessly for about two seconds after choking somebody.

11. The way you can easily bypass critical lifesaving devices, such as the thermal goggles.

12. The way the game takes all these cheap shots at you: you can't look ahead because your radar is "jammed" and then you get shot by a security camera. Or blown up by a mine.

13. The way you are prevented, after having killed a boss, from going back into the battle area and collecting the stuff you didn't collect during the battle. Is it there or not? So why can't I get it now?

14. The absurd anti-nuclear message at the game's finale.

rating 1.0. Major letdown I think the game was a huge dissappointment, I've been a fan of final fantasy since I was about four years old and now am twenty-four. For twenty years I have enjoyed many of final fantasy titles such as one through ten. 10-2 was the first game I disliked of the series because of the storyline. The game play itself was not bad but I felt the story was bad to the point of not even playing. One of my favorites was final fantasy 7 like for many other fans. I wish there was another title worthy of seven but I doubt we will see that rare Jewel in our lifetime again. This title has a decent story line but the gameplay is absolutely ridiculous on many different levels. If you like a mixture of final fantasy 11, 13, and a realm unborn you may have satisfaction with this game. I was really excited to see how this new game would be especially for being one the first titles to be rated mature and I still don't fully understand why it's not rated teen. There is no more blood and gore than any of the other titles and there is little swearing between characterd, no nudity and the ability to roam freely is very limited. It seemed cool how many characters you could choose from until you play each character. Most the characters have very limited abilities and also is difficult to heal when you have to charge when you being bombarded. Then you have characters that do things like play flute and supposedly the notes attack the enemy's but by the time they do you Will be dead. It's upsetting in many different ways and I see no reason to finish playing. I have shown a few friends of mine that like final fantasy as well and they all agreed this game is a waste of money and kind of a slap in the face for those who know and love the originals. I don't speak for everyone but I suggest doing some research before buying the game. The only good thing I saw out of that game is hope for final fantasy 15 after playing the demo.

rating 5.0. A Nice Trip Down Memory Lane I was so excited when I found out that Yoshi's New Island was coming out for the 3DS. Having had owned and loved the original Yoshi's Island, I felt like I had been waiting an eternity for the newest addition to the series. When I got the game and put it in, I was pleasantly surprised that for the most part, not much has changed! I say this in the lines of game-play and graphically, this game is a perfect continuation of the original SNES title. The cartoonish look of the Yoshi games has always been so visually pleasing, and the easy learn-as-you-go control scheme is fluid and has great reaction time.

It's a simple review - if you loved the original, or you are looking for a fun, mario related escape for the 3DS. Pick this up! The only downfall is it is kind of a short game, but the replay value is high because of the graphics and fun imagery you might miss the first go round!

rating 3.0. Strange game with seemingly no balance I got this game because I was expecting a bit of a homage to the original Yoshi's Island game. However, there was a gigantic fatal mistake that happened with this game: When you get a midpoint ring, it DOESN'T save your star count!

It feels like they tried to make up for this by making red eggs become more easy to get, but two stars a red egg means if you are rather behind, and already used up the cloud born stars, at the most you would need to launch TEN RED EGGS to make up for it.

Another fault is hiding flowers or stars mostly in INVISIBLE CLOUDS.

The final fault I found is that you NEED to hit a flower on the goal ring to get a collectable that is REQUIRED to get 100% in that level, the medals. This pretty much means that some of the collectables to get 100% in the level isn't skill based at all, its entirely luck based. You could be the best Yoshi's Island player in the entire world, but if you don't land on a flower at the end, you might as well start the level over, since it didn't count.

This is why the game seems to have no balance.
The first game made you feel like the normal game ranges from easy to challenging, while trying to get the secrets makes any level a challenge itself. The midpoint is a sign of relief. It says to you "hey not to worry, if you mess up, at least you start where with your saved stars instead of from the beginning!"

But in the new game, it feels as though just trying to beat the game is too easy, while trying to get the collectables is actually TOO HARD. Non star saving midpoint, luck based medals, and trying to patch these with red eggs. It gives off the impression that the game creators didn't care about people trying to get 100%. It feels like someone FORCED them to put collectables in the game when they didn't want to, so to spite them, they did things such as medals that are entirely luck based.

When I got the game, I played through the first world trying for 100%. It took me TEN TRIES to get the medals from the FIRST LEVEL'S GOAL RING. Yes, you really did just read that, ten tries. By the time I tried to get the medals from the second level's goal ring, I was tired of it already. When I failed to get the medals for the third time in a row, that's when the game had finally broke me and I said "I give up, I will just try beating the game normally then."
Then world one was just too easy for me. Not easy as in "well this is the first world", but easy as in "even a beginner wouldn't find this to be a challenge" Even some of the new power ups feel basically like hand holding. Like making a gigantic metal egg, and just following it as it clears the level for you basically.

So all in all, this is the second time a Yoshi's Island sequel/prequel has disappointed me, with Yoshi's Island DS's baby swapping system getting in the way most of the time.
3/5.

rating 2.0. Too Kiddy I had hoped that underneath the crayon art style there would be a playable Mario game. I didn't play any of the other Yoshi's Island games (were there even more than one?) and as a result I was looking at this as a spiritual successor to Super Mario World. And it's not. It's dumbed down and feels like some independent flash game on the web. It doesn't have any of the polish I was expecting from a Nintendo game using their core properties, and that's a major "no thank you" for me.

In short, if you want your small child to play an irritating platformer, this is probably fine for them. If you're not a child, and you're just bored with the 3DS offerings at the moment, look elsewhere.

rating 3.0. Very mediocre Yoshi title. Better than Yoshi's Story, but not by much. I've been a longtime fan of Yoshi, and was pretty disappointed in this game. The game is initially fun, and hearkens back to the glory days of the Super Nintendo classic, but the "new" stuff is all pretty lame. Giant eggs appear only periodically, there is an annoying animation pause if you decide to throw an egg while standing, and the game is very simple if you don't go for a perfect playthrough (collecting flowers, coins, stars). And if you do decide to go for a perfect playthrough, the game becomes frustratingly difficult since all your "stars" reset if you restart at a checkpoint, no special items are there to help you out with collecting red coins, and many of the levels are designed in such a way where you can't go back in case you missed something in a previous area.

It's alright, but because of the reasons mentioned above, was nothing more than that, sadly.

rating 5.0. Everything I was hoping it would be. I rated this according to everything that I was looking for in a headset.

Sound:

These will definitely cancel out outside noises though. Not a bad thing in my book but if you have someone is trying to talk to you outside of the game, they'll be quite frustrated with you.

rating 2.0. No directional sound at all. And unconfortable. But surround is good. I am a gamer, bought this for games, and I was using a Senheiser headset, which has great sound, but wasn't surround neither had mic.

First and most important. The directional sound feature just does not work. At all. The front and rear channels sound pretty much the same as the left/right channels. There is NO DIRECTIONAL audio at all. In Battlefield 3, you just can't identify the direction of events just by the sound of it. And that is so disappointing. Even in the Logitech sound demo, a voice says "this is the right rear channel" and makes a sound. If the voice didn't announce it, you would never guess which channel is that.

It's not a configuration problem. I double checked it. Logitech software lets you know that surround sound is active. I did some tests. Threw a grenade in BF3. Turned on my back. The sound was absolutely the same as when I threw a grenade and didn't look back. But you can tell when it's right/left. Just like ordinary stereo headsets.

Another big disappointment is, as some other reviewers posted, this is unconfortable. The ear and top cushions are hard, the head band is small and tight. Such a difference from the relaxed and confy Senheiser!

Now the pros (there is some, at least) there is a mic, a volume button that controls the Windows master volume, sound quality is, in my opinion, very good, bass as well, and the surround feature works, so you feel more immersed in the game, events like rain feel more realistic, even though there is NO directional audio feature at all.

Overall, despite I still don't have directional audio, and I have a less confortable headset, now I have integrated mic, volume control, and surround audio, so I think I will stick with these, until I get a chance to buy and try some other 7.1 headset.

Directional audio was the reason I bought this. But I'll give 2 stars instead of the minimum 1, because at least the surround sound is cool, and it also has integrated mic and volume control.

rating 4.0. Overwhelming Sound I first bought the G35 headset because of reviews that had said it gave the speaker a clear voice on recordings, and since my main reason for purchasing this was to record Youtube videos, I gave the G35 headset a try. And even after a short time, headset already goes above and beyond what I would expect of it.

First off though, I would like to point out a few minor details that, if altered, would have made me give this product 5 stars instead of 4. In the picture provided by Amazon, you cannot see a tiny wire connecting the speakers through the over-the-head piece. There's a small loop of 1/8 inch wire that seems to be in the best place for damaging - right between the headphone and the mount for the headphone. Granted, even in it's fully compressed state, the wire doesn't get crimped or bent in damaging ways, but it's a design flaw that could manifest itself after a year or two. I guess that'd be why the G35 comes with an extra 4 year warranty for about 20 dollars.
Secondly, the software that auto-installs on your computer (if possible) can be a bit cumbersome at times. Since the instructions (I'm not going to even bother mentioning the picture-book instructions that came with it) don't mention how to access the software after installing it, you must figure out where this magically interface is located.

However, if you disregard those flaws, and handle this headset with care - just like with all electronics need to be treated - then this headset will probably last you several years. The main USB cable is strong, coated with a rubber-kevlar shell, and seemingly two time thicker than the actual wire inside the covering. And this headset has got some of the best bass I've have ever heard. It comes default with the settings of Bass and Treble set to 0 & 0, but if you up the bass by about 10, and the treble by 5, you're going to experience brilliant surround sound. The high notes can be a tad shorter, due to the fact that the 7.1 surround sound is made through a chip and not actual speaker cones, but it's as close to a theater experience as most people will get inside their own homes.

When handled with care, the Logitech G35 would make a great addition to a Youtuber, or someone who makes online calls frequently, or even if you just want to listen to some rock music while slaying zombies. This headset has incredible potential, and I hope to see improvements over time. If not, then it's still a great headset for a great price, and I encourage you to see it's potential for yourself.

rating 5.0. Great while it lasted (Edited: They replaced the broken headset) Great while it lasted. Almost 2 years 4 months.

As a lot of people have mentioned you have to be gentle with this headset because the swivel joint between the ear and the headband is a very tiny piece of metal. But beyond that I loved this headset.

Pro:

Sound quality was good. (I'm not sound person but it was the best I've had for a headset ever)
Microphone. As I was told this was crisp and clear. I could have a large fan near by and the people on the other end would know no difference.
Programmable keys. Just like any other G key for Logitech they are easy to use and fairly straight forward.
Light indicated for muted mic. I tend to mute my mic when ever I do not need to speak and the indicated light is something I've come to rely on. I've never had one before, but not with it I don't know how I'll ever be without it.
Mic swing arm. The swing are feels nice and sturdy and even after 2 years of constant use it still functions like the day I had it. On an added bonus when the microphone is stored away at the top it is muted as well.

Cons:

Pivot point As stated previously the pivot point is the weak spot of this headphone. Please don't get me wrong I've dropped the headset a fair number of times and it has never broke, but I'm always expecting it to break when I drop it.
Volume control. The volume dial is a digital read meaning it spins freely. Over time these can get dirty and lose accuracy. After roughly a year and a half mine started skipping while adjusting it. Nothing to bad and nothing that would cause me alarm. Its just something to note.
Ear piece covers: What ever material that covered the ear piece and headband started to deteriorate about the two year mark leaving small black flakes everywhere. By the time of this writing of the review I'd guess roughly 70% of the material has been eroded away.

Broken:

2 years 4 months 2 weeks is how long this headset lasted me. No physical external pieces of the headset broke. Windows stopped recognizing the headset as a G35 headset. It now only recognizes it as a USB headset. I can change the audio source to the headset still but no audio will come out. The microphone works, but nothing else. Logitech gaming software does not recognize it as well. This was verified on 3 different machines 2 Windows PC's and one Mac OSX.

Edited:
I contacted Logitech support and they had me send in my headset. Once they verified the headset was broken and not due to my neglect. They sent out a brand new headset to me. I've adjusted my rating to 5 stars because of how quickly they responded and sent out a new headset to fix the internal failure of the headset.

rating 4.0. Fantastic headset with a few quirks and frustrations I just recently purchased this headset and my first impression on this product is that it is high quality and sounds fantastic. Right out of the box, it FEELS like it's worth $100 and it doesn't feel flimsy at all. It comes with a few different pads for the top of your head, so I'm sure you will find one that is most comfortable for you. Listening to music it sounds great, and of course, for games it is also fantastic. But rather than discuss great things we already know about this product, I'm going to discuss things that people may be wondering, or things needs that need a bit of explaining.

First of all, this isn't a "true" 7.1 surround sound headset. It makes use of Dolby Pro Logic II technology which is widely used in video games and some films. The idea is that when you have a stereo file, or stereo data, you encode "surround" data into it. They used to do this back in the day on older films before they had true surround systems. The idea is when certain sounds are in or out of phase with eachother, they are thrown to a certain side or a certain channel so that they "pop" out of your stereo image, creating a wider, surround image. Some have noticed that it sounds like there is just "Reverb" thrown in the rear. This may be true, but it is simply to help create a better space and to create depth. Without it, it wouldn't sound like any sort of surround at all. When I first listened to a few games in surround mode, I wasn't mightily impressed. It felt a bit watered down. In fact, I upped the bass in the software a tad and I've grown quite used to it. It sounds fantastic in games like Battlefield 3. Everytime a rocket goes over my head I duck and get the chills. It's really immersive and I enjoy having the surround on while others may disagree. This is coming from someone who is a sound designer.

Secondly, the software it comes with is stereo. I did a decent amount of research before I got this headset, but obviously not enough. If the G35 software is ON, and you are in stereo mode, some of your games center channels will be tossed to the right channel. Logitech says it's nothing they can do and is up to how the developers program their games and audio channels. I think that's a bunch of crap. I just played Black Ops and Rage and all the dialog is in the right ear when you are in stereo mode. Terribly disappointing. However, turning off their G35 software fixes these issues. Of course, you can't use the switch on the headphones to switch back to surround mode since it's software driven. Basically: Software ON = Surround Mode, Software OFF = Stereo Mode if you want it to work correctly.

The USB cable is long and durable, however like many other reviewers have pointed out, it's stays creased and it's like an accordion that never quite stretches out straight. *shrug*

I've noticed other reviewers mentioning that it breaks easily at the joint where the actual earpiece rotates. I've been extra careful to set it aside when I'm done with it and not throw it around like crazy. Yes, it is high quality but the joint rotates so it fits your ear better. If you have kids around, or like to throw your headset around when you get pwned, this probably won't be for you. I can imagine it snapping off in a rage quit. However, I think for anyone looking for a high quality gaming headset for FPS's, you will not be disappointed. Sure, the 7.1 is a little "gimmicky" if you don't understand the technology behind it. It works best with games that are Dolby Pro Logic II enabled. Hope this has been helpful.

rating 1.0. Frankly, they're crap. I got these headphones a few years ago as a birthday present. I've seen every issue these headphones have and I'd be happy to report that here.
A. Memory Leak- The driver software occasionally leaks memory, causing an excruciatingly loud static hiss in the speakers, leaving my ears ringing on many occasions. I've only managed to remedy this by using a 4 year old driver which you won't be able to find without scouring Logitech forums.
(seen here - [...])
B. Blown speakers- By a combination of two factors, the speakers themselves blow and rattle quite easily. The first being the issue outlined in A, but also because the speakers themselves can't handle the bass they produce (which is iffy). About a month into using them you'll notice a very common crackling/bottoming out.
C. Cutting audio- I'm unsure if this is a fault of the blown speakers or the driver software, but high bass can make all audio from the speakers cut out. One instance I can think of right now is playing Project Zomboid, when your character is panicked, there is a fairly quiet heartbeat. This bass proved to be too much for Logitech, and conversation with my friend over skype came out as a garbled mess.
D. Poor quality control- The plastic on the headphones is as cheap as it comes. Two of my headsets crumbled to bits on the segment that attaches the speaker to the headband. I've rectified it on many occasions with duct tape, but due to the placement, it's a very temporary fix as before long, the cup will be falling off during use. (see attached)
E. They are not noise cancelling whatsoever. If you have music or something on, sure, you're going to have a difficult time hearing. But in quiet moments of movies/games, I can still hear background noise, people talking, etc....

When they do work though, I must say, they're pretty okay.

rating 4.0. Outstanding sound with a few minor issues I've had the G35 for a couple of months now and overall I think it's really good. Is it worth $117.99? I would say no. There are some things with the G35 that could use some improvement.

A headset is all about two things. One, the sound, and two, the microphone which is used to tell the other players how noob they are :) I'll talk about those two things then the structure of the G35 and the voice morphing.

The sound is awesome. I was seriously impressed when I tried the G35 for the first time playing L4D. Then I tried the G35 with L4D 2, and wow... even better. The sound is nice and crisp, and very clear. I mean, these days in video games the sounds and noises sound pretty realistic as is, but with the G35 it enhances it so much more. The sounds of shooting guns with the G35... awesome! I've also used the G35 with a bunch of other games too and it makes them sound better. The bass is great. It works very well. But the 7.1 integrated sound?? I don't know about that. I mean how much of a difference is 7.1 from 5.1? But I'll tell you this, with the G35 you do get great surround sound. But again... I can't really tell a difference between 7.1 and 5.1. I'm big on sound, especially when it comes to gaming. I have Logitech's Z-5500 5.1 (external speakers). Unfortunately, I can't use them because of the thing walls at my apartment. I don't want to tick off my neighbors, especially with that awesome subwoofer. I moved those speakers to the other room, which is further from my neighbors side, to use for surround sound with my TV... but I still have to keep the bass very low on that subwoofer because of my neighbors down below, but anyway...

The microphone works well. The design of it is awesome and it looks really cool. It is flexible and you can position it to your liking. It also can be pulled up to get it out of your way. Whenever I'm not using my mic I just fold it up so my mouth can have access to twinkies and hotdogs. JK. It has two ways of muting. One, folding it up, and two, press the mute button on the left ear side. Very simple and convenient. When it is muted, there is a red light at the tip of the mic so you know it's muted incase your forgot to unmute it. When playing Left 4 Dead and L4D 2, I use the mic all the time. It transmits nice and clear with very little and/or no distortion. In the logitech sound panel, you can adjust your mic transmission volume. You can also do that in most games that use mics.

The voice morphing - the idea of it is really cool, but it needs a lot of work. There are six sounds. Of those six, I like three of them - the giant (very deep sounding), the space squirrel (high-pitch funny voice), and the troll, which I think sounds pretty funny and unique. The other three sound very similar to each other. The problem with the voice morphing is that it doesn't transmit too well in the games. They aren't very clear and they're a little distorted. Also, they need to give you more customizable options in the sound panel. The "giant" voice sounds pretty good when playing online though. Sometimes I like to be a weirdo and use that voice to communicate to my team with. Usually gets a good laugh or it get people thinking "WTH is that!?" :) And the G35 also has a sound panel so you can adjust the sound (base, treble, etc.) to your liking.

The buttons are well placed. There are three "G" buttons on the left ear along with the mute button, volume control, and a switch for surround sound or stereo sound. The G buttons are used to customize what action you want to make happen when you press it. For instance, I use the G buttons for my giant, troll, and space squirrel voices. But like I said earlier, the voices aren't transmitted too clearly except the giant voice... people can usually understand what you're saying with Darkvader talk :) The stereo/surround sound switch is cool to have. For games, go surround sound, for music and videos go with stereo sound. Overall, its very, very convenient to have all the buttons on one side of the ear piece, especially for righty's because you can keep your hand on the mouse and kill noobs while controlling the headset with your left hand. The volume control works very nice too.

The overall structure of the G35 looks so cool. That's what really appealed to me... the looks of it - the nice black coat, the look of the mic, oh and I didn't mention this yet, but the cord too. The cord is so cool. It's a braided fabric-like cord which covers the wires as opposed to the typical rubber or plastic coating. It's also like 10 feet long or something like that. More than enough length than you'll need. Also, I have a bad habit of pulling on the cords of my devices... tugging on them too strong sometimes which have ultimately pulled the internal wires out and disconnected them from my devices (other headsets and other miscellaneous stuff). That's also a reason why I bought a new headset... I yanked the cord too much from my old headset which caused the mic to not work anymore. That would be very difficult to make happen with the G35 with its tough, rugged braided cord. But back to the structure of the headset. It looks nice, but I think that it's a tad too big. It has some very cool adjustments on both sides to get it to properly fit on your head, but it still leaves a decent size gap between the sides and your head and it makes it look big. But even though it may LOOk big, it fits pretty nice. I still think they can do a little more work on future headsets to make them a little more snug and fit better. When I shake my head with the G35 on, it gets loose up top. As far as comfort, it feels very nice. Maybe a tad bit on the heavier side, but not heavy enough to make it feel uncomfortable.

Last but not least is the USB connection. I think it's pretty cool because it's only one connection at the end as opposed to the typical three. However, I wish they had the standard headphone connections at the end (whatever they're called) with an adapter that comes with it. I think there are some other headsets that come with adapters at the end so you could connect it to gaming consoles and even your MP3 player in addition to your computer. For this amount of money for the G35, I feel that they should have implemented that.

Overall, I'm very satisfied with the headset. The sound is AWESOME and the mic works great, and that's what really matters for a gamer - one who wants great sound and a great mic. The voice morphing is pretty cool, but needs work... but it's a BONUS feature to have and your decision shouldn't be based off of that. I think $117.99 is a little overpriced for the G35. Maybe I'm naive, but I think around $75 would be very reasonable. But if you have some extra cash, then treat yourself to the G35. You won't be disappointed. If you're a little short on cash then I recommend going with a Koss headset. I used one (I think it was model SB49) before the G35 and I was very satisfied with it.

Hope this helps. Happy gaming :)

rating 1.0. Weak Joints Let me preface this by saying the sound quality of the headset is just barely above average, and that is not why i chose the 1 star rating.

I have gotten 2 of these headsets, the first the joint connecting the left earpiece to the head band SHATTERED, luckily no plastic in my ears or anything. I just considered myself unlucky. I ordered a return and replacement.

The second one lasted about 2 months longer. Then cracks started to show on the left earpiece, I taped it up to prevent shattering. Today I put on my headset and shards of plastic came down from the left earpiece below the tape as the joint completely failed.

This Headset does not meet my standards for something that is almost $100. To skimp on what could have easily been metal joints, and use cheap plastic that is not durable at all, is just embarrassing, Logitech. You've lost a customer.[...]

input: rating 5.0. REALLY NEAT GAME!!!  MY 6 YEAR OLD SON LOVES IT!! ALOT LIKE MARIO PARTY 8!!! FUN! FUN! FUN! Our family really loves Mario Party 8, of course nothing compares to it.  Mario is the bomb!!!  But this game is really neat and plays much like Mario party 8 (at least we think so).  It's alot like a board game.  I will admit in the beginning I was a little confused, but once you give it a chance and play a few different boards and spend some time getting to know the game, you realize how much fun it really is.  The confusing part for me was that when its your turn to go (you don't always see your man moving).  It took some getting used to but the mini-board on the side of the screen shows your man moving when its not his turn to see him move.  Also, your running and eating cookies (pac-dots as I call them) so, you never really know where your going to land since there is no distinct spaces on the ground like what you have in Mario party 8.  This way, I think it keeps you interested and surprised.  But its all good, the mini games are really neat and is a welcome change from playing the same mini games over and over in Mario Party 8.  I am a pac-man fan!!!  I have played pac-man when I was growing up, so it was nice to see the original pac-man game on here (like an extra bonus) along with dig dug and galaga.  I would definately give this one a try.  I pre-ordered it for the pre-order price, in the end I think it was around $40 something.  I'm not sorry either.  IF you could get it cheaper go for it!!! Also, about the wii controller, it works good with the game just as it does in Mario Party 8, but I would definately recommend a classic controller for the pac-man, galaga, and dig dug bonuses (it plays better with the classic controller).  Have an open mind and remember to play it a few times before making a judgement.  The first time we played, I played 5 times (different game boards).  Some game boards are longer than others, so if you don't want to play a longgggggggggg game you can play a short board.  I like that feature.  HOpe all of this helps.

rating 4.0. Fun for the Gaming Challenged! If you're not a gamer, you'll probably like this. The graphics are cute but not high quality. The dialog leaves a bit to be desired and the sound effects and music are very repetitive.

It's a board-game style with lots of mini-games worked into it. Great for kids and fun for adults that are willing to try something different. Very simple rules = no confused frustrated googling for answers.

I haven't run into any bugs that actually affect game play, just a couple of lag spots but never during the minigames.

Overall a very fun game, maybe not $40 fun but close.

rating 5.0. Pac-Man + Multi-Player Game = Family Fun I purchased this game (from my son) for my husband for Father's Day. We love it. The new game is a lot of fun play. You go to different places, try to get castles and collect cookies. If you land on a free spot - free castle. If two players land on the same spot and no castle is there yet - you get to pick a present to see who gets that castle. However, if you land on a space that has someone else's castle - LET THE BATTLE BEGIN!!! The battles are pretty funny (shaving sheep, making pizza, shooting flying pumpkins). You collect the cookies as you go through the game. You get them just moving, when you land on your own castle you get a bonus, and when you defend your castle and win you get more cookies (from the other player(s). The object is to get all the cookies first and make it back to the Cookie Factory. My husband, son (who is 5 years old) and I love it. Now if playing this new way is not your thing - no worries. The classic Pac-Man is on this disk to enjoy too. It also has the original arcade classics of Dig Dug and Galaza! Too much fun to miss!!!

current_product_reviews:
rating 5.0. Great Addition to the Pac-Man Franchise I've played a few dogs over the years with Pac-Man attached to it (what was the name of the game with the slingshot?), and I've been blown away by others (Championship Edition DX for XBOX is still addictive). This game is a perfect addition, especially for someone like me who is introducing my kid and my nieces and nephews to video gaming.

The game plays similar to Mario Party and like games, but there's unique ways to "roll the dice" to advance around the board. At first I didn't get the concept of "building castles" but after a few turns it begins to make sense. While the adventure and board games are fun, the real fun is the ability to battle in the mini-games without having to unlock them. Plus, it doesn't hurt that the original Galaga, Dig-Dug, and Pac-Man are all packed into this game. There's also much controller variety, from aiming and shooting to actually controlling characters with the d-pad.

For an old school gamer with a 4-year-old son, I would definitely recommend this game, not only for the young gamers but for adults as well
(and FYI, I picked this up for $15 for Christmas, which I think made this a win-win).

rating 3.0. Okay, but Sims 2 is better I love the Sims 2, but having it on my old desktop is frustrating because it is so slow. I recently got a new laptop and decided to try the Sims 3. I got the starter pack because I wanted the Downtown expansion. It's very different from The Sims 2. First off, I don't like that you cannot go into all of the buildings. I understand for the job buildings, but for the spa? Also, why are all of the nightlife locations in skyscrapers, yet you can only go to one floor of the building? It's not at all like apartment life, where I could choose my apartment, or have multiple families in one apartment building. There is one available apartment per building. It's odd.

I haven't really noticed how other families in the town age along with the playable household. I may end up liking that because that is my one complaint with Sims 2, it can take forever to age a family, and one your kids move out they can out-age parents if you play that household a lot.

The graphics are nice. I like the scenery. I guess the main gripe with the game is that even though it's easier to go to a community lot, it's much less satisfying. There is a limit to what you can do in the community lots, maybe that changes with some of the other expansions.

One thing I haven't tried because I don't understand how it works is buying a community lot. I don't think it works like Open for Business, so I am not sure what benefit it provides the Sim.

rating 4.0. Grows on you I've played Sims since Sims 1- this is the only video game I actually enjoy. Sims 2 was a clear improvement, but when I first installed Sims 3, I was really disappointed. I loved making different families all around town and having them interact with one another, creating unique stories. I never even used expansion packs- the game alone was enough for me. I decided to buy Sims 3 when Sims 4 came out and Sims 3 went on sale. Initially, I hated it. Story Mode was NOT for me. This is a function that allows other Sims in your neighborhood continue to live out randomized lives (having babies, getting married, even moving away) while you are playing your base family. This was a deal-breaker for me. However, I found a way to fix this- turn off aging and Story Mode. (You have to do both or else the other Sims will age and die while you're playing your base family). Then, use the testingcheatsenabled true cheat to age Sims whenever you're ready. This allows you to play in the same way as Sims 2, but now you have better graphics, smarter Sims, more options, and a whole town to explore! Having figured this out, Sims 3 is definitely my favorite game.

The great thing about this deal is that it comes with Late Night, which everyone says is the best expansion out there, aside from Generations and Seasons. It definitely is a lot of fun. The town in the base game is a little tame. In Late Night, your Sims can go to bars, become Vampires, and explore cool new lots (my favorite is the butterfly park). The town itself is beautiful and the premade houses are gorgeous. Getting this starter bundle is definitely the best option and I'm glad that I did. Overall, this is a great game and it only gets better with time.

From PC versions, on my phone, tablets, Xbox, and Wii you name it I've owned it. And this is definitely one of my favorites. The case does have three separate discs inside. One for the officailly sims 3 game and the two expansion packs. You will only need internet access to sign into Origin for the installment process. It takes awhile to download the game and additional expansion packs. The codes was a little of a headache but finally worth it when I actually got to play. The game can be played easily offline but do take note that you cannot earn badges or unlock acheivements and things like that in offline play. So playing online does have some perks. Overall I am highly satisfied with my purcashed

rating 1.0. I Goofed, Guys. I want to be as fair as possible here and explain my very poor review.  I bought a copy of the Sims 3 used from this store, not knowing that I needed an unused code to be able to play it. input:
rating 5.0. Best Sims Expansion in a Long Time I received my Sims 3 Island Paradise in the mail today from Amazon. Loading it up was just as easy as any of the other past sims games. I also received the limited edition material, but I havent gotten far enough to use it yet. So far, I love this expansion pack. It is fresh and exciting. The graphics are beautiful. The additions of the house boats, house on stilts, and resorts are awesome too.

Boats are how you travel from island to island. Sims can own their own boats and park them, just like you would a car. There are taxi boats too, which is something I didnt realize at first. Be aware - your sims may get sea sick but you can buy a reward to avoid it.

I have only played the game for a few hours but here are some of the additions I have noticed:

There are 2 new traits: Sailor & Loves to Swim.

Several new lifetime wishes: Deep Sea Diver (collecting items), Grand Explorer(discover/own all hidden islands), Resort Empire (5 star resort with major profit), Seaside Sailor (Lifeguard rescues)

New skills: Scuba Diving
I am really enjoying this feature. First you must learn to snorkel then you can move on to scuba diving. Some places are too dangerous to snorkel so you need to increase your level by practicing more. This aspect reminds of the World Adventures since you explore new caves - similar to the dive wells. You collect items, catch fish, and meet mermaids under water. There is probably more but I havent discovered it yet.

Honestly, I love this game so far. It is awesome... if you love sims, you will LOVE this. Its a GREAT addition.

Side note - there is not a prima guide for this game, which is surprising so if you want tips, you will have to seek them out on the internet.

Happy Sailing Sims

rating 5.0. I Love Being a Hotel Owner!! I absolutely love this expansion pack. I have missed owning a business like in the Sims 2 "Open for Business" expansion pack so this is a good substitute. I stayed up all night when I received it. My Sim took over the beach hotel in foreclosure renamed it and turned it into a "5 Star Resort". After reaching the 5 star level I received a deed to one of the undiscovered islands to do with as I please. I can build a house and live on it or open another resort. Running the resort is fun and hard work. I have not figured out how to get rid of the roaches even though I have hired the maximum number of high quality maintenance crew members working around the clock.

I used the blue print option to make updates to the property and added a lounge, dining area, pool with pool bar of course, a gym and an activity area consisting of Food stand and fire walk pits. I also added gambling at my hotel with the poker and roulette wheel. My Sim works sometimes as a bar tender or manning the front desk. It helps if your manager Sim is handy then you can make repairs easily. Of course Witch Sims can magically upgrade and repair items much faster. You can tell how well you are doing (or how bad) from the reviews from other Sims staying at your hotel. They critique everything food, landscaping, cleanliness, staff etc.

You decide the quality of food, whether or not to chlorinate the pool water, what shifts staff they will work.

As you can tell I have been busy running my hotel and have not played around with a lot of the other features. The island is beautiful and I love that you can scuba dive, windsurf, sail. There are a lot of new items: hair styles, clothes furniture. And of course mermaids and mermen have checked into my hotel. I could not wait for this EP to come out and I have not been disappointed.

rating 5.0. Awesome!!! I'm having so much fun playing Island Paradise! I love the new career options/lifetime wishes (Lifeguard, Deep Sea Diver, Explorer, Owner of a 5 star Resort) and don't even get me started on the mermaids. I certainly won't be bored this summer!

rating 5.0. Finally!!!! Finally EA has made an expansion pack, that i truly love (since Generations and Pets) and will never get bored with, this EP has so much to do in it and it is so much fun. My rich Sims can truly live the "Life Styles Of The Rich and Famous", You can own more than one home (I waited so long for this) and your Sims can live on house boats, swim, snorkel, become a life guard (new Job added) meet mermaids, live on your own island, own/run a resort, travel Island by Island on houseboat, speedboat, paddle boat, water cycle mobile, wind surf, you can still drive cars on land, deep sea diving, treasure hunt while under water, get attacked by sharks, it is also possible to be attacked by a Kracken/octapuss and more stuff to do. It is a series of Islands spread out in the tropics (of course) and yes you can change the weather to be always "summertime", you can buy house boats already build (1 to 3 bedroom) or build your own on a blank barge, it`s up to you. There are also hidden Islands that you can find (hidden by Fog) and other Islands you can explore/discover. There is also an "Island Survival" downloadable you can get and pretend you are "Tom Hanks" stranded on an Island. (smile) This EP is truly a dream come true and you will fall in love with The Sims3, all over again and become even more addicted than ever. I really and truly recommend this add-on to "The Sims3" Thanks

rating 4.0. Prepare to run a resort that the sims want to come to Like its nine predecessors,Island Paradise has all the bells and whistles you'd expect. New objects, new venues, new traits and skills, and new careers – all of them themed around this island and boating lifestyle. But the real attraction here is the addition of houseboats and the opportunity to build and manage your very own resort.

No longer is your Sim restricted to the ground beneath his/her feet. Houseboats are exactly what they sound like, your Sims' house on a boat, giving you the ability to go anywhere in the ocean. Aesthetically and functionally, it's just like any other house you'd build. Aside from the nautical themed decorations and All-in-one bathroom, there's not much different. You still have all your basic rooms and can perform all the same actions you'd do in a regular house (even going to work by way of water taxi). Being in the ocean is in no way limiting, but it's also not as rewarding as I'd like.

Gameplay when owning a houseboat does open up a bit. You can travel from dock to dock and travel the local islands, or you can take your houseboat (or ski boat or Jet Ski) for a whirl and unlock uncharted territories and open up new areas for you to drop anchor and discover. Underwater explorations through the scuba diving and snorkeling activities can lead to the discovery of exotic fish, caves, and shipwrecks. Like everything else you do, these activities serve as a nice break from the monotony of every day Sim life. It's worth noting, that to take part in some of these activities you need to increase your Sim's scuba diving skill.

But for as neat as owning a houseboat and swimming underwater can be, that alone isn't the attraction of Island Paradise. The notion of building and running your own resort was the highlight, mostly because it adds a new element to The Sims 3 gameplay. Resorts in Island Paradise don't follow the typical build-and-play routine. You can actually manage the resort, making all the decisions from what amenities you offer to the uniforms of your employees.

Owning your own resort can be a bit overwhelming, but it gives you something to strive for. Whether your building a resort from scratch or purchasing an existing one, it's up to you to get it the best rating possible – decided by guest reactions to the amenities, food, environment, and a ton of other factors. there are a lot of extras in the limited edition that makes it worth getting.

I love going underwater with my sim (not waiting on the side while it does its thing) and i love being able to search the ocean floor. Great game! highly recommend it!

rating 4.0. Excellent expansion for the Sims 3 franchise. ** NOTE: This is an expansion pack (EP). You must have the Sims 3 base game in order to use this expansion pack. **

EA's struck gold again with this expansion to its mega-popular Sims franchise. In this one, the theme is "fun in the sun," with beach- and resort-themed play and objects to enjoy. With Maxis at the helm, what could go wrong? Indeed, this is just one solid hit after another, with gorgeously thematic objects and Create-a-Sim (CAS) stuff and added gameplay that reinforces the theme.

EA's well aware that one of The Sims 2's strengths was the ability to own and run a business, and here, they give players the ability to develop and run a resort. Resorts are one of the most obvious new features to the game, and they're addictive to create. They can be themed a variety of ways, though the ones EA's favored are a traditional beach theme which is a bit Bohemian, "Spanish," which looks like Southern California to me, and something modernistic. The blueprint system they introduced a while ago gets a major update here with a variety of new rooms including pools, which have been majorly updated too and aren't just pretty landscaping anymore; you can install pool bars, slides, and waterfalls as well as the old-school diving boards. Every day, guests visit the resort and rate it, and their activities and ratings influence how much money the resort owner gets. Resorts can be placed in any gameworld just like any other lot type. Along with resorts, players also now have the ability to have any number of home lots in a world--and can move between lots (as well as gameworlds) almost effortlessly.

As to home lots, players can build as many homes or resorts in one town as they like, or chuck it all and just go live on a houseboat. Yep, houseboat. They're easy to build and handle, and their biggest strength is that they can sail--and dock--just about anywhere! I love houseboats.

Beaches have also been updated. Now players can direct their Sims to "play in the ocean" or even play with toddlers in the ocean, holding their little hands so the tykes can safely kick and laugh in the water. Sims can lay out in the sun (they get their own bright-colored towels to lay on). Any age of Sim can now make sand castles. And with a lifeguard chair on the lot, a Sim can now be a lifeguard, one of the new professions.

One of the other major updates to the game involves dive lots. A Sim can now go snorkeling or SCUBA diving and find treasure and meet mermaids. These underwater dive jaunts are so incredibly beautiful and evocative. I can't say enough how gorgeous these lots are. Some have treasure chests that can be opened like mummy sarcophagi in World Adventures; many have secret caves that can be explored (but watch out for the tentacled beasts!) as rabbitholes. And with careful planning, players can create their own dive lots.

The expansion's town, Isla Paradiso, is incredibly thematic. Each rabbithole and house looks perfectly in place for a Caribbean sort of theme, with colorful yellows, blues, pinks, and oranges predominating. The plants and building supplies are colorful and gorgeously wrought. The pre-made Sims are also perfect--I liked the back stories and appreciated the diverse cast of characters. The islands are time-consuming to travel, but there are some new methods of transportation--windsurfing boards, swan boats, speedboats, or just water taxis if you're poor. Or your houseboat, of course. The island chain also features secret islands discovered via gameplay.

I didn't see much here that was too sexually suggestive beyond the stuff that the Sims 3 already features; there are some new WooHoo locations, but as always, sexual activity is adult-only and carefully censored. There is obviously no cursing, and aside from the pool bars that serve juices of various kinds, no suggestion of alcohol or drugs that I've seen. Some of the new outfits for men and women alike get a little skimpy, which didn't bother me but might be of concern to others. If so, make sure to check out the online fansites' writeups about them before picking this up.

The Limited Edition version of this game includes a bunch of "stranded castaway" objects and CAS stuff. I LOVED that stuff; it's well-made all the way around and definitely looks spot-on for what it is. Anybody wanting to build "poverty" lots or needs more ragged clothing for a werewolf will like this stuff. I recommend getting the LE if you can.

Overall, I hugely recommend this expansion to anybody even vaguely interested in this particular theme.

As noted, this is an expansion, so it requires the base game in order to be played. It doesn't require an internet connection or Sims 3 forum account to use. I use the disk on both my Mac and PC, and will mention that on the PC it runs like a charm, but on the Mac, I get a lot of memory errors due to my machine not being top-of-the-line anymore. It doesn't run at all if I use all of my expansion packs, stuff packs, and Store downloadable content, but with the game totally crippled (I use about 20% of my total TS3 purchases on the Mac), it runs fairly tolerably. I'd be very cautious about getting this expansion if your Mac is already throwing Error 12s or crashing-to-desktop and you don't want to uninstall oodles of stuff; as you might be able to tell from my description, this EP is very memory-intensive and will definitely wreak havoc on a delicate ecosystem. I'm reluctantly docking this EP a star because I just can't play it on my Mac without seriously gimping my Sims 3 build. I wish EA would make a more Mac-friendly experience with this franchise.

rating 1.0. Not Ready for Prime Time I will agree that this expansion is the most entertaining one EA has ever produced. Sadly, it is unplayable due to constant huge lag spikes that can last for a minute or longer. Also, even in the short intervals between lag spikes this game has more bugs than a tenement, too numerous to list in one review. The ONLY way to play this game and actually enjoy it is to download and install a third-party mod called Overwatch from NRass. This will take care of 95% of the horrendous lag. But you have to wonder about a game that requires installation of third-party software just so that you can enjoy it.

EA is not known for their quality control, but this absence of quality staggers the imagination. My theory is that they wanted to get it on the shelves in time for Christmas, quality be damned.

But, as I said, once Overwatch is installed the game is fantastic. I just hope I don't have to wait too long for an update that renders the game playable on its own.

When the game arrived, I installed it and then checked for any updates. It said my game was up to date. Unfortunately, I had the same glitch problems as many others. The game would freeze for 30 seconds and then continue only to freeze again. This happened repeatedly so it took all the enjoyment out of playing. I spent most of my time waiting for it to unfreeze. Also, there were times when I would click on a specific destination (restaurant) and it would take me somewhere else (home). Luckily this expansion pack did not affect my other expansion packs which continue to work flawlessly. Now I will have to uninstall it. Such a waste of money for such a beautiful concept. Too bad because it was a lovely idea!

rating 4.0. Looks great so far. Looks great so far. I haven't had a chance to get deep into the game play, but it does have some pretty interesting things to do. This probably not for everyone. It is a CPU and graphics hog. I have a really good gaming PC with AMD 4.3 GHz CPU with 8 cores, Corsair 2400 MHz DDR3 RAM, and eVGA GTX760 graphics card with 2 GBs of video RAM. It's a great system for Call of Duty with everything turned up to the max. On Island Paradise, there is a little lag now and then - not bad but noticeable. I can't imagine what it would be like on my wife's laptop. I haven't tried turning all the settings to low or off and won't ... because.

I'm thinking this would be a nice vacation world. I'll play some of my other worlds most of the time and use NRAAS traveler and World Adventures to vacation on Island Paradise.

Happy sim'ing.

rating 4.0. Meh, use Paypal. You get a free month. I used this when I first started a toon and before my Warcrack addiction took hold of my soul.

If you don't have a checking account or credit card than this will work just fine for you to get your fix. If you have the option though, I'd recommend just doing the automatic debits.

rating 4.0. price watching I'm sorry I missed the $23 price mentioned in other reviews.  I have been tracking the price for the last 6 months and it seems to fluctuate between $24.99 and $29.99.  If you want to track it yourself, the easiest way is to put it in your shopping cart and move it to the "save to buy later" section.  Now every time you bring up your shopping cart it  will tell you if the price has changed since your last visit--there will be a message at the top of the shopping cart page.  If you can get it at $23, more power to you, but my recommendation would be to grab it when it hits $24.99.

rating 5.0. Good, easy gift for anyone who plays WoW At Christmas, I got this same thing for five people who play Warcraft, and they all loved it. rating 4.0. Safe way to pay for online account This card is worthwhile mainly if you do not wish to use your credit card on the NET and have one less location storing your financial information. I purchase this when Amazon has it on sale or at least at a price that would be less than the 3-month recurring charge you can purchase direct. And now that you can purchase the card and receive free shipping it usually is able to meet my criteria.

This prepaid card at the current price allows you to play the game cheaper than if you pay month-to-month or using the three months recurring plan by using a credit card with Blizzard. For me the added security of not having to give your credit card info for your subscription is a great bonus and this is perfect for anyone who wants to purchase a gift of playing time for anyone they know that plays the game.
rating 5.0. $30 WTF, Why raised Price They raised the price, it was $25 for 2 months. It is no different then regular subscription price now

rating 5.0. Why the BIG BOX!? this is a game code on a credit card sized card.  WHY THE BIG BOX!?  Yes, I understand it's a retail shelf box, but couldn't Amazon work a deal with Blizzard to get some kind of simple lightweight (GREEN) product version to ship?  come on...

rating 3.0. A better alternative Simply put, this is the best alternative when you want to control your subscription payments. As the nature of such, you can pay in advance and activate the card at a later time when you feel it is best. This can help to save you money in different times and seasons when you don't feel like playing. I have this issue since I hop, skip, and jump to different games, be it older or newer.

I also want to comment on the great pricing of the card. The standard one month rate is $15.99 last I checked, so you're saving a few bucks. Now, this can be refuted easily by suggesting that you can get a better discounted rate if you purchase the longer subscription renewals but you must be willing to play the game almost every day you spent the money for. I am not one of such players who can. I can attest to all the breaks-some short term and long term ones-over WoW. However, if you do get into the game enough, you may find that paying for a single month will not allow you enough time to enjoy what you're playing the game for.

I believe that 60 days at its own sequence is enough time. The best compromise between not having enough time from one month and realizing that you have too much game time that will end up getting wasted when you are well satisfied.

rating 4.0. Does not include the full version of WoW; it is only a time card! WoW is exceptionally fun, and this is a good way to pay for it, however, if you buy months in bulk on the Blizzard website, it is less than $15 a month.  Also, this card IS NOT AN EXTENSION TO TRIAL TIME AND DOES NOT INCLUDE THE FULL GAME!!! You MUST buy the game for 20$ BEFORE you can use this card.  Other than that, it is very easy to activate the game card using the instructions given.

rating 5.0. Safer than ongoing credit card subscription It's a time card. It works. Used one and giving the rest for Christmas presents.

As for the game, it's not perfect but haven't found a better one yet.

EDIT: Update January, 2015. Last year I had two credit cards compromised. Both times fraudulent charges appeared within a few days after I had purchased the expansion or renewed a subscription by entering credit card info at their website. I have several other credit cards I use all the time at other websites and none of them have been compromised, only the ones I used for WoW. Therefore I will no longer give Blizzard my credit card info. I am not saying Blizzard's site was breached (they denied it of course), I am just giving the fact that MY cards were breached, just after I had used their website, on two occasions. Might be coincidence, but I'm not going to risk a third event.

The credit cards I have with Amazon have never been compromised. Therefore from now on I will buy time cards instead of setting up the ongoing subscription, it appears to be safer.

rating 5.0. prepaid is good I buy these for my son so he can play WoW and I don't have to worry about someone jacking his acocunt and somehow finding a live credit card number.

rating 4.0. Cheaper than paying monthly on website Got this for $29.95 here at Amazon. As of this writing game costs $14.99 a month via the game website. But game website charges sales tax so bring monthly bill to around $16.50 so thats $33 for 60 days. With Amazons free shipping and no tax charged in NYC area I get it for $30. so a savings of $18 a year. Some say why not use the 6 month or 12 month plan on website to save money. Well cause I have to lay out that money up front. So that 12 months up front could be in bank getting 5% interest. Only have to lay out for 2 months at a time.

Better deal as far as I am concerned.

rating 5.0. Does the job well! I always purchase these cards for my boyfriend through Amazon. We love to play WoW but I love to spoil him by getting him these cards every once in a while. The shipping is always extremely fast (go, UPS!) & the cards work like a charm.
I guess my one complaint would fall on the lap of UPS. They pack the card in a ginormous box & use the air bag things to fill it up. Why not save packaging & use a much smaller box? No need to waste that much space on this little card.

rating 4.0. The most convinient way to get your WoW fix After many months of debate I finally caved and bought World of Warcraft. It's great fun and all that but I found one thing I didn't like: The Payment Method. You are expected to give a credit card number and every time your subscription runs out they go and charge that credit card for the next period.

I don't like that that at all. First, I'm not fond of just giving my credit card number for a long term charging option like that. Not just in Warcraft but gyms, online subcriptions and the like. Second, you leave yourself suceptible to being charged when you dont want to.(Like when your trying to square away your credit card)

That is why I find the pre paid card the best payment option. You buy what you need. When your running low they let you know and when I finnaly get bored, its over. I dont have to make calls or get online to de-authorize the use of my card. I also think this is a lot safer than credit card subscrptions.

The only flaw? This is a LOT of packaging for something that you are going to use just once and throw away. Such a big box for a card the size of a standard credit/phone card and even the card you throw away after you scratch the number and punch it in. They are all the same so its not like you collect them or anything.

Despite that, this is the ONLY way I play WoW. Highly recommended.
