rating 5.0. Great price for the quality Firstly, one important thing to address is that this is a review for the Razer Basilisk X mouse, not the Razer Basilisk Ultimate, which is the majorly upgraded version of the mouse. Most reviews, for some reason, are of the Basilisk Ultimate, but with the one picture and the one you order on this page, you will be getting the battery powered Razer Basilisk X.

TL;DR: I used this mouse for a month for office use. Great price for what you get. Feels nice in hand using common grips (claw, palm, fingertip), responsive clicking, nice scrolling, glides well across mouse mats, fair for gaming, really good battery life.

I have only ever used wired mice. I wanted a wireless mouse for office use because my desk got smaller. I also share a desk now, and wires go everywhere from our headsets, keyboard, desk decorations, etc. To say the least, I wanted something cheap, but something I could still rely on with decent quality for the price. After doing some extensive research, I limited myself down to Logitech or Razer. When I finally laid eyes on the Basilisk X, I liked the look and the features. The more I thought about it, the more I fell in love with it. I was skeptical, as I've never used a wireless mouse. However, below are my thoughts after a month's use regarding the mouse and why I think, for the price, this mouse deserves five stars.

To start, the price falls at $59.99 at the time of writing this review. Even in paying full price, I felt that I was receiving a great quality mouse. Below are some more specifications of this.

Firstly, when getting the mouse, you get some detailed instructions regarding how to set it up. This is a nice touch, as trying to offhandedly figure out where the battery compartment and the USB stick is can be difficult. The cover lifts up nicely, but is a little hard to put back on. Ultimately, though, it has a nice compartment for the USB stick and for the battery. This mouse takes one AA battery inserted into the mouse. The weight of the mouse is very light and it's easily mobile. The build of the mouse feels decent for the price, and seems durable. The look overall of a matte black with some glossy parts and highlights is personal preference, but I do like its simplistic look with no lights on it.

Overall, the feel of this mouse is nice in the hand. I used it in a few different grips to sample how it would feel. I used claw, palm, and fingertip, the most common grips I could think of. When using each grip, each time, I did feel relatively comfortable. The mouse fits rather well in my medium-large hands. I personally use a hybrid palm and claw grip, in the sense that my palm encompasses a large amount of the mouse, but my fingers are raised slightly. It is a little less comfortable for this type of grip, but you can still feel comfort using the mouse. It seems from my usage the mouse is mostly intended for palm grip, but that shouldn't discount other grips at least trying the mouse. It's much better than the mice the office provides me, trust me on that. As for the other parts of the mouse, such as the buttons and scroll wheels, I like the clicky responsiveness of the mouse buttons. Also, my fingers don't hang over the edge, even when using palm grip, which I personally love. I rather like the scroll wheel because It doesn't take too much pressure to move it up or down, but locks into place pretty solidly with ease. It is relatively easy to scroll at fast paces, but not seemingly as easy as a Logitech mouse, which my friend allowed me to use for a moment (more on that later). There are three other buttons to be used, one below the scroll wheel, and two next to the thumb. One complaint I had about some other mice I used, including Steelseries and (in some mice) Corsair, involved me accidentally pressing this sensitivity button below the scroll wheel. Well, I can safely say I've never had an accidental press of the sensitivity button. Since I am clumsy, though, I do accidentally press the two buttons on the side of the mouse every so often. However, whenever I have a mouse that has buttons in those places, I always click them on accident, no matter the mouse, so I cannot fault it for that. The buttons on the side stick out a little more than I usually see on other mice, but they don't get in the way, especially the sensitivity button. It sits flush with the two buttons used to click. Overall, you get pretty easy access to the thumb buttons when using the provided thumb rest as well. I can say it has a more simplistic design, which some will favor over multiple buttons. The cover in the back for the battery and USB holder have never come off accidentally for me, and I haven't had issues otherwise with the build. I'd say if you have medium hands, this would probably be the mouse for you, but with someone like me who has hands that are somewhat large, it works well still.

Moving along swiftly, I'll now cover how the mouse performs when moving it around, and then dive into gaming with the mouse. Overall, on a decent quality mouse mat, the mouse glides really smoothly across the surface and calibrates easy. All you have to do to calibrate it to the surface you're on, software or not, is hold down the button just below the scroll wheel for a few seconds, and then it is calibrated and optimized for the surface it's on. It's never caught itself on anything and feels pretty smooth, albeit a little bit speedy. Otherwise, it's very elegant in its dance across a surface.

Finally, the point that I've been looking forward to most, which is gaming. This is a gaming mouse so, after all, how does it perform for gaming? Well, after spending a few days gaming with this mouse instead of my regular mouse, a Corsair Nightsword, I can say it isn't too bad. Granted, I will always recommend a wired mouse for certain types of games, but if you want something wireless where delay and response time are not as important, this could be a great mouse for you. I spent time playing Destiny 2, Call of Duty Modern Warfare, and even the rhythm game osu! The delay is a little more noticeable here, but it is still not that bad. It was easy to be precise, though, of course, it was an adjustment. After playing with it a few days, I was able to play at around my normal level, which isn't that good to begin with. Needless to say, it does its job with certain types of games, but for the FPS genre or reaction time sensitive games, you might notice the delay. Granted, you could probably get used to it, but you might get better results from a higher tier mouse or from a wired one. That's not to say that the mouse didn't perform, because it very well did. I'm sure that if I played Dota 2, an RTS, or an MMO, that this mouse would still be viable, but from what I understand, you may want some more keybinds readily accessible if you're going to play an RTS or MMO. To wrap it up, though, it does its job.

Lastly, battery life. I went into this with a grain of salt. I saw a review saying it ate batteries in the matter of a week and a half repeatedly, so for Razer to claim up to 285 hours of hyperspeed wireless, I was a bit skeptical. However, I had one Energizer battery that came with the mouse (thanks Razer!) and it lasted a full month. Keep in mind, this was for office use, and I have a desk partner. As soon as she leaves, I am right there to take over her station (she loves the mouse too by the way). She works eight hour shifts five days a week, while I work six hour days, four days a week. I figured calculating out this mouse's approximate battery usage using just these numbers would be inaccurate or possibly a bit skewed, so I'll just leave it at the pure statistics of how often we use it to prove the battery life. Also, only one AA battery. To say the least, you may pay for some batteries, but I really can't complain with the battery life I get from it.

So, if you've stuck around this long, I want to thank you for sticking around, and I hope that if you're considering buying this mouse, I have laid out what my experience with this mouse was for a month regarding office usage. Take the gaming section with a small grain of salt as I didn't use it for more than a couple days. And again, this article is mostly opinion, but I tried to keep it open to show some cons, but mostly pros for why I like it. Thanks again!

rating 2.0. It's good when it's working correctly, which used to be often and now... isn't. I got this to replace an absolutely infuriating Logitech wireless mouse, and for a month or so it was pretty good at it. The switch on the bottom is excellent for switching between two computers without having to pair and unpair over and over again, and the battery life has been adequate enough for my purposes. But there are a couple of serious issues, one that was present from the beginning and one which has cropped up within the last two weeks.

First off, the software is weird, and the way the mouse works with it is even weirder. After figuring out that I didn't actually need to sign up for a full Razer cloud account just to access the settings for a device that is literally on my desk in front of me, I tried to set up some custom button actions. I use multimedia software a lot so I like having Undo and Redo on the two side buttons in that software, while still having those buttons assigned to Forward and Back for when I'm on the web. The software made setting that up easy, except that for me, I had to explicitly assign every single application on the computer to use either Back/Fwd or Undo/Redo because it only detected changes between apps. I've had to reopen 3D apps multiple times a day just to remind the driver that I was no longer using them. The issue also messes with switching between two computers, because the mouse apparently remembers what the buttons do, so if I'm stuck on Undo/Redo, I have to return to the system and app that caused it to be set for that, then change from that app to another assigned app that has the settings I want, then finally switch back to the system I was using before. (As an aside, the driver is supposed to notify you when you go from one set of button assignments to another, but in practice it only occasionally does so and it's usually a minute or two late, a bit like a fuel warning light in a car that only comes on after you've pulled into a gas station.)

But bad drivers are something I can deal with. What I can't deal with is the hardware failure that just showed up in the last three weeks. The left mouse button now intermittently either fails to click, double clicks when pressed once, or releases and clicks again while being held down. At first it just happened once or twice a session, but it quickly turned into a constant issue. Even the most fleeting of single clicks results in a double click, while it takes serious pressure to avoid dropping a dragged file while attempting to move it. New batteries do nothing. Uninstalling and reinstalling the drivers does nothing. Blowing canned air through the thing does nothing. The switch itself is clearly failing, and this is a device that is less than 3 months old and is supposedly rated for millions of clicks.

Every time I buy a new mouse, I have to remind myself not to get a Logitech. Unfortunately, it looks like I'm now going to have to include Razer in that reminder as well.

rating 2.0. Dropped to two stars - audio does not work outside of games. Otherwise, pristine Xbox gaming experience! Update: I cannot, for the life of me, get this headset to works in apps outside of a game. I've tried Plex, YouTube, Halo Waypoint, Groove Music Videos, and more. For some reason, the audio will completely drop-out, and the only way to restore the audio is to unplug/replug the audio dongle in again. Sometimes, I can get audio to work briefly in non-game apps, but it almost always will go haywire and disconnect. This has to be a driver issue as it makes no sense whatsoever - but something is up with how the Xbox routes audio to the dongle. I've tried this on two Xbox consoles that I own, so it's not just a defective console. Additionally, I tried it with a separate brand headset which does the same thing (PDP AG7) - so this is an Xbox driver thing, but I don't know who is responsible - MS, or Turtle BEACH!

PROs:

- Excellent wireless gaming audio - clear, crisp - significantly improved over plugging a headset into the bottom of an Xbox controller. Occasional static crops up over a couple hour gaming session, but I have yet to experience a full drop-out.
- 50mm drivers do pack a really nice punch, and bass boost mode does not drown out the rest of the audio spectrum. Mids are a little muddy during music listening – but overall the sound quality is pretty good here, but obviously tuned for gaming. I don’t think the audio modes work with line-in music sources, but I’ll keep testing.
- Fast power up and sync with Xbox – it takes just a second to power on, and you get nice audio cues through the headset to confirm
- Mic feedback is executed very well - voice quality on "other" end seems high!
- Removable mic (see also CONs section)
- All plastic design is very lightweight (see also CONs) - also perforated ear cups help SOME with heat build up inside over other synthetic headsets.
- Can be used for simultaneous audio with another audio source - or stand-alone with your favorite audio source (tablet/smartphone music service, et al). Yes, you can listen to your favorite music through your phone while GAMING at the same time! Volume is controlled through your phone/iPod volume.

CONs:

- Light effects - please TB (and all other game console headset manufacturers), stop putting LED lights in every console headset. There's no way I can see to turn off the green LEDs. In a dark room, the bright green light on the USB dongle itself is too much as well. Time for some black electric tape!
- No Passive listening mode: Using with tablet/phone/iPod requires the headset to be turned on! This results in the logo LED lights on both ear cups to blink rapidly - also, the headphones will go into "power save" mode because they are not connected to the Xbox console. You can press the power button once when an audio prompt chimes in that the headphones are about to turn off - "press power to cancel". Unfortunately, you might not hear this prompt if you listening to music or another audio source loudly.
- All plastic headset feels a bit low-quality for this premium price - I guess that's the tradeoff for the weight? I would have preferred a steel or aluminum headset band however. Price should be $99 for this material level - hopefully it will come down in price over time.
- Comfort - I wear a 7-3/8 ball cap - I have to stretch these headphones to their limits, and the pressure on the crown of my head is still uncomfortable during any listening/gaming session.
- Controls – the little buttons and wheels are a bit hard to find when on your head. I wish the MUTE button was easier to find

SUMMARY - The Stealth 420X is better than any headset plugged into the bottom of the headset from my experience. The signal quality is reliable (in-games), and game audio is clearer and more powerful – truly immersive with no jarring static/audio popping or requirements to unplug and re-plug into the bottom of a controller to improve a bad connection. Since becoming a new dad, I’ve been waiting on this type of truly wireless setup. But until my odd issue with the audio not working reliably in Apps other than games, I have to give these a 2/5.

Some of the way they advertise this headset is a bit deceptive (use them for tablet gaming) – because it seems like more of an afterthought that you can use these for anything other than Xbox gaming. Yes, you can use them for general purpose use, but you’ll have to deal with the lack of passive listening, deal with the aggressive power save shutdown (you have a couple seconds to hit the power button to prevent a shut-down), and the rapidly blinking LED lights when not sync’d to the Xbox.

I plan to buy the new PDP AG7 to compare, which cost 50 bucks less. I think we are paying for the recognizable Turtle Beach branding here. It might be worth the premium – if TB support and warranty coverage is better than PDP.

rating 5.0. This headset is a step in the right direction.. After some disappointing purchases of Turtle Beaches in the past, I decided to try one more time for the perfect headset for my needs. I really did not want to spend 300+ dollars, so I decided to go middle ground with this headset. I have completed my first marathon gaming session and this 420x performed like a champ. As for the fit, the headset is very lightweight and comfortable, but the earpads were slightly stiff. Let me get right to what I thought of the sound with respect to my needs. The sound is much better than my PX4's and is on par with my Kingston HyperX Cloud II's as far as clarity and overall sound field is concerned. I would not consider myself an audiophile, but I have quite a few headsets over 100+ dollars to compare with. The 420x's don't have surround sound, this is stereo sound, but it is done with great balance throughout the sound field. Also the sound was great outside of Xbox One, I am listening to Spotify on my PS4 with my 420x's while I write this. Now onto my positive and negative points.

PRO's
-Very lightweight
-Great battery
-No hissing or distortion
-Suprisingly good stereo sound (right amount of treble and bass, not overpowering).
-ability to control every aspect of chat, mute, volume, and audio presets on the headset.
-100% wireless
-four types of audio presets
-active mic monitoring
-outstanding chat clarity
-can achieve very high volume with little to no distortion
-Microsoft's play and charge usb cable will also charge this headset. That cable is about 8 feet long.
-can work with PS4 for audio with a 3.5mm to 3.5mm cable (not included). Chat also works, I chatted in a private party on my PS4.

Secondly, now for the things I thought could be better or could be improved upon. I was concerned with the all plastic headband and frame. After asking many of the vine reviewers here on Amazon, none really answered the question of stability and if the headband was cheaply made. I reached out to Turtle Beach support and was reassured that the mistakes made with the Stealth 400 headset and the cracking headband for PS4 were not present in the 420x. I also felt that 158 dollars shipped isn't quite a great deal either, I feel that this is slightly a overpriced headset at this time.

CON's
-build quality could be much better, far to much plastic
-not a versatile headset, I will discuss this in more detail below
-149.99 is much higher than Sony's offering of the Gold Wireless Headset (wireless and simulated 7.1 surround sound) that is usually less than 99 dollars.
-not full noise cancellation
-mic monitoring is non-adjustable
-usb cable provided for charging is very short
-the overall headset headband is not flexible at all

My list of cons is mostly things that are slightly nitpicking, but this headset is really terrific overall, I am surprised at how it performs. Also this headset did work with my PS4 as well, but could not be powered by the 3.5mm alone, it needs to be plugged into the DS4 and also needs to be powered on which is going to drain the battery, this headset was made primarily for Xbox One only. It also works well with mobile devices, but this isn't a headset I would pickup for PS4, so please take my advice and go with another solution for that. My big worry is the plastic headband, I will update this in a few months to discuss durability. Despite any cons I could find, I am still going with 5 stars because this headset does many things right and not many things wrong. I commend Turtle Beach for going in the right direction with the 420x.
Update:09/27
The durability seems to be good so far, nothing is showing cracks. I have replaced the stiff earpads with Brainwavz HM5 memory foam earpads and the comfort is really great now.

rating 5.0. One of the best headsets Turtle Beach has put out in a while! This is a great product. I was pretty harsh in my review of the Turtle Beach Elite 800x... but this headset is an improvement in many ways.

Mic: First and foremost this is a gaming headset. It has to work perfectly in that respect or what's the point. This mic picks up my voice crystal clear. The mic monitoring also works great. And the simple volume & chat controls makes it easy to adjust so that the balance is just right during a party chat.

Wireless: Connectivity here is amazing. Can't stress this enough. No hissing or any noticeable clicks or anything. Honestly it's probably the quietest headset I've ever used in terms of annoying background static/noise/hiss (that includes wired and wireless headsets). The range is more than adequate for me to put the controller down in between matches to get a drink, bite to eat, or use the restroom (with mute on of course!) while still hearing good humored party chatter between games. It's also worth noting that my Xbox is less than a foot away from my wireless router, but I still don't get any wireless interference.

Comfort: The cushions are a bit stiff IMO, but the headset is so light and it doesn't squeeze into your head so it doesn't really matter. It's no problem wearing this for an extended period of time. The padded bar at the top is probably the best feature and keeps everything secure w/out being tight. Not the most comfortable headset I've ever had, but it's good.

Sound: Stereo sound is just as good as I've heard in other headsets. Comes with a few presets to crank up the treble or bass if you want. I'm no audiophile, but I have no complaints here.

Overall: This is a great headset. The best I have found for the XB1 for sure (even compared with more expensive options such as the TB Elite 800x)! And the fact that it's 100% wireless (not even a chat cable is needed) is what really pushes it over the top. I can't help but feel like some of TB's offerings back in the Xbox 360 days were superior, but I've been waiting impatiently for a really good XB1 headset that worked well in every area and so far this is the 1st one that checks all the main boxes. I'm very happy with them at $150, but as the price starts to fall over time these are going to be an extremely good bargain.

5 stars! Not because it's perfect, but for right now it's the best bang for your buck.

***EDIT*** I did have one issue with this headset that was resolved... the mic works a little too good and picks up my breathing. I contacted TB support and they said they would send me a foam cover for the mic head which would filter out my breathing directly on the mic. Was glad to hear this because I had already figured that out and used a mic cover I had pulled from an older headset (which did fix the problem). May not be necessary depending on where you keep the mic, but for me it was.

rating 4.0. Houston, We finally have WIRELESS GAMING on XBox1.......for under $300 Good headset from Turtle Beach.  The Pro's:  EASY Setup with your xBox1, comfortable fit, mic monitoring (so you hear yourself), Mute, independent volume for game and chat volumes, 4 presets to somewhat change sound profile, quick-charging, long-lasting Li-Ion battery, NOT as Expensive as 800X, COMPLETELY WIRELESS GAMING (again, and finally) without an adapter.  The Con's: sound presets aren't appreciably different, not surround-sound-grade like previous XP500 headset, but also $50 cheaper.  Overall review, good headset for long gaming sessions where sound isolation, comfort, and WIRELESS connectivity are desired.  Setup took less than 3 minutes, worked without intervention and others said my voice was clear and normal sounding, even better than my Polk Audio 4Shot.  I think if you're looking for a headset that is completely wireless and has good sound and are willing to lay out $150, this is the best investment going.  Does it sound like the Stealth 800X?  No.  Does it cost $300?  No.  Is it wireless and sound good?  Yes.  Not audiophile good, but pretty impressive.  You'll hear all the little things you've been missing and you'll enjoy the soundscape. Also connects to phones/portables/tablets and it sounds fine.  Will they last?  Time will tell.

rating 5.0. This is the headset you want for Xbox One (as of 8/27/15) After two lousy experiences with Turtle Beach, I bit the bullet and bought these. I'm shocked by how much better this unit is than the others.

I had a Turtle Beach 800X on my original Xbox and it was a love/hate relationship. Much of the hate was that it was tied to a massive base station for charging and that it completely failed me (even with purchased converters) when I tried to move it to the Xbox One.

For my birthday this year, my wife bought me the 500X. The offering seemed great, but it was issue after issue. After feedback through the mic, I updated it per support instructions. It then lost complete mic ability. Support then lead me through a factory reset and re-update. It worked for a week, then just plain wouldn't turn on. I returned it for a refund and cursed Turtle Beach thoroughly.

After much research, I couldn't find a decent option, so I decided to give the 420X, which had just released, a chance. Wow, am I happy I did.

Pros:
- Good sound and clarity
- It charges from any outlet with a USB adapter, so I can keep it near my sofa instead of at my console 15 feet away.
- There is no massive base station. It's a simple USB thumb drive sized adapter.
- Literally plug and play. Plug the USB adapter into the Xbox, wait a few seconds and you're on. No changing settings, no updates, nothing.
- The buttons are laid out well, and simple to use.
- All of the things the box says. It's fully wireless, has a good battery, mic monitoring, etc.

Cons:
- I really haven't found any. I guess the sound could be a bit better, but that's really it. I wasn't expecting Sennheiser quality, so it met my expectations.

rating 2.0. Volume level Issues - otherwise great Downgrading review from five stars to two stars after a few weeks of use. You can see my original review on how to get dolby below. However, I have noticed that the lowest volume level is actually not low at all, but very loud. In fact, the problem is that the value cuts off completely about 60% of the way down the dial. Basically from zero - 40 there is no volume, then the volume kicks in at a level a tad higher than is comfortable, and then of course you can make it louder. This is across all games and apps on xbox one. I am sending in for an RMA to see hopefully if a new one addresses this problem. I will post the results when I get the new one back. There is no setting in xbox to adjust the volume.

Original Review:
They sound great and are incredibly easy to setup. A few tips:

1 - Fully charge them before you do anything else. When you first get them charge them before use - mine took about ten minutes to fully charge from the moment they arrived (partially charged). They are red when charging and not when fully charged.

2 - You need to find a paper clip to push a small hole to sync the transmitter to the headphones. You only need to do this once. I actually had a hard time finding a paper clip. I am not sure why they couldn't include one in the box. Pleas give me a paper clip with a Turtle Beach logo on it - that would be more useful than the refridgerator sticker with logo that is included.

3 - You still need to turn them on though by clicking the turtle beach logo on the right headphone panel to use them. Turn them off when not in use to save power.

4 - To get 7.1 surround sound, get a Turtle Beach Earforce DSS (I have a version 1 not a 2) - plug the DSS the optical cable into the xbox optical out and the DSS USB cable into a USB on then back of the xbox. In xbox settings go to 'display and sound', change optical to bitstream out and bitstream format to digital dolby. Push the button on the DSS to enable dolby. Plug the headphone USB dongle into the out port on the DSS unit. Congrats - you now have 7.1 Digital Dolby surround sound.

rating 4.0. Great product, almost no noise Noise level:
For what it is, it’s pretty good. The noise isn’t completely silent, but I’m not sure if you could find much better. If you’re not sitting directly in front of your screen then it’s hardly noticeable, and pretty much completely unnoticeable if you have your volume turned up slightly. One downside is that I’ve noticed that it stays on for a few minutes after you turn off your system however it’s manageable for the price and product.
Sheerness?:
Not 100% on what this word means but if it means the quality of the product then you’re in luck. I’ve had mine for a few months now and no problems at all with heavy usage. Even having it on playing for 10+ hours, no increase in noise, no lagging, and the actual power box doesn’t seem to reach too high of a temperature where it might be dangerous. Overall I am very pleased with how well this has held up.
Value for money:
Trust me, compared to all the other more expensive options out there, I think this is your best option. It’s cheaper than most others I could find and has given me plenty of value. input:
rating 5.0. Better than 2011 I used FS 2011 for hundreds of hours. It was good but it wasn't great! FS 2013 is much better. When you set up a hired hand to take a tractor/implement the program aligns it with the field. Great improvement. The real nice feature is the new implements and items added by Giant that load flawlessly. I've tried several sdown load sites and it's harder than FS 2011 download placement. It does work at times, but mostly doesn't. I haven't tried the easy or hard mode, but chose the Normal mode. It's hard but far more realistic than FS 2011. The milk production income is harder to read and mowing grass seems spotty. Would have been nice to see hired workers to drive fertilizing, mowing so we would have better time to haul harvested crops. The horseshoes gathering is not worth the effort. Hard to find and no where as good as the bottles in FS 2011 which were difficult enough.

Overall, great to have the new version and I look forward to many great hours running the farm.

rating 5.0. FARMING SIMULATOR 2013 LOVE IT..I STARTED WITH FS2011 AND COULD NOT WAIT TILL 2013 CAME OUT.
IF YOU ENJOYED FS2011 YOU WILL LIKE THE NEW FEATURES IN 2013

rating 5.0. Enjoyable I love simulator-style games, and I thought I'd give this one a try. I never played any of the previous versions. It was after watching several "Let's Play" videos on YouTube that I decided that I wanted to try it out for myself. First thing I did was go into the game setup and change the keyboard controls around to make it more comfortable, and then I was good to go.

I'm glad I watched the YouTube videos first; the .PDF documentation that came with the Amazon download seems kind of sparse. The ingame tutorials helped, but I got lost on one of them. Luckily someone made a YouTube video on that as well.

I still haven't tried the Multiplayer settings yet. I've discovered that my friends get offended when I start making demands that they come and work my fields and milk my cows, but I keep trying. Anyway, despite the learning curve and reluctant friends, I find the game relaxing and fun.

Bigger airports look excellent, but as with all sims like these, if you want truly gorgeous scenery, go with addons.

input: rating 2.0. poor technical support for faulty joystick I bought this for elite dangerous on the pc, initially it worked great but a day after I got it one of the buttons just stopped working altogether, the worst part is that there's no quick way to get help on this issue as the company makes you jump through hoops just to ask them a question, it is day 3 that I write this so I wouldn't be surprised if other buttons suddenly stop working as well.

rating 5.0. I haven't used it a great deal since I received it I haven't used it a great deal since I received it, but what little I've used it leaves me with a positive impression.  It seems pretty solid for the price I paid, and I would recommend it to anyone else.

rating 4.0. Excellent value for money Very good stick, well built, weighty, and the ability to separate the components is terrific. This stick was made with Elite Dangerous in mind. The only slight downgrade is for the lack of a way to secure it to a surface.

rating 3.0. It was cheap after all... I purchased this HOTAS six months ago and have used it off and on. Fortunately, I've only used it with one game and Steam shows my total time in that game at about 220 hours, so that's about how long this HOTAS worked for me before one of the buttons broke. Unfortunately, those of you who play Elite: Dangerous will understand, that's one broken button too many. I need all of the buttons on my HOTAS to play at my best, most of them having multiple functions.

I had a generally good experience using this setup, but it did always feel cheap to me and has now proven to be short-lived. It is a cheap HOTAS after all. I suppose 220 hours for a little less than $50 (so about 4.4 hours of use per dollar) is... annoyingly low. A my $50 Xbox One gamepad still works just as well as at purchase after at least 1000 hours of far heavier usage.

Unfortunately I have been unable to find any warranty information or a means of returning the product, at least without having to create an entire user account at Thrustmaster's support website (a little too high an entry fee for mere customer support I find). I'll just be swallowing this loss and buying a better HOTAS in the future.

rating 5.0. Great HOTAS for anyone looking for a quality joystick Pros:
-Great Control, as well as adjustment for force needed to move Joystick.
-Great feeling buttons with fantastic action.
-Quality of build is superb. Feels very sturdy and durable.
-The ability to screw together the two pieces leaves you with a very solidly connected HOTAS.

Cons:
-The cord connecting the two main pieces is rather short, giving you limited space to separate the two.
-The main USB cord to the computer feels slightly shorter than I would like, but that is more personal preference.
-I would have liked there to be more buttons available on the HOTAS, there seems to be a lot of empty/unused space.

Who would I recommend this to?:
-Anyone looking for a joystick (Trust me, just buy this one instead)
-Anyone who wants a HOTAS but doesn't want to break the bank
-Someone who is not 100% hardcore with flight sims (I.E. You aren't learning to manually do each step to turn the A-10 on in DCS)

rating 5.0. Awesome for my flight sim Awesome for my flight sim.  I can finally fly helicopters with my rudder peddles.  I was surprised by the nice weight and feel of this stick and thrust.  I was expecting it to feel a bit more cheap.  I just plugged it in, hit the preset button and didn't have to map anything.  Was plug and play for Prepar3D and made helicopters a blast to fly.  Did have trouble getting controls to work in Arma 3.  The game did recognize the stick and my rudder peddles but I couldn't get the to work.  I think that is an Arma issue.  I am sure I can Google a fix for that.  Overall, my first few hours of flight time was great.  Controls feel nice and much higher quality than expected for so cheap.
rating 5.0. Very Nice!!! It is a great Joystick!!! Perfect with FSX, it might be better if it has ForceFeedback, but in the end it doesn't matter.

rating 1.0. You Get What you Pay For! Yes, the price is low; however I would not recommend this to anyone for use with FSX. To be fair, I may have received a faulty unit but calibrating this thing was simply ridiculous...it never performed correctly and I mean NEVER. I would describe it as cheap, insensitive and wobbly.

rating 5.0. Best Bang for the Buck I started playing Elite Dangerous, and wanted the best experience possible. Keyboard and mouse wasn't great, Dualshock 3 & 4 both sucked, so I decided to get this. It's a great HOTAS at an affordable price. Works right out of the box, and is already mapped for Elite Dangerous. Can't wait to use it on my other flight and space sims.

rating 1.0. Not for Battlefield 3 very very hard to use, even harder to setup. The movement of the stick doesn't translate very well onto BF3 on PS3, can't say how it works for the pc, but I recommending avoiding this flight stick.

rating 5.0. This joystick is everything I was looking for. This joystick is very accurate in its control, and feel even though there is no force feedback. Works really well with the different models and types of aircraft in FSX. The weight of the product is immpressive and is not needed to be attached to anything to operate well. I would recommend this product to anyone with a passion to fly on a budget.

rating 4.0. Best for PS3 only If you're looking to flight sim in PC, don't get this. PC has much other stuff. Look into CH products for PC flight sims, they are the best hands down for PC.

But PS3 doesn't recognize CH stuff, so for PS3 this is the bomb.

Thrustmaster is a good company. I remember using their TOp Gun stick in the MiG Alley days in PC. Awesome stick lasted for 7 years.

If you're flight simming for PS3, this is the best. PS3 recognized it from the moment the usb went in. Remember the PS3 switch! Plug n play is big for consoles n pc's.

Construction is solid, good plastic, it survived a 3 foot drop (don't let excited dogs near your rig) and works like the day I pulled it from the box.

I use it for IL-2 PS3, so they had preprogrammed settings, but everything was fine for custom mapping also. I'm hoping flipping Ace Combat 7 will come out for PS3 as Ace Combat 6 betrayed the Playstation (series was only for PS until Gates threw a billion dollaz at Bandai!!!) That said

Its dead zones are great, sensitivity good. Movement is great, has a spring. Also stick returns to middle, which is a bonus. I think it should be mandatory but not all sticks for this price range don't.

Buttons are good. Not too hard to press and not so flimsy you feel it's going to fall out.

The hat is excellent and responds with a solid touch. The hat isn't as well made as a CH fighter stick's, as there one you can feel the direction the hat is being pressed and its more sensitive to touch; not so for the X.

Its not as heavy as older sticks (the tmaster top gun, you had to literally try to pick it up) so if you're stick jerker, it'll move around. But if you're a gently caress type virtual pilot, the weight is good enough.

Another plus is you can separate them. BIG additive for me as I like to have them apart for ergonomic reasons and create that "cockpit" feel. Not that I go all out and build an actual mockup of a plane like hardcore guys do.

Its reliable and if its anything like thruster master PC's offerings, it'll last years.

Cons. Nothing's perfect (Well except God, but I'm not qualified to review God). First off is the stick shape isn't generic enough to fit in my hand. The CH fighter stick I speak of is based of the F-16, which is sized for general public. Not the X, it has some deep curves in it.

Also there is a button on the top right corner that sometimes I touch, depending what its mapped to, can be inconsequential or disastrous (its bomb drop so WW2 plane, there goes your loadout).

The throttle feels funny in my hand. Its not as easy as the CH throttle (which has a potato [imagine potato with "eyes"] layout were all fingers can reach buttons with minimal movement). So some buttons my hand can get to easily, others I have do a "playing guitar stretch" to reach. Well its looks like something that belongs in a Klingon Warbird than a flight sim.

The cons is looks. I was never into the space fighter style sticks, primarily Saitek loves that look, but the X goes for space race. But most simmers are into 20/21st century jets or WW I - II prop era planes.

Another one is although you can separate it, you can't really move it that far apart due to the connecting cable being short.

All in all, good stick for PS3. I'd get it again if someone stole it or it broke just outside of warranty. Use a solid surface like a desk when using this. It doesn't stay on your lap.

rating 5.0. Elite Dangerous I switch from a Logitech Extreme 3D Pro and I can see the difference. for the same price you get a separated throttle slider and more buttons. It was more efficient in Elite Dangerous with all the extra buttons.

rating 5.0. Excellent value Works as advertised. Very sturdy. Good stick for the enthusiast.

May want to go higher end if you are "hard core", but will still serve you well

rating 2.0. Nope, move to something else. I was looking at upgrading from a Logitech to the Thrustmaster sticks due to the numerous positive reviews on their HOTAS setups.

I have used this for one day and its throttle does not register properly with any game that I have (ARMA, PS2, War Thunder, Battlefield 3, and a few space sims). After attempting multiple ways to reset the axis it would never register a negative value, only positive. After reassigning throttle values to other buttons I then had the problem of not being able to adjust the curves for ANY axis. Thrustmaster programs (Calibration and Feedback) did nothing more than show me which axis was being utilized.

This is the first product I will ever return that I got from Amazon.

Anyways here's my normal breakdown:

+ Stick is easy to grip and doesn't cause fatigue after long use.
+ Feels pretty well constructed for an entry level HOTAS.

-- Absolute pain to calibrate to get to work with the games I play
- Buttons on throttle are too close together and can cause cramping (but that may be due to the fact that my throttle is more of a place to set your hand than an actual throttle.

rating 5.0. Made Elite Dangerous much more fun to play This works perfectly. I looked at some of the more expensive HOTAS setups, but wasn't sure how much I would play the game so I went with a cheaper unit. Glad I did that, this does everything I want at a cheaper cost. The flight stick part could be a little heavier, but not a huge problem.

rating 4.0. Logitech vs Thrustmaster in Star Citizen: Arena Commander This review compares the "Thrustmaster T-Flight Hotas X Flight Stick" and the "Logitech Extreme 3D Pro Joystick" specifically in the context of Star Citizen: Arena Commander

At this point in the development of Star Citizen I don't recommend buying any kind of stick or HOTAS (hands on throttle and stick). Currently keyboard and mouse are more fully optimized. You can pilot and dogfight more precisely with a mouse than with a stick. They are however working to make sticks and full HOTAS setups a better option. So if at some point in the future you're trying to decide between the Thrustmaster and the Logitech here's my thoughts.

Comparing just the sticks, I prefer the Logitech. First thing that stands out is the base is heavier and wider. I never tipped the Logitech off of any of its feet when I was pulling back hard or anything like that. But I did with the Thrustmaster. Also there are more buttons for your thumb on the Logitech and they are in better positions and they are more satisfyingly clickly. The stick itself is a little smaller in diameter. Which I like. The Thrustmaster fit my hand fine, it was just a different feel. The Logitech takes more force to move the stick which feels good to me right now. However I could see in the middle of a long gaming session I might wish for an easier push.

On to the throttle controls. Obviously the Thrustmaster wins hands-down here. However I found that as I was dogfighting I didn't need the level of control that the Thrustmaster throttle provided. There is a command in the game that will match your speed to your target. Basically like radar enhanced cruise control on new cars. I find that when I target someone it's easiest to trigger that and I can stick to them easily. Beyond that I really only need full throttle and full stop, which I can get with voice command or a quick double tap of W or S. There are a lot of buttons on the throttle which is nice, but there's a lot of buttons on my keyboard too.

Basically if you're not going to get something with toggle switches and double thruster control like the "Mad Catz Saitek Pro Flight X-55 Rhino H.O.T.A.S." or the "Thrustmaster Hotas Warthog Joystick" (and there may never be a reason to) then save $15 and go with the Logitech you'll be happy.

rating 5.0. Great buy. This product is really great with the new Hawx game on PS3.  Definitely a great value and makes the aerial combat a lot of fun.  Easy to use, didn't take too long learn the key functions and very durable.  Only drawback was that the USB cord wasn't long enough and I had to buy an extension.  But overall, a great product.
rating 1.0. One Star did not work any games or system I have vista window 7

rating 4.0. Great HOTAS on a Budget This is a great Joystick if your on a budget and don't want to spend hundreds of dollars on the "next level" joy sticks. It performs well, the joy stick is sturdy and has a nice adjustment knob underneath on the bottom for spring tension.

It also comes with a small 'Allen' wrench (Hex Bar), that slots into the underneath of the joystick also, that's used for coupling and de-coupling the throttle via hex screws. The Throttle is decent and has a good feel to it. It does have a 'Stick' or snap feeling at center position that I kinda like, lets me know where center is without looking, but still allows for easy sliding.

There is 12 buttons total, this includes the two silver buttons you see. The black button on the throttle above SE and ST acts as a switch to set the joystick to a 4 axis mode or 5 axis mode. Some games do not support 5 axis so this is helpful.

The documentation that it comes with is decent, but, you will find more on their website along with drivers and such. The manufacture site looks like it was made Circa 1996, but, seems to be updated regularly as they have Windows 10 drivers via drop down menus under support.

I tested this under Windows 7 on Star Citizen. It does work, HOWEVER, and a VERY BIG However, you will need to manually map the controls as the default controls for it are not the best in my opinion. There is a link on RSI on how to do this in their forums.

rating 2.0. Mostly nice, but large deadzone was a dealbreaker I Got this stick several months ago. I ended up reselling it after a few weeks: the stick has an exceptionally large deadzone, and combined with stiff springs, this made any sort of precision movement extremely difficult. For me, and the games I was trying to play (Freespace 2, Falcon 4) this was a dealbreaker.

Other than the deadzone, the stick is quite nice: lots of buttons, good feel, easy to configure. It's hard to beat at the price, but I simply couldn't stand the huge deadzone.

rating 5.0. best thing i bought for playing EliteDangerous been using it ... best thing i bought for playing EliteDangerous been using it for a few months now and it still working good, a few numbers on the buttons wore off from to much use no doubt!  Pair this with Voice Attack program and youre good to go!

rating 5.0. Excellent Gaming Experience ! Almost like the real thing ! Adds excitement and realism to most flying games, particularly any game that involves fighter aircraft. All the switches and functions are programmable, and easy to set up and use.

rating 4.0. Solid flight stick, one or two issues It's a perfectly functional HOTAS system, and the price can't be beat. Of the twelve PC-mappable buttons, ten of them are in easy reach of the fingers: four on the joystick and six on the throttle (four under the thumb, two under the index finger). The rocker switch on the throttle is nice if you don't want to use twist for rudder.

I do have two quibbles, though: first, the throttle has a detent at about 50%. It's not a huge problem, but it is a bit annoying if you're trying to fly around half throttle--it'll fall into the detent, and it's tough to get it out without a 10%+ jump in throttle setting. Second, although the stick has adjustable resistance, I don't particularly like the curve: it's got very little resistance around neutral, and much higher resistance at the extremes.

Still very much worth the money, though.

rating 5.0. Absolutely unbeatable value I absolutely love this stick for the money and consistently recommend it to friends who want a competent flight controller without spending big bucks. While it doesn't measure up to the advanced HOTAS offerings out there as far as features, it absolutely nails the basics.

If you need a flight controller to be a comprehensive solution for hardcore sims this will not fulfill that need though it could likely at least perform passably well. For just about any other level of gaming however, it is a great, if not THE BEST choice out there.

I have an X-45 that I have used for a few years, and while that is a capable controller in, it is actually markedly inferior to T-Flight in terms of feel. The throttle detent is a little obnoxious, but the stick feels wonderful and is adjustable. Unless I specifically need the advanced features of the X-45, I always use the T-Flight because it feels so much better and is actually much better for "Pedal and stick" flying because of it. The X-45 is incredibly unpleasant to fly with.

Unless your application is something like DCS and really demands a plethora of buttons and macros, I can't emphasize how great this stick is for the money. For the massive number of users out there that want a solid solution for casual to intermediate flight sims and don't want to spend a lot of money, this will exceed your expectations and put only a minor dent in your wallet.
rating 4.0. Very nice unit This flight stick is exactly what I wanted as a companion to my Microsoft Flight Simulator X. Provides good feedback and it was a plug and play installation.

rating 5.0. its a great system for its price didnt know weather to put a 4 or 5 star, its a great system for its price, but would been nice if it had a hat on the throttle as well. but once i buy rudder peddles it will help do the job i wanted it for, needed the thrust forward/backward, thrust up/down and thrust left/right for minor adjustments on manual landing aproach For elite dangerous or any vtol (vertical take off and landing) sim aircraft or spacecraft.

rating 5.0. Awesome stick for the price Have had the stick for a few months and it is great for flying helos and jets in Arma 2 and Take On Helicopters.

The only minor complaint I have is that I would not recommend separating the throttle and joystick if you plan to fly aggressively. In this mode, the base of the joystick is not heavy enough, nor does it have enough footprint on the desk. If I'm flying aggressively, there have been a few times where I knock the joystick over, or the base starts to come off the desk. I've since gone to keeping the throttle/joystick attached - problem solved.

This a great joystick/throttle combo for the price. Especially to have a full-sized throttle and all of the buttons/sliders you have on much more expensive rigs, this is a great start for beginners and those that can't spend hundreds on "pro" setups
rating 5.0. Excellent product. You won't be dissappointed. I could not be more pleased with this product. It is very well made and everything I wanted. I highly recommend it.

rating 2.0. Creaky, sketchy input and wears out fast I have replaced this peripheral 3 times due to various issues such as creaking for the first one, the second one wouldn't stay centered, and the third one wouldn't detect certain side to side input. I finally got one that seems to work but at this point I don't really use it much because I was frustrated with the quality of the other units.
The other thing I noticed is this device is crazy old, the packaging looks like it's from the 80s and I have a feeling there is a warehouse full of these that have been there for 15 years. Go with a slightly more expensive one if you are really serious about playing flight sims, this just doesn't cut it after extended use.

rating 5.0. Flight stick for Elite: Dangerous on a budget I can't afford the top-end flightsticks and needed one for Elite: Dangerous. Haven't owned a joystick in more than a decade. After considerable research, the Thrustmaster T-Flight Hotas X Flight Stick came out clearly as the best candidate on a budget. Ordered and shipped to Singapore in about a week, really fast.

The Thrustmaster T-Flight Hotas X Flight Stick performs even better than I imagined. I was expecting it to feel flimsy at this price but it's solid, reasonable weight and accuracy is fair. Obviously it cannot compare to the top-end flight sticks, but for a first-time flight stick + throttle user, this performs solidly. There is a binding for the controls already shipped with Elite: Dangerous (gamma), and you can of course customise that. I find that there are a nice number of buttons on this stick without appearing too busy. You can also use "Shift"-bindings to increase the number of combinations. As an Asian, I'm supposed to have smaller hands - the Thrustmaster T-Flight Hotas X Flight Stick fits fine. Only the buttons 9 and 10 on the throttle's front seem a bit awkward, but perhaps it's just that I need more practice. All in all I am very pleased with this buy.

rating 3.0. this controller is otherwise very good, responsive The stick has a very large mechanical dead zone that cannot be adjusted. If you demand ultra-fine-tuned sensitivity from your peripherals, this device will not suit you. If you're okay with a sizeable dead zone on your joysticks, this controller is otherwise very good, responsive, and feels durable.

rating 5.0. Quatlity product My son says this is an incredible product, I'll have to take his word for it. He's had it for a few months and no problems yet. rating 5.0. Amazing for the money. I bought this flight stick when I acquired an interest in Elite Dangerous. Well, a year later, I'm still playing Elite and this is still my flight stick of choice. rating 4.0. But movements are amazing. It is REALLY hard to aim (be precise). But movements are amazing.

rating 5.0. Very recommended! This controller is fantastic.  Great price, solid build quality, and I love the "notch" in the middle of the thruster so  you can tell when you're at 50% without looking.  I use it for Elite and it's perfect for it!

rating 4.0. Awsome but touchy This flight stick is incredible. Everything works great. Only problem i have seen is that it is very touchy. Barely move the stick and you are all over the place. rating 5.0. Works great with my MacBook Pro I had to return other joysticks because they didn't work with my Mac, but a previous review mentions this one being used with a Mac, so I bought thinking if it doesn't work I can use it with my PS3, but it work great!

rating 5.0. Excellent budget HOTAS setup I have to be honest, I was a little leery of this product since it was considerably cheaper than any of the competition. But for $35 it has been EXCELLENT. My only complaint is on the throttle there is a detent at 50%. While in Flight Sim this may be useful to hold the throttle at 50%, a smooth transition would suit my needs much better, especially in X3.

rating 4.0. You won't find better HOTAS at this price range. I've bought this mostly for Arma 3, and I'm pretty satisfied with them.. No deadzone issue, just make sure to download their drivers. rating 1.0. Why I returned the Thrustmaster T-flight hotas X flight stick The one star is for the poor quality. One of the buttons stuck (the o button) the first time I pushed it. current_product_reviews: rating 5.0. Good Hotas for the price Works well.  So far addresses all my flight sims correctly.  Still learning the tweaks however!

input:
rating 5.0. Great product and sound for the price! PS4 user. Full disclosure: I am a verified purchaser and afer registering the product for their 12 month warranty, I was contacted by the seller who requested that I post a review in exchange for a free product. Having said that, they did not ask for a 5 star review, just for a review.

Overall, the set looks very solid although I have only used them for a couple weeks so I can't tell you how they will fare over time. I use them with my PS4 via a USB hub plugged into one of the two USB ports on the PS4. You do not have to have these plugged directly into the console. They work just fine with a aftermarket hub. Just be sure to adjust your audio settings accordingly.

The cord measures just over 7' which is nice but I would like for it to be a bit longer since I play on a 55" TV and need a bit of distance from the monitor. Luckily the addition of the cord coming from the USB hub allows for a greater distance. You could probably get away with using a USB extension as well if need be. The pads are soft but the headset does fit very snug on me. I do have a large noggin though.

The controls are simple with a volume adjustor and switch to mute the microphone. I have not yet tried any online play using the mic so I can't speak for the quality and performance of it.

The sound is great! Deep rich base with decent mids and highs. I mostly play racing games such as Dirt Rally and Gran Turismo with some Modern Warfare in between. These headphones sound great with any of these games. The noise canceling works surprisingly well considering the affordability of these headphones.

Overall, given the price, quality of the materials and great sound, I would recommend this for the average gamer. They're definitely a bargain not to be missed.

Hope this helps with making your decision. I will update this review if anything worth noting comes up.
rating 1.0. Weak? The volume is very low and the one I ordered and the replacement they sent.

rating 3.0. Loud click when muting mic I bought this to use it on work conference calls via Zoom, Webex, Teams, etc.... While it does appear to have good audio and noise cancellation, one of the main reasons I bought it was for the mute button built in to the cord. The problem is, every time I flip the switch everyone on the call hears a loud clicking noise. I don’t hear it on my end so I wasn’t even aware until someone complained about it while I was on a call.

I thought maybe I had the cord drawn tight and the vibration of me flipping the switch was coming through over the mic. That was not the issue. It is the switch itself. So while I can still use the headset it’s disappointing that this feature cannot be used.

input: rating 3.0. Great, yet horrible. Sounds great, fits comfortably, and the software is absolutely horrible. Driver problems? Oh, no that would never happen on Logitech equipment, right? Driver problems? Yes, they happen. A lot. Oh, don't unplug this thing, it might break and you will have to reinstall the drivers. Look, if you are stationary, you're not going to be unplugging it, and you're ready to find a fix to every problem you come across, then this headset is for you. Back to the good stuff, sound quality. It's great! The Dolby 7.1 surround sound is awesome, and when playing CS (why I bought this) I can hear virtually everything. The mic quality, decent. I use it to talk to friends and thats about all. They say it's fine and I haven't had any complaints about the quality of sound. Now, back to bad stuff. Don't use teamspeak and game at the same time. Or mumble, or Skype. It breaks the damned thing. Alt tab? Who cares! Break his headset! That's basically how it works. Again, just be prepared for driver issues and just issues in general. I have found a fix to all of my problems by lowering the quality of the sound in the Windows sound configuration thing. Occasionally though, I still have to reinstall drivers for no reason. I even had an issue once, where if I pressed caps lock it would mute sound from the headset until I reinstalled the drivers.

rating 2.0. Good Headset, Fatal Design Flaw The G430 has excellent audio quality, good drivers, and made an extremely positive impression for me at first. The real problem is that it suffers from a fatal design flaw that seems to plague the current generation of Logitech headsets, that being that the connection between the headphones and the headband is weak plastic and it's extremely prone to snapping off after only a few months of usage. To specify, this isn't rough, harsh usage that puts a lot of strain on the headset... even if you're very careful with your headset, it will likely break off on the left side where the cord is inevitably going to occasionally put strain on it. This is due to a very small and relatively weak plastic connector that joins the two.

If you're reading this and thinking "well, don't be so harsh on your headset," I had the same reaction upon reading reviews that complained about this. I'm fairly gentle with my peripherals, especially headsets, but these still lasted under a year before breaking. If you still want to buy them, I highly suggest avoiding placing any pressure on the headphones whenever possible. Preferably only handle them by the headband.

It's extremely annoying. The USB attachment doesn't work, you have to connect both the Microphone and Audio Jack separately if you don't want the microphone to play through the headset. Google it! rating 5.0. How it fix mic problems- Mostly everything about them so far is great.

You do have to do some research to fix the overlay of the mic and hearing yourself in the headset and auto adjust that makes your mic volume go wonky if you have any kind of back round sound.

To fix hearing yourself in the headset go to your play back devices - go to the properties on your head set then go to levels and turn the mic sound down to 0 problem fixed!
To fix the back round cancellation of the mic so you come out 'normal' on chat programs
in sounds go to 'recording' go to the properties or your mic and go to Advanced from there change the channel to 1 channel,16 bit, 48000 Hz (DvD Quality)

Hope this fixes these problems for anyone else who is having them

rating 2.0. I am a female and I take really really good care of my stuff First things first: I have had these headphones for about six months. They are now cracked from the sides. Let me assure you I am not an aggressive user nor do I have a large head. I am a female and I take really really good care of my stuff. I bought a hard carrying case to store these headphones in and put them back in that case every time I was done. These headphones have never left my desk or it's protective case. So the fact that they cracked after 1-2 hours of daily use made me pretty sad. These are marketed as gaming headphones... Gamers tend to wear headphones for hours at end so them cracking after a few months of use was highly disappointing. On the positive side, I called up Logitech and they sent me a new replacement after I showed them proof of purchase etc. So they were pretty helpful in that regard. There are a lot of people who have had this issue so I would think twice about buying these.

Another thing I did not like about these guys is that USB dongle it comes with... it is absolute rubbish. You can't truly enjoy the bass or the surround sound because the volume doesn't go high enough. Now, don't get me wrong I am not deaf nor do I like to listen to music/movies at super high volumes but the volume in these cans didn't go beyond the mid range. Which made is really difficult to understand the dialogue in video games/movies/tv shows etc. So, I ended up just using the regular line in audio cable for the most part... My husband got me these headphones for Christmas so that I could enjoy the bass/surround sound experience but it's truly not enjoyable if you can't increase the volume a little bit higher than the mid range which I think is quite important to gamers. After much research I found out that it may be the USB dongle that logitech makes that is the issue and not the actual headphones. I have not tested that theory.

The Good Part:
These guys are very comfortable. I would wear them for about 1-2 hours every other day and barely felt that there was something on my head. They were at times a bit big for my head ... the months that they were working great for me I liked them enough to purchase a second pair for the husband. The mic on them is quite sensitive and people can hear you even if you whisper. Great customer service by logitech. They are really easy to clean. Nice long wire and its very very durable. Comes with a 3 year Limited Warranty as long as you have proof of purchase.

Conclusion:
I would think twice about getting these if you are buying them for surround sound... Also, the fact that they cracked for an Audiophile like myself who takes good care of her stuff... it may crack for you. It seems like it is some kind of manufacturing defect... it may happen in a month or three years but it does eventually happen. I have headphones that are almost 10 years old and are still in pristine condition... so I know for a fact it was nothing that I did. Just an honest opinion from someone who has owned them for less than a year.

If i am on discord, ts, vent, etc every now and then the headphones will completely stop working and I will have to fully reset the audio settings for the program or I'll have to unplug and replug the headphones and then still perform a reset.  There are tons of headphones out there that are far superior to this.  If you only expect to ever have one thing on your computer producing sound ever these headphones are great for you, otherwise just look elsewhere.

rating 4.0. Great headphone quality, the USB 7.1 isn't worth it though Great, comfortable headphones. I've owned quite a few headsets over the years, my favorite still being the classic Icemat Siberias, but they suffered from fragile plastic and broke easily. These G430 headphones are very comfortable, not too heavy at all, and have nice sound quality for the price. The only difference between the G230 and G430 headphones are the color, and the USB dongle that emulates the 7.1.

In my opinion I disabled it immediately as using it for games such as Battlefield 4, and Counter Strike Source or GO, it was difficult to pinpoint exactly where the enemy was due to the emulation. I also have a pretty high end PC and I have the Sound blaster Z sound card. I took off the USB dongle and plugged them directly into my sound card and have it enabled as headphone mode, not 5.1 surround. Sounds much better to me in my opinion. I have the Logitech Z-5500 5.1 system, but I wanted a headset to use at times as well. The speakers are still much better for positioning an enemy, but the headset comes in handy if I have a friend over that wants to use it instead of their laptop speakers.

The microphone on this paired with my soundcard sound amazing. Crystal clear with no hum or buzz. Anyone experiencing issues with a slight hum; to fix it do this: Control Panel>Hardware and Sound>Sound>Recording Tab>Click the microphone for the mic and hit properties> Go to Listen Tab> Check the "listen to this device box", Click Okay> Go back into properties> Uncheck box and hit okay. This for some reason has removed the hum/buzz I experienced when first installing the headphones on three different PCs.

I say save the money and get the G230's and a sound card.

rating 4.0. Just be cautious when you buy this product I first thought this is going to be a memorable experience, like my old g35 back in the day until they broke and I moved to various other headsets, and it kind of felt short of that past experience.

Speaking of comfort..the cups on my head for some reason the bottom front part pushes into the soft part behind the corner of my jaw and ear and is slightly uncomfortable. The left ear cup is slightly heavier than the right cup due to the mic and wire placement on the left cup, which makes the left ear pad put more pressure around your ear which is comfy and snug but I had to constantly readjust the right ear cup every now and then. The swivel points on headset feel somewhat fragile and somewhat creaky. Cord is plenty long and includes velcro to hold any slack. The in-line controls box has a clip, which is nice.The ear pads can rotate 90 degrees, and removable ear cups for cleaning.

Speaking of sound...these are essentially just the G230 headset with a rebadge and an included USB to 3.5mm surround sound adapte, which enables it to emulate the 7.1 surround sound. When surround is enabled you'll hear up to 7 discreet channels of audio plus a low frequency effects channel, which you can individually change the volume output using Logitech Gaming Software. Surround sound's quality varies with music playback, but personally I had to turn it off for music and watching videos on youtube, or watching movies on VLC/windows media player. I played with the EQ settings with and without surround, and I came to a conclusion that in most cases surround was mediocre due to sometimes being wobbly, distorted, and sounded like being inside a cave. I have yet to try these out in games...BF4, CS:S/GO, and a few other non-fps games. Otherwise, the headset's stereo functionality was sharp ...speaking of technicalities I would say the bass response is very good and not too overbearing, but you can turn it up using the Logitech gaming software. The 40 mm speakers were neutral sounding, and fairly accurate to the source with a slight emphasis on the highs. The EQ feature provided by the software was very very useful, but the best feature the software had to offer was turning OFF the Dolby surround.

rating 5.0. Initial Thoughts (PS3) Obviously I haven't yet put in my 200+ hours to adequately review this game, but there are a few things I'd like to mention that I think potential buyers should know. Some of these comments may apply to all versions of this game, but I am particularly referring to the PS3 version of the game. With Oblivion, certain errors occurred on the PS3 but not on the 360, so these comments may not apply to all versions. I've also updated this review after playing quite a bit further into the game.

The graphics are nice. The mountain scenes present a high quality view slightly better than Oblivion, but during the progression of the game the graphics are about the same as Oblivion. One very nice exception to this are power attacks that slow time down and give a very stunning final blow to enemies by thrusting swords through them, slit throats, etc. Some very sensitive people may not like the brutality of these finishing blows, but I don't mind my toddler watching them. I did not want him watching the initial beheading scene, but with that exception and perhaps one torture scene, I don't mind him watching me play. The PS3 version only has 480p, 720p, and 1080i. Despite the fact we're nearly 6 years into 1080p graphics, Bethesda didn't feel the need to give it to us. However, the text is very small, so unless you have a 60" TV, I'm guessing you wouldn't be using more than 720p anyways. For my 32" Panasonic Plasma, I set the resolution to 480p and the text is still very small and difficult to read.

The game takes up a lot of RAM that causes skipping and short-freezing (no more than 5 seconds). If you played Oblivion when it first came out on the Xbox 360, you can compare the it to when you crossed invisible lines that caused it to freeze and say "loading" at the bottom for 2-5 seconds. Except in Skyrim, it doesn't say "loading," so it just freezes. Usually the freezing lasts less than a second, but sometimes it can be up to 5, making one concerned that the game is frozen. Further into the game this seems to happen less often.

The vast openness in Oblivion is enhanced in Skyrim. With Oblivion, when you went into a city it'd stop and load. With Skyrim, you can walk in and out of cities without having to pause to load. You do still have to pause to load if you walk in a building or a dungeon or certain cities. About 1/4 of the world is completely barren in terms of interaction, but it is still breathtaking even up to the North, West, and East "You can't go beyond this point" messages.

The fear of crashes on the PS3 version of Oblivion may be extended to Skyrim. Before you leave the initial dungeon, there is a bridge that collapses, not allowing you to go backwards. If you jump down into the ravine created by the lack of a bridge and try to jump back up to the other side of the ravine, you'll get stuck between a rock and a hard place--literally. So frequent savings are advised, especially before doing something you think might cause it to freeze. About 40 hours into the game, I've had this occur about 4 times total that I had to reload from a previous save because I was stuck and could not get out.

The leveling system is nice. Similar to Final Fantasy XIII's Christiarium, Skyrim has astrological symbols for each skill. Each time you level up your character, you can use one "Perk" to unlock a "star" in the symbol. Each star requires a minimum level in that skill, and each star either increases or decreases the effectiveness of something or grants you a special skill. The increasing/decreasing effectiveness usually has 5 different levels for how effective it is, but you only have to unlock the first level to move on to the next star. Choose your perks wisely, as you only get one per character level. Personally I chose to max out Sneak early on which has some nice combinations with Archery and One-Handed weapons that allow for instant-kills of moderate-difficulty targets.

Unlike Oblivion where each race had specific skills that would be beneficial, Skyrim's races only start with a higher set of stats. You can level any set of skills to the max. I'd recommend picking a race either on their 24-hour skill, their special abilities (i.e. water breathing), or their appearance. No race has a speed advantage in Skyrim. The character creation has greatly been enhanced so you can make one race look like many others. Obviously you can't turn an Orc into a Kajiit, but you can make a Wood Elf look like pretty much any other elf as well as most "human" and elf races (except Orc).

Regarding leveling, each time you level up your character (not skill) you can choose to increase your Magikka, Health, or Stamina by 10 points. You also get 1 "Perk point' as mentioned above to unlock new abilities in a particular skill set. You level your character by leveling your skills. I don't believe there's a particular ratio, and it seems that the higher a particular skill goes the less effect it has on your overall level.

If you preordered the game (or if you buy it from someone who preordered it), you get a premium physical map inside the case. When they say premium, they mean it, because it seems to be made of parchment (leather), not paper. It could just be a trick, but it is really nice. However, unless you've got a game room or are playing it in your bedroom, there really isn't anything to do with it. It's too big to get out and put back, and the map is not very topographical, so you don't know if you can trespass between two landmarks or not. If you're the average adult and have your TV in your living room, it's not something you'd want to hang on your wall unless you already have some type of Medieval theme to your decor.

The difficulty level is not as noticeable in Skyrim as Oblivion. In Oblivion I put it towards the easy end and went up and down for specific enemies. In Skyrim I've had it dead in the centre the whole time. I had some difficulty with one dungeon and put it all the way to easy and didn't notice any difference. I eventually just used my 24-hour ability (Wood Elf) to control the giant spider while I walked past it. I have had several other bosses that I was unable to beat on any difficulty that I eventually just changed strategies and beat on a moderate level.

Lastly, if you're one to look for cheats and loopholes, the "Sneak Cheat" from Oblivion is still applicable in Skyrim, but it's more difficult. If you want details, search on a site for FAQs, as I won't include it here for the many people who don't like handicapped advantages.
