rating 5.0. So How Does Skyrim Run Now? This review is designed to provide information on how Skyrim runs now. I love Skyrim, but at this point I think it will be more valuable to talk about how the PS3 version worked for me, since I can't really say what hasn't already been said about the quality of the world, environment, leveling, and so on.

I don't know if anyone is still trying to figure what (if anything) made Skyrim run horribly for some players on some PS3s, but just in case, here are some tidbits:
-Original (fat) PS3
-750gb SATA HDD
-Autosave turned off (Early on when people were talking about all of the problems, some speculated about autosave being a problem. I have no idea if it really matters, but I decided not to risk it)
-I tried to keep my quest list short (Again I'm not sure if this really matters. But as a precaution, I would eliminate short, random, radiant, and non-guild quests--or quests that didn't lead into another quest--before playing others)
-Started playing around 2/12/2012 (I'm not sure which patch was just released, but I think it was 1.4)
-Total playtime: 113 hours (and on going. I only finished the Thieves Guild, believe it or not)
-Save file size: 9.64 mb

So with all of that out of the way, how does Skyrim run now, and more broadly, how did Skyrim run for me? The game played well. It didn't play horribly. It didn't play perfectly. Technical issues do exist, but they are usually best described as infrequent or insignificant. I have provided some detailed descriptions below, but if you don't want to read all three sections, I'll summarize my thoughts here.

All big Bethesda RPGs of this generation have problems. With Oblivion it was load times or how some things slowed down. With Fallout 3, the game eventually becomes filled with more and more weird bugs popping up. With Skyrim, the game runs a lot better than Oblivion ever did, especially when you consider how Oblivion had to load constantly while you were roaming the world while Skyrim doesn't, yet it is superior in every technical aspect. It also appears more stable, long term, than Fallout 3. Even though the PS3 version of Skyrim is infamous for poor performance issues, the game runs considerably better than Oblivion and Fallout 3, even though some issues do happen every once in a while.

DID MY GAME FREEZE?

Sometimes. I don't have an exact number, but it's safe to say it didn't freeze every day. Let's just say less than ten times. (10 out of 113 hours isn't bad, right?) The times it did freeze seemed random to me. I remember one time it froze when I was performing a shout, but I can't (and frankly don't want to) say my game froze because I did X. So, through my experience, the game doesn't seem to freeze because I'm in a huge area, or fighting, or opening doors. It is infrequent and random. I'm not sure if this is an issue with other players, but I should go ahead and mention I never had a corrupted save file or any issues with my actual system after I started playing the game.

DID MY GAME LAG?

No. At least not like how other people are reporting it. Personally, I thought Oblivion had more issues, especially during scenes with a lot of people on screen and roaming the world while having to load every 20 or so seconds. In Skyrim, the frame rate will drop every now and then, but it doesn't last long. Most of the time it seems to happen when I run through a town with a lot of buildings, people, and other things like running water *looks at Whiterun*. Other people have experienced significant lag, that makes the game unplayable or at the very least no longer fun to play. It never reached that level for me, and the lag and frame rate issues are hardly present in smaller areas.

DID MY GAME HAVE A LOT OF GLITCHES?

I had some, but fortunately they weren't that bad. I saw one guy, at one point, with an invisible head. One unimportant side quest, out of the 150-200, couldn't be completed because the quest giver couldn't take my items. And yeah, my body flew spectacularly high in the air when I was killed by a giant. (This event seems like it's part of Skyrim's orientation) That isn't the extent of my glitches, but those were the bigger ones, and those three examples are non-existent when you look at my entire experience with Skyrim. In terms of bigger issues--like, but certainly not limited to, flying horses, dragons flying backwards, not being not being able to finish a quest that actually matters--I didn't have any of those. The three examples I gave happened around 5, 20, and 90 hours into the game. From my experience, it doesn't seem like the game has more or less glitches the longer you play it. Just like with the game crashing, it simply does or does not. With Oblivion, I had some glitches. With Fallout 3, I had more glitches, especially after playing through two DLC expansions and having a file that was over 50 hours. While those Bethesda RPGs got worse as time progressed, my Skyrim file, which is longer than my longest files in Oblivion and Fallout 3, seems to be just as stable as it was when it started.

rating 5.0. Most impresive and visually stunning game I've ever enjoyed. I've bought this game twice now. I can see it being one of three games that will never leave my library. I cannot convey the scale of how big this game truly is. I've loved open world games since Grand theft Auto 3, and Skrim has nearly perfected the game genre. Though there are no guns, there are swords, axes, maces, knives, staves, and magic. The leveling system is intuitive and limited in that you cannot unlock every thing within one playthrough. Which being the kind of gmae this is, is a nice way to appreciate a second playthrough even more. Though I'm at 160 hours of playing and I'm only at level 45 out of the max of 80. One takes place in a madman's dream, several take place in an underground mushroom kindgom with giant glowing mushrooms (google search blackreach). I highly recommend this title to all gamers.

rating 5.0. A wonderful addition to the TES series [UPDATED] I've been a fan of The Elder Scrolls since Morrowind, and I was quite excited for this release. I must admit, that between the issues I had with finally getting Oblivion to run, and what I heard about Fallout: New Vegas (I fortunately didn't have time to play that one right away,) I was nervous that Bethesda would release yet another title with game-breaking bugs. Well, I haven't had any problems thus far (several hours of play so far.) What I have noticed is how much they improved the system! It looks to me like they borrowed heavily from the recent Fallout titles. My review will be focusing on the mechanical aspects of the game:

*Skills and leveling seem less obtuse. They got rid of the stats, so you have more freedom to decide which skills you level. You no longer have to worry about the age-old TES question "Should I tag that skill? Or will it cripple my character?" They also removed the "you must rest in order to level up" requirement, which is a bit more convenient.

*The crafting system seems to borrow heavily from Fallout: New Vegas (and games like World of Warcraft.) You no longer need to carry repair hammers around. Instead, you find raw materials and instead of maintaining your equipment, you can improve it. Over all, this makes the skills feel more useful, and at the same time, it seems that it reduces the tedium (stopping in the middle of a dungeon to repair my damn boots in Morrowind/Oblivion was always annoying!)

*Questing has improved! The quest log is far easier to follow than it has been in previous titles.

*Combat hasn't changed too much, though they added the ability to equip a weapon in your offhand. Getting the rhythm down to fight this way takes a little time, but it's a welcome change!

*The voice-acting is immensely better than it was in Oblivion! The towns feel more fleshed out this time around.

*Of course the graphics have improved. TES series seems to have always been on par or ahead of the rest when it comes to visuals. And consistently with the previous titles, the music is still great!

*The men in this game are so manly that they don't wear coats! EVEN WHEN STANDING AROUND WAITING TO MUG YOU ON A SNOWY MOUNTAIN!

A word of advice: Leave the giants alone!

Update (1/7/12)

Still adding on hours to the game, and I haven't ran into anything game-breaking. Every now and then the game will threaten to lock up, but I don't think I had to ever reset my console. Also, I haven't had too many problems with quests, which seems to be a common complaint from other users. Again, I've played other Bethesda titles, and I think dodging some of the big glitches is the luck-of-the-draw; I've had attempts at Oblivion and Morrowind that stalled within the first few hours, and I've also had complete play-throughs with very few hang-ups.

Update (4/3/12)

Still playing this game from time to time! And I still haven't run into many serous glitches. A few graphical errors, and that's about it. If I play for a long time, the game seems to get a little fussy (and it may be because of heat issues with the PS3 itself.) There are still many areas to explore, and tons of quests to complete, so I haven't tested everything yet (even after all this time!) Skyrim still has my highest recommendation! In fact, with respect to my experiences, it's probably the most stable game Bethesda has released to date, and every bit as fun as the previous titles!

rating 5.0. A Totally Immersive Experience This game is so much fun that I bought copies for both the PC (via Steam) and the PS3. The graphics are clearly best on a PC with a powerful graphics card, but are quite beautiful on the PS3 as well. The bottom line, though, is that the many complex but logical role-playing elements, coupled with the unbelievably enormous variety of places to go and things to do, all serve to make this a strong candidate for "game of the year."

Some Bethesda-haters don't want to hear this, but I have had only one glitch on the PS3 after many hour of play (I am now Level 22). The single glitch occured when I got "stuck" between a couple boxes and couldn't get out. For some reason, I couldn't exit the game or the PS3 system by the usual method, either, and had to turn off the power switch! Nevertheless, I was able to reload my last save after a period of integrity-checking by the system. Since the installation of a recent patch, I have had no such issues. Considering the enormity of this software creation, a single glitch after so many hours of play is completely acceptable. I can't explain why other user are plagued with frequent problems. Perhaps an older machine? Or a bad power supply? Whatever, they might consider completely removing the game, then reinstalling it (a pain, I know).

I love the whole "sandbox" concept of accomplishing all your main and secondary goals in the order YOU want to do them. Many times, I'll be heading for a specific location, but another pathway will cut across the trail, heading up into the hills, and I'll just have to find out "what's up there."

While the graphics for ranged combat (e.g., archery and magic) are quite good, character interaction for close-quarter fighting could look a bit more convincing when you swing your sword, or axe, or whatever. Nevertheless, the fights are challenging, and your opponents get increasingly tougher as you level up, so things never get too easy. Sometimes your follower gets in your line of fire, or blocks a doorway you're trying to go through. At other times, however, this computer-controlled ally is a real life-saver in a tough fight (though it's about time I parted ways with Lydia and signed on a follower who is capable at a higher lever, and is also "marriagable").

I just wish that, when I decide to sleep in an inn, my partner is not unrealistically standing there, hovering over me, when I get up--that's just creepy. And a little more randomized banter would help cement the illusion that my sidekick is a real character I should care about. Other games do this quite well. For example, look at the Mass Effect series for great team interaction.

I really wish the inventory system could be improved, especially as regards easily moving items between party members, storage, and a shopkeeper. If Lydia (your first assigned party member) says "I'm sworn to bear your burdens" one more time, I'll . . . just have to put up with it, I guess. For example, when trading items at a store, you have to sell what you have in your inventory, close the dialogue with the shopkeeper, than take items you want to sell from your follower, then reopen a dialogue with the shopkeeper--very clumsy and time-consuming. You have to go through the same rigamarole when storing items in a trunk, too. Because the game is so good, these relatively small annoyances are forgivable. Still, if Bethesda reads this recommendation: could item exchange possibly be improved in a future patch? And could you give my main gal, Lydia, a little personality?

rating 5.0. Another way-too-late review: Skyrim 1.9 Skyrim was released in late 2011, but I didn't get around to it until the spring of this year due to a back up of games and all the bugs it suffered from early on. In fact, let's start with that. For a game of this size, and with all the bad press I'd been hearing (and maybe because of that), I felt it was actually very bug free. I kept a freeze counter; the first one occurred 20-25 hours in, but considering I played for almost 300 hours and experienced about a dozen more, it was very rarely a problem. As for glitched/unfinishable quests, I encountered just six (though none prevented me from platinuming the game), one in a primary quest, and five in miscellaneous objectives. FYI, I played the entire game after installing patch 1.9, which (reportedly) got rid of a lot of bugs, and added Legendary difficulty and Legendary skills - two options I could barely imagine playing the game without.

PROS: No game features more freedom; to play the quests in the order you want, to build your character the way you want, and to explore the world how you want.

The combat is way better than Skyrim's predecessor, Oblivion.

It features an amazing world/map. My favorite part of the experience was exploring.*

There are so many ways to build and equip a character. Do you want to build a warrior-mage? Or a nearly invisible thief who kills with stealth? Or some combination of all three? There exist seven weapons (four one handed, three two handed, all of varying levels), a multitude of shields, scores of magical spells, dozens of staffs, and even (if you're so inclined) your fists. And, of course, a vast assortment of armor and clothing to adorn your character.

No other game has done dragons as well. They look much like your traditional dragons, but are well rendered, awe inspiring, dynamic and deadly. Your first few encounters with them will be long remembered.

Loads of content/quests. Skyrim easily took the cake for the longest I've ever played one game: 285 hours. (Did loading screens count though?) It took about 180 hours to visit all nine cities and about 250 to complete the main quest.

Huge diversity of indoor areas. No two caves, lairs, vaults, crypts, dungeons or keeps were identical (or barely even alike). Everywhere you went you were literally seeing it for the first time. Remember Dragon Age 2? I didn't think it was so bad at the time, but after Skyrim, Bioware should be embarrassed with the (lack of) effort put into the map variety in that game.

Grand, beautiful music that (usually) knew when to swell and when to shut up.

J'zargo!

A perfectly usable third person perspective, something sorely lacking from Oblivion.

Surprisingly unbuggy (relative to the reviews I'd read). For comparison, I played Fallout 3: GOTY edition (another Bethesda game) and found it unplayable after about the 75 hour mark (possibly due to the 15 mb save file). Skyrim was still chugging along well even at the end (with rare lagginess). Though thinking back, the key was probably that I fast traveled everywhere, spending as little time 'outdoors' as possible. And again, that I played entirely on version 1.9. You just have to know that with Bethesda, don't start playing until several months after the release date.

CONS: The combat still doesn't compare well to RPGs like Dragon Age, Demons/Dark Souls or KOA: Reckoning. It still felt too much like swinging chopsticks in front of your face.

Skyrim was probably a little too long. Instead of making the game longer/bigger, it would've benefited from using all that time and energy on refining it (even if it meant losing 100+ hours of gameplay). Get rid of the bugs, refine the combat, feature more enemy variety, shrink the world, and I'd be very happy with a 150 hour game.

Even after patch 1.9, it was still buggier than the aforementioned RPGs (e.g. minor quest bugs, freezing, lagginess, occasional very long load times, bodies falling through floors, weapons disappearing from hands, etc.).

Loading screens were too frequent and too long. If loading screens count toward play time, then I would've been under 250 hours for 'actual playing time.' This also goes back to the game being too long: Make the game world smaller, but reduce the frequency and times of loading screens.

It was generally too easy. (I had to change the way I played to make it more challenging.) My changes in difficulty went as such: Adept 0-20 hours, Expert 20-70, Master 70-120, and Legendary the rest of the way.

The skill/character building got a bit tedious after about the 100 hour mark. Like KOA: Reckoning, respeccing would have helped keep it fresh. (I don't care that it was in the DLC.) Patch 1.9's Legendary skills (that allowed you to reset a skill and regain all the perks) helped, but not completely.

It needed more 'wow' spells and abilities; things that made you say, 'that's fu--ing awesome!' Think of the meteor spell in KOA, or 'firestorm' in Dragon Age. Skyrim had 'mass paralysis' and the Dawnbreaker sword, but could've (and should've) had more.

A small quip, but I wanted to (and thought I would) fight two dragons at once. Sadly, that never happened. The closest was when I called in my own dragon to fight another dragon, or when...
-------------------------------------------------
* While exploring, I decided to find out what was on the other side of a mountain. I made my way up past a giant statue of Talos, down a steep hill, fought two trolls, then woke an ancient dragon (that breathed fire and ice) and a draugr overlord. It took all my skills (e.g. Sanguine Rose, recharging weapons with soul gems, using potions and shouts, changing apparel) to survive that encounter. I also recall my follower, Lydia, using the Dawnbreaker sword to cause a chain reaction of explosions among a group of skeletons. It was an incredible adventure completely unrelated to any quest.

Before that, I happened upon a lighthouse and discovered a murdered family inside. That motivated an investigation that brought me into a deep underground maze populated by giant insects, and eventually to the queen herself.

Conclusion: If you like RPGs even slightly, buy this game. You can get the vanilla version, which features enough content to keep you busy for months, for about $20. Do it.

rating 5.0. OK, so the wife and I are fighting ... We purchased this game with two Amazon gift certificates we received for Christmas from my sister, and it has turned out to be the hit gift of the season. We're fighting over it, edging each other out for game time, competing to see who levels up quickest, discovers the most sites, earns the most trophies, etc etc. We both love it. The great thing about Bethesda and Skyrim in particular is that the game is suited for our totally different playstyles. I love to do missions, and there are a BAZILLION in this game. I tromp right through them and love playing out the many intersecting storylines. My wife plays like she's a real denizen of Tamriel, wandering around the map and reacting to whatever she encounters, missions be damned. The great achievement here is that Skyrim is perfectly satisfying to BOTH of us. Great graphics, improved inventory/skill management and levelling since "Oblivion" and a plethora of great mysteries, stories and characters to uncover. Some crash problems, but I hope that will be fixed with future patches. A near-perfect action RPG.

rating 5.0. Great game, well-priced Anyone who enjoys the other games in the Elder Scrolls series will enjoy this one too. Very open-ended and almost endless radial quests, Skyrim offers great replayability and hundreds (yes, hundreds) of hours of play. Highly recommended.

rating 1.0. Awesome... until the frame stutter kicked in... The graphics are outstanding - they actually evoked my emotions and feelings (like vertigo) in ways no other game has. The dragons are awesome (if a bit simple-minded). The dungeon designs are much less predictable and more exciting and actually provide some puzzle power in places - a very good thing. The combat is more challenging, less predictable, more realistic and thoughtful - it works well.
This "WAS" a 9.5 out of 10 game for the first 100 hours I played it, on my PS3 system. Now the stuttering and freezes have forced me to look for answers online... and I am disappointed. There are many others with similar issues. Since I am a clean player, my file size was just 4.8 Mb, so the problems are only going to get worse... and they are already truly annoying. I have deleted house purchases (kept only WhiteRun) and many saves. I have deleted the last several days of play. All of this so I might reduce the Skyrim demands on my PS3 to a level where I "might" be able to finish the game.
This truly sucks. How can I lose myself into a game where I have to constantly be careful to avoid gaming activities that will cause problems for my system? I can't explore freely or visit every local because the Bethesda developers did not engineer this game for the platform. It is inexcusable. I don't want a game that requires some massive patch, delivered by some interminable download, at the conveniece of some developer, who has already taken advantage of my trust. Patches are often problems in themselves, and I hate being dependent on being an online membership to reload my system in the future.
Like others here I feel ripped off. There is no way Bethesda didn't know this was a problem before distributing it. I also believe they chose to grab as much money as they could, and damn the consumers. I wish there was some way to get my money back, since I don't want to own products that won't work out of the box, anymore.
Finally, the "physics" engine, for handling items totally SUCKS!! The center of gravity effect is gone so it is much harder to handle objects. You have to grab objects, then rub them against other things to get them to turn in the direction you want, and then the object just slips back to the position it started from. This is very aggravating if you like personalizing your house. Another problem is that shelves and tables in the house "reject" items you place on them. I spent a half an hour putting out a few treasures I collected on my trips - I left the house - and when I came back half the stuff was laying around on the floor. I experimented for awhile and finally gave up on having a "nice" place, because the game basically rejects how I want to arrange my swag around the house. One example is that the shelves in the entry of the Whiterun house will hold one or two objects each, otherwise it will spit most everything off when you leave. The shelves and tables are "cluttered with junk" when you buy them - so how did Bethesda cheat the system to be filled up initially, when it won't hold anything for you?
Furthermore, the houses you can buy seem to be cobbled together by someone with no sense of practicality, or interior design skill. The alchemy and enchanting stations generally lack cupbords to store things in, forcing you to drop and arrange them, or walk many steps over to an inconvenient barrel or nightstand or "sack", etc... Dedicated areas with "3 or 4" well placed cabinets (and get rid of the sacks) would have worked muck better.
It would have been nice if the floor plans had included an area that worked like an armory, instead of just dropping in armor stands, weapon racks, and wall displays all about the houses.
I don't think Bethesda realizes that owning a house, that can be easily decorated to suit the player, is a very powerful mini-game, in and of itself, to transport many players into the world. When you make house ownership less personal and enjoyable, you break an important part of the illusion for many players.
I find it very sad that I can't get immersed in Skyrim the way I did in Oblivion or Fallout 3. There is just to much clutter, to many bugs, and to little personalization to let me become a part of the game.
For me what started out as a great gaming experience has been trashed by Bethesda's lack of developement, design, testing and forethought. They have produced a magnificent disaster for PS3 owners. I hope it is better for PC and X Box owners, since Skyrim has so much potential, it would be sad if it was totally spoiled.

This is my final edit/comment :::

Amazon gets a 10/10 for its customer service and Bethesda gets a "zero". I am excited because Amazon is providing a full refund for my purchase. I will have to eat the cost of the excellent Prima Games guide because they did nothing wrong and there are obvious signs I used the guide (I pre-ordered it with Skyrim). As for playing Skyrim: I have not seen the old Bladesman in Riftin, I never found the Staff of Magnus, my enchanting level is only 68 so I never got to create a double enchantment, I don't know why or how the dragons came back --- and I doubt I'll ever have the experience. I guess I'll just have to read the book, which I just happen to own... (drum roll & cymbal sound)

Im a 55 year old Bethesda fanboy who just doesn't trust Bethesda anymore. This is such a major issue that I don't believe Bethesda will fix it with their promised patch. They should not have released the product in this condition. I find I agree with others who say its time to stop being a "tool", and express my disappointment by refusing to own such a product. I will never pre-order another Bethesda product. I will never again be first in line for anything they make. To be clear for you at Bethesda - I DO NOT WANT A PATCH!! I WANT A GAME THAT IS TESTED AND DEVELOPED SO THAT IT WORKS "OUT OF THE BOX".

If you don't want to develop quality PS3 products, just be honest and honorable enough to admit it upfront.

If you DO want to, then how about investing another couple of hundred thousand to prove them - it is certainly less expensive than tens of thousands of returns, and the loss of customer trust.

It would be nice if Bethesda could rerelease a functional Skyrim PS3 product, so that I could try it again. But sadly such a rerelease is generally limited to GOTY - and I don't see how Skyrim can qualify for such an honor, under the circumstances.

rating 3.0. Great game - Badly bugged The game is awesome! The landscape is beautiful, and it is a marked improvement over Oblivion (the previous Elder Scrolls title on the PS3). There is so much to see and do, that you could play this game for a very, very long time.

The changes in character action are awesome - assigning a weapon or spell to each hand is great! There are new animations that increase the flavor of combat, which is a great. However, if you have young ones, or an aversion to graphic violence, you may steer away from this game, as there is no way to turn it off as a setting. For example, there is a decapitation in the first 30 minutes of the game.

However, the longer you play this game, the more flaws become apparent. The game will stutter and lag. You will eventually experience your system locking up, requiring you to do a hard reset of the console.

The software designers have a horrible history of support for their PS3 titles. I highly recommend buying this on PC instead.

If you cannot buy it on PC, I recommend waiting until it hits the bargain bin.

rating 1.0. Its success is also its downfall. Imagine hundreds of hours of playtime and every single object you interacted with throughout that time was in the same place/position you left it when you interacted with it. Well over time the save file becomes bloated allowing glitches, slowness and errors to creep in until you cannot play the game further.

Yes it's cool to fill a house full of plates, armor, weapons, books and all the various other trinkets you can find. What is not cool is that all of your time leveling and collecting leads to the only ending that is inevitable; game breaking slowness/glitches.

So if you want a medieval grand theft auto that will eventually lead to you having to start over or lose everything then this is for you. If you want to log in and see every object you ever interacted with in the place you left it at the expense of it eventually breaking your game play then this is for you.

Also dragons kill quest people, traders/blacksmiths and a main game quest was an npc who was going to sneak my gear into a certain building where a party was being held but when I got to him inside the building he only had my necklace and chest armor out of a whole suit(this was early in the game). I killed him and realized it is healthier if I don't play this game ever again. This game is over rated. If you are the millions who say the other then more power to you. I just hope this is not the new way of developing video games. If so then pretty soon games will be like a blue screen of death.

rating 5.0. Much better than the Steelseries Siberia V2 What an AWESOME headset.

Firstly, the cons:
The headset is a bit bulky.
The mic sound quality is pretty good, but sometimes can let in the sound of breathing.
They aren't very comfortable around the neck

Now for the positives:
Sound quality, in my opinion, is incredible. For audiophiles, it might be a bit lacking, and it definitely is not a reference headset, with bass and treble amplified a bit. However, I think they sound just as good as the Audiotechnica ATH-50s, and definitely beat out the Siberia.
These are also VERY comfortable. The real leather ear-cups, with the included velour spares, allow me to wear these constantly without any problems, even with my glasses. They isolate noise very well with their nice oval-shaped ear-cups and are able to accommodate my large head and large ears.
The accessories it comes with is almost excessive. with a 4-pin adapter for your smartphone, an airplane adapter and TWO extensions, they have pretty much included at least $10 worth of accessories along with the headset itself.
Audio quality seems fine on the mic. None of my friends have complained about it, and it seems very similar in performance to my roommate's Siberia. Much better than the built-in mic on my webcam.

After doing much research on these, as well as having owned them for a couple of months, I think this is the best headset (might be one of the best headphones as well) short of buying audiophile headphones and slapping a modmic on them.

UPDATE 11/23/2014: I have since removed the wind screen on the microphone, since it seems to actually create more noise than anything. I would definitely recommend this for anyone else with microphone troubles.

rating 5.0. HyperX Cloud is one of the best 3.5mm headsets available at this price point - Fantastic! I have been a hard-core multi-player gamer for at least 20 years. In that time I have used at least a dozen different headsets ranging in price from $30 up to $150, and at least as many different brands. My current headset had broken a while back and as I was taking a break from gaming due to a new demanding job and girlfriend, I didn't bother to replace it. Recently I got back into gaming and decided I needed to find one.

My first choice was the Razor Kraken PRO USB, which I bought from Amazon for $80. The non-PRO version costs around $50, and I think the only difference is in the 7.1 Dolby surround sound. I chose this model because I have used Razor keyboards and mice for years, and they have always been the absolute best gaming peripherals in those areas. I figured their reputation would carry over to gaming headsets. Upon receiving the Razor Kraken PRO and putting it on, my first impression was that it was extremely heavy and very uncomfortable. It sounded great, but there was absolutely no way I could wear it for any longer than 20 minutes without my head feeling like it would explode. I decided to I would return this item to Amazon, but I wanted to order a new headset first and compare it to the Razor before making the final decision to send it back.

So I began my search for a new headset when I stumbled upon the Kingston HyperX Cloud Gaming Headset. While it's not USB (3.5mm jack) and it's not 7.1 dolby surround either, for the same $80 price it is a far superior headset not only to the Razor Kraken, but to any headset I've ever owned at THIS price range ($75-100). I've known for decades that Kingston makes very good quality memory, and more recently, great SSD drives. I had never thought of them as a company to make a headset, or any gaming peripherals for that matter. Upon further review I came to find out that this headset is based off a Qpad design, for which I believe Kingston has licensed the technology. Qpad is a well known gaming company that makes award winning headsets. So it's really just a "Kingston" branded version of one of their designs, and actually has some improvements over the Qpad model to boot.

Upon receiving the HyperX Cloud in just a couple days thanks to Amazon Prime, I began my comparison. The first thing I noticed was the boxing was impressive, and the kit contained a ton of fantastic and useful components that could be used for all types of setups and applications. The HyperX will work not only with PC, but also with iPhone/iPad, all Android phones/tablets, Playstation and XBox. If you fly on airplanes sometimes, as I do for business, the handy traveling bag that comes with it and the ability to use with your phone/tablet will be most welcome. The noise cancellation on these is just enough that it would block out jet engine and background chatter, but not to the extent that you can't hear yourself speak or hear typing on keys. That is a good thing. Perfect balance on these in my opinion. Also it comes with an extension cord that of 5 ft that you can attach to the 3 ft cord, allowing you to easily get them plugged into a 3.5mm jack on the back of your desktop computer and reach all the way to your head. I have way more than enough cord in-fact for this use. The best part is if you take these on an airplane for use on your smartphone or tablet, the base cord is just 3ft so you don't have this monster long cord all over the place. Smart move Kingston!

The next thing I noticed was that these felt lighter than the Razor Kraken headset, even though the built quality on these seemed much better. You would think with more robust materials and build quality that it would be heavier. Alas, I took at look at the specs on both to see if maybe my sense of weight was off, but sure enough the Kingston HyperX Cloud is indeed about an ounce less than the Razor model. Razor is 10.35 ounces, and HyperX about 9.5 ounces. Doesn't seem like much but it is noticeable in the hand, and on the head. Regardless the backbone of the HyperX is a very solid aluminum build and it felt like it had a lot less chance of breaking or cracking than the Razor model.

Now let's talk about comfort. For most gamer's you are going to be spending periods of time with these things on your head for hours. So I'd say comfort is equally if not more important than sound quality or mic quality. The HyperX Cloud's are extremely comfortable. If you have an extremely large head they may not fit you, as they length they expand is a little bit more limited than on some other headsets. If you have an average size head, they are terrific. The band at the top has just enough soft cushion that they won't dig into the top of your head, but not so much that it gets in the way. The band itself is very flexible and the ear cups swivel for different angles and head shapes. For that reason they do not press hard against your head causing ear pain, jaw pain or headaches like other headsets can. They press just enough to block out loud background noises. The ear cups are oval shaped and large enough to cover even the biggest elephant ears inside the cups. They are around ear not over-ear. Also in the kit you get an optional pair of super soft velour covered earpads in addition to the leather covered ones. Both use a super soft memory foam that feels great against the head. After wearing them for a while you will forget you have them on. For comfort these things are a 10/10 rating when taking into consideration the quality build.

Now let's talk about sound quality. Being that these are 3.5mm instead of USB, you are already going to get better sound quality as an internal sound card (even the built-in motherboard ones) will sound better than the on headset version found in USB headsets. This headset has a 53mm transducer, which is quite large compared to most other headsets at this price range, and even twice as much price. Also the frequency response is 15Hz to 25,000Hz. This is a wider spectrum of bass/treble than you will see on many headsets at this price point. Needless to say they sound great. They aren't overly bassy like other headsets I have used. Sound quality is fantastic overall for an $80 headset. They work well for voice communication in gaming, but they also work excellent for music and movies.

I have seen numerous reviews of the mic on these being poor quality. I don't understand this. First of all this is a "gamer" headset. It's meant to be used to communicate with other players in games. It doesn't market itself as a headset for doing studio quality voice-over work. In the past few days I have talked with numerous friends on TeamSpeak, Ventrilo, Mumble and other in-game voice applications. All of my friends tell me that I sound clear as day. In fact I had compliments from friends I've gamed with for years that I sound better with these than with the last few headsets I have owned. So unless a few people got bad mics, I would say the mic on this headset is more than okay for it's purpose of gaming.

Overall I am really happy with this purchase. They are well worth $80. If I had paid twice as much for these I would be happy with the purchase. You cannot go wrong with the Kingston HyperX Cloud gaming headset - unless of course you have a neanderthal sized cranium.

rating 5.0. Great product for the price with almost no issues. One of the best, most comfortable gaming headsets I've used. First of all, I'll start with the looks. I got the white one even though I wanted the red one because only the white one was in stock. I was unsure of the color but after getting it I realized just how nice and sleek it looks. I especially like the white stitching on the top section. The headphones look and feel well made and not cheap at all.

Speaking of their feel, they don't feel like a $100 headphone. The plastic sections feel more expensive and they don't show fingerprints, which I love. The leather headband feels nice and so do the interchangeable ear pads (although I like the leather one more temperature-wise).

They are extremely comfortable. I can wear them for hours on end and still feel like I'm not wearing headphones. I used to wear Beats by Dre headphones to play on my PS4 (since I still didn't own a good sounding gaming headset) and they hurt my ears after a couple of hours. These I can wear for much longer.

Sound quality is pretty good. I actually don't notice a difference between these and the Beats audio wise (I'm by no means an audiophile but I can tell when something sounds bad). Surround sound is great and they are even good for listening to music (tested this with my iPhone).

The mic from what I can tell works as intended. I like that you can remove it from the headset but I also wish you could just swivel it around so it's not in your way when you're not using it instead of having to unplug it. Now I haven't recorded myself with the mic, but my friends have never complained that they can't hear or understand me, even though I'm a quiet speaker.

The headphones come packed with more accessories than you would imagine. You even get things such as airplane adapters! There are many different cables for different needs, extensions, extensions with mic controls, and an adapter to plug it in to devices that only have one input (such as my PS4 controller or my Macbook pro.)

Now the negatives I have (which are quite few). I already mentioned the mic connection issue. I wish I could swivel it up instead of having to unplug it when not in use. Not a major issue, but slightly inconvenient. Also, I don't like how the mic controls are on one of the extension cables and I wish they were on the cable already attached to the headphones closer to the headset itself. For example, to play on my PS4, I have the regular cable, the extension with the mic controls, and then the adapter to plug it into the controller. That's three connections. If the controls were on the main cable I would skip one of those. Also, with the extension the cable is massive. I have it wrapped up on my lab, and when I want to mute the mic or lower the volume, I have to look through the cable for the buttons. If I had this headset attached to a PC with the mic controls, it would be nowhere near me, and even more difficult to use.

Other than those two gripes, I am extremely happy with this purchase!

rating 5.0. i am super happy with this product the sound is amazing Hi, i am super happy with this product the sound is amazing, it has good sound quality, great build quality, and it feels incredibly comfortable (i use the cushioned ear-pads not the leather) but my ONLY issue is sometimes when i`m listening to music i can noticeable hear a static white noise but like i said it comes and go but with the quality of this product i think i can handle it not to mention it might be fault but either way i still love the product :) take it from me who is always online at least 9 hours a day weather it is doing my school or just pure gaming i can always rely on these there amazing :) thank you Kingston for making this beautiful headset.

rating 4.0. This headset was made for comfort for all head sizes! I really love this headset. I have a smaller head (petite female - 5'3), so I was worried that it wouldn't work. HOWEVER, when I took it out of the packaging (which was elaborate), I noted that the adjustments go from small child to huge head so I was easily able to find my perfect setting!

The default ear cups are extremely comfortable, and there is the perfect amount of pressure so the headset will stay on your head and keep the sound mostly within the cup, while not causing discomfort. The comfort given by the headset is great because even after hours of use (and I'm referencing 6 hours straight), you won't have raw ears or too much pressure on your head that might become distracting or painful like with other headsets. If your focus is comfort, and you use headsets a lot, then you will love this product.

The cup is not at all noise cancelling though, so if you are around annoying people or prefer isolation, you might need to look somewhere else. On sound, the quality was not what I had hoped for, definitely not a Bose quality, but it is still better than what you would get from a cheaper headset. The sound is a little sharp and the low tones don't transmit as well, but overall it is still above average sound.

All in all, if you game a lot, see this item on sale (I was able to get it for 50% off), love comfort, and don't get angry at your surroundings, then you should buy this headset.

rating 5.0. About as good as they come. Great all-around headset! There are several things to like about this headset. Here are the things that impressed me the most:

- Comfortable!! I read several reviews here and on gaming hardware review sites and the one consistent message they gave was that this is possibly the most comfortable headset you'll ever wear. I haven't reviewed or tried as many headsets as they have, but I can tell you I've never worn anything this comfortable. The over-the-ear cups are large enough to encompass my large ears so that almost nothing touches the ear. It comes with leather and foam (velour) pads so you can choose which you prefer. I've worn the headset for several days with the leather pad on one ear and the velour pad on the other so I could choose which was more comfortable and I honestly can't make up my mind - they are both super soft and comfortable. I've worn these at work as headphones for several hours at a time at work with no headaches afterwards. If comfort is high on your list for a headset, look no further - this headset definitely lives up to it's "Cloud" moniker!

Great sound. These headphones come with 53mm drivers, which are a bit larger than most headsets, and you can tell it in the bass end. You won't get the boomy bass like you do with Logitech or other gaming headsets that are designed to make explosions sound good - you'll get a nice, full bass that fills your ear without overwhelming your senses. The highs are crisp without being tinny and there just seems to be a good balance in the bass and treble regsiters. The mids are a little understated, but that is probably by design for a headset as opposed to audio headphones. But, it is only noticeable when listening to music with exposed vocals and you're really listening for it; if you use an equalizer to boost them a bit, you're fine.

Built for Real Gamers. These headphones are obviously made by people who have had gaming headsets and they addressed many things that are frustrating about most of them.
- Cords are either too long or too short. Kingston solved this by giving you a base headset with relatively short cords (4ft) that are good for sitting close to your PC or for use with your phone as well as a very long extension for when you're far away or using them with your console. The cord is a strong braided cord as well,
- Volume & Mic Mute. Like the cord length, they give you a choice. The in-line volume and mic mute are a separate item that you can plug into the end of the cord or not, depending on whether you want them.
- Aluminum Construction. The headpiece and swivel arms are made of aluminum, not plastic. Even many expensive headphones I've purchased had plastic swivel arms that broke easily when I dropped them (Bose). These are very sturdy aluminum but still light in weight.
- Removable Mic. Finally - a headset built by someone who understands that if you make the audio quality on a headset good enough, we will use them as headphones as well. I remove the mic until I actually need it. When I'm using them at work or playing single-player games, it's nice not to have to deal with a mic hanging near my face.

The one thing you need to be aware of is the impedence. Most gaming headsets have an impedence of 32 ohms - this is essentially the amount of power needed to drive the speakers properly. This headset has an impedence of 60 ohms, which is closer to that of stereo headphones. I assume this is partially due to the larger speaker drivers. What this means is that you will need a little more power to make them sound their best. It is not prohibiting at all - I listen to them all day plugged into my laptop or smartphone with great sound (I previously tried a Sennheiser PC-350 headset that ran at 150 ohms and it was almost impossible to get loud enough). But, for the best sound, you'll need a headphone amplifier or sound card that has one built-in. Most motherboards use a Realtek (or equivalent) chipset for audio that won't do any amplification. I purchased a Creative Labs Audigy FX sound card for $24 that has a 600 ohm amplifier built-in and the sound quality is noticeable when there's plenty of power to push those big drivers. It doesn't necessarily mean it's louder, the quality just shoots up significantly - it's worth the investment in a separate sound card or motherboard with a high-quality sound chipset.

Not insignificantly, the price is incredible for these headphones. For $100 or less, I don't believe you can even come close to these. In fact you might have trouble finding a better sounding headset for less than $300. As for comfort, I think these are probably about the best you can find.

rating 2.0. Microphone broke in 2 months Bought in mid October, microphone gave out today mid December. Frustrating because I was so pleased with the product but I guess Amazon reviews don't lie, the mic really is shotty. It really should be called headphones instead of a headset.

rating 5.0. PS4 - Outperforms my other options As far as budget headsets go, the HyperX Cloud (HXC) is hands down the best headset I own currently and will probably remain to be my go-to option on PS4.

For starters, this headset oozes high quality from the Apple-esque packaging / un-boxing to the sturdy build of the headset overall; not only that, but the leather used on the ear cups feels near-perfect on the ears when combined with its light weight form factor. This headset really does feel like a cloud, pun intended, wrapped around your head as it causes nearly no strain.

In comparison to my 2 other headsets, the Sony Gold Wireless and the Razer Kraken 7.1, the HXC managed to cause minimal strain/fatigue on my ears after a long session of 6+ hours playing Destiny and Battlefield 4. No lower jaw pressure was felt from the headset squeezing too hard either, as my Kraken 7.1 can do that sometimes and it's truly a pain.

That said, as the product claims this will be one of the most comfortable sets you have ever worn. I have a big head and Incan vouch for this claim personally. It may not be the same for everyone, but this outclasses my other two headsets in the comfort department by a wide margin.

Sound performance is astonishingly good seeing how it is powered through the Dualshock 4 controller via the headphone jack in the underside portion. Smooth bass, distinct clarity, and a spatial awareness that sounds to be more on than the Sony Golds or Kraken 7.1. The Krakens in particular drown you out with quoted volume and too much bass, with the HXC you are getting the best of both worlds as the bass is just right here; much akin to the Sony Golds but even better.

The inline volume controller works wonders as well, as the HXC does not have buttons on either ear cup, so you are left to use a little volume turner and a mute switch as well that works easily enough. This feature right here is a big one as the Sony Holds have an onboard mute button that comes in handy whereas the Kraken 7.1 have one on its digital boom mic, but due to a lack of firmware updates on Ps4 it shuts down chat functions altogether, thus requiring you to re-plug the USB back into your Ps4. So, in this regard, the HXC is better than the Kraken due to have a mute button that works and is comparable to the Sony Golds. The only trade off is that you are tethered to your controller due to the HXC not being a wireless set. No worries though as you are given ample cord slack, so there are not pull and tug issues going on.

To close this out, my biggest concern going into this purchase was that the HXC would not be loud enough. Other reviews had mentioned this and cited the game controller you plug this set into as being the culprit due to not powering them enough. I'll lay that to rest here, as the Dualshock 4 powers the HXC efficiently and the volume is more than loud enough.

This review was a little all over the place, but in closing I give the HXC my highest recommendation for a budget headset that doesn't suck and actually manages to convey a quality, both tangibly and aurally that is absent is other options at this price tier.

Kingston will most certainly be receiving my business when future iterations of this product are released if this i rial impression has any clout to go off of.

Pros:
* Excellent aluminum build, interchangeable leather ear cups with a cloth pair, and a premium feel that is noticeable all throughout the product.
* Nearly zero ear strain after 6+ hours of play
* Sound quality that outshines other options that I own
~This includes bass that is just right
*A microphone that is clear enough and audible by teammates on the PSN with no complaints thus far.
* Inline control for volume and muting others; this is fantastic as you won't have to use the PS4 dashboard to manually adjust the volume bars. Just leave the set at max volume within the PS4 and use the volume wheel from there on.

Cons:
* Not wireless - this is minor and does not bother me at all, but for some the excessive cord slack may be a mark against this set.
* Detachable microphone - concerns that this could bust at some point or get lost if utter carelessness befalls me.
* For very large heads this could still be a strain on your cranium, but take it from me, my dome is huge and this fits like a weightless glove.

It is removable, which is nice, but I never remove the mic and usually rotate it out of the way if I'm not using it, or if I'm eating something.  This mic is bendy and easily gets out of the way, but it's just different than what most of us are used to.  It is in no way a negative.  The thing comes boxed like whatever is inside costs $1,000.  If you're getting this thing at a reasonable price, you can't go wrong.  I play BF4 with it a lot and it gives me a nice advantage being able to hear where people are coming from.  I've never bought into the whole surround sound headphones, so I can't say how they work.  So, with these I can definitely tell clearly if a sound is coming from my right or left, but not if it's on my level, above or below me, so there's no surround sound.  Cords are nice and thick.  It's comfy too.
rating 2.0. Good while it lasted - then it stopped working. Worked great for about two weeks, then I started getting intermittent audio drops which got worse and worse until it just stopped working.

current_product_reviews:
rating 2.0. Good headphones, AWFUL microphone This headset is being touted as a great bang for the buck and the best budget headset. I don't agree. It's a great pair of headPHONES but the microphone is god awful.

Some issues with the mic are:

- Constant hiss in the background
- Voice volume is very low unless you turn up "Mic gain" (Mic gain is very bad because it makes the static/hiss very loud)
- Sometimes when I listen to Youtube videos (as an example), the Youtube audio would go into the mic (this is not a case where the video was too loud. The signal actually bleeds into the microphone sometimes)
- This microphone in particular picks up annoying breath sounds despite moving it to the side

I gave one to my sister and had her chat with me while we played games. I couldn't stand listening to her with this headset. I also know 2 people who own this headset and they are unfortunately the butt of many mic jokes because they sound so bad.

There are a few things that you could do to somewhat remedy this headset's mic quality.

- People say you could buy a sound card but I'm not really willing to accept that as a reasonable solution.
- Similar to above, there are $10 adapters that converts 3.5mm headphones and microphone jacks to USB. I wouldn't say that is a solution, but $10 makes it sound a little better.
- There is a solution that makes this microphone completely okay though. That would be push-to-talk. It is however, only a solution to people who enjoy using it. I myself hate push-to-talk.

Moving on from the mic, the build quality feels very sturdy and the packaging is amazing. I can't comment too much on the audio as I am not audiophile, but it was a pleasure listening/gaming to. The headset was comfy right from the start, but it did start to hurt both me and my sister after using it for more than an hour.

Again I say, I don't believe this deserves the status it has, but maybe people just don't care how annoying they sound to others.
other_product_reviews:
rating 2.0. Less satisfying than my Turtle Beach X31 I bought this headset mainly because it was completely wireless and it was on sale for $100. I had it for 3 days and returned it primarily because there was a constant buzzing noise. The louder the game volume slider was turned up, the louder the buzzing. Lowering the game volume however was not a good solution. I set up the XP 300 the same way I had my X31 set up as well.

Pros:
-Completely wireless (For me, this is a great pro)
-Works for xbox, ps3, pc and phone (I loved how I could use it for xbox and pc)
-Batteries lasted for a long time and you can play while charging
-Battery charge cable is very long
-Felt very fancy/made of high quality parts

Cons:
-Buzzing. I don't exactly know what's causing it. I hear it as soon as I connect (or even touch) either the red or white audio cables to the xbox hdmi to audio adapter or to the audio out ports on my TV. I have tried different wires to make sure it wasn't a faulty wire and I have tried the same setup with my X31 and there is no buzzing on those. I want to say that it's the transmitter. It could also have been a defective item but still.

-Dual-band frequency wasn't as good as it should have been. One of the major reasons why I bought this was because my wireless router, wireless mice, and other RF signals would cause interference with each other in my gaming area. Despite advertising dual-band specs, there was still interference (I don't even have my wireless router on) and there was still game audio being dropped randomly which would last up to almost 10 seconds.

-Switching between xbox mode and bluetooth mode (PC with bluetooth dongle in my case) was confusing. I honestly am still unsure how to do it.

-Voice quality. I am somewhat vague because, to my xbox friends the voice quality would seem good. However, my PC gaming friends claim that they could tell it was an xbox headset and that the voice quality is subpar compared to a PC headset. Take this with a grain of salt but to me, it seemed good.

-Microphone picks up audio coming from the headset speakers very badly. I know headsets aren't made to pick up what comes out of its own speakers but it's important to me personally. With the X31 headset I have, I can put the microphone in between the two speaker cups and the audio is SO clear that my friends can enjoy it (for example I can play them a funny audio clip from a TV show). With this XP 300, music is very difficult to pick up and when it does, it's most likely ear-piercingly loud.

-Earcups can be rotated but I don't see a purpose to this.

-The xbox 360 wireless puck needs to be turned off and on separately from the controller. It's easy to forget to turn the puck off after a gaming session (it can be on even if the controller is off).

-Headset was tight and uncomfortable. Not sure if it is small or just needed "breaking into."

Thoughts:

If it wasn't for the annoying buzzing, it would have been a good purchase for the sale price of $100. For the regular price of $170 I would say that it is a huge waste of money. With the exception of ability to use it on bluetooth devices, it doesn't hold a candle to my 2+ year old Turtle Beach X31's.

tl;dr don't buy unless it's on sale for a $100 and even then be wary.

rating 3.0. Pretty good UPDATE:

It turns out that having this on makes it harder for the nunchuk to detect movement.
_________________________________________________________________________________________

The wireless adapter is a good piece of merchandise.

I have used this for Legend of Zelda: Twilight Princess, Dead Space: Extraction and Muscle March.
It worked great for Twilight Princess and Dead Space: Extraction. Muscle March is a game where you need to have fast reflexes with both the nunchuk and the Wiimote and I can't tell whether the wireless was part of the reason why the game was as challenging as it was. However, regarding wireless lag, there doesn't seem to be any.

It does take 2 AAA batteries so I suggest that you get some rechargeables. Since the batteries are stored at the bottom, the nunchuk becomes a little bottom heavy. Not so heavy that you struggle to hold it, but heavy that it makes waggling the nunchuk left to right a little bit more sluggish. I am sure though with more use, this can be adapted to.

Now, onto the biggest problems.

Biggest problem: The wireless dongle that attaches to the Wiimote is loose( tested with 2 Wiimotes). So loose that I can pull it out without pushing down the 2 nobs on the side. This is very troubling to me because I like to over-exaggerate my hand/arm movements and I get worried that the dongle will fall off.

Fix: So far the only fix I could find was using a rubber band from the very front of the Wiimote to the back of the dongle. It is a good fix however.

Second biggest problem: You cannot use this with the silicon sleeve unless you do some snipping at the base of it. Some people don't worry about this but I personally like to keep the sleeve on.

Last problem: The 2 bright blue LED's on this accessory are very annoying. They blink quite often and the nunchuk LED is pretty strong.

Fix: I had an over abundance of rubber bands so I decided to cover up the LED's with those. It works pretty good too.

Final opinion: There are a few modifications you need to make and some getting used to, but for most games it should work great.

RetroN 3 Sound quality is much better, but RetroTRIO is more sturdy and has viable controllers (Retron 3's are usless) This is a nice looking system, and it's also sturdy (except for the front flap, which can just be removed).  It plays the retro games like it says.  If your TV accepts regular Composite RCA/AV signal (not to be confused with Component), you're in good shape.  My TV only accepts Component signal (no signal when plugging this system in cause it is a Composite signal), so I tested on a friends TV.  Color and Graphics are great, games start up on first try, and the controllers are pretty darn good too.  My only complaint is the sound quality.  I tested Contra and Life force.  Life force had a strange ticking in the background, and Contra had a strange high pitch ringing with every bullet shot... not horrible, but annoying.  It's not the games themselves.  I know this cause my friend has a RetroN 3 and the games sound like they do on the original system, when using his Retron.  I ended up returning this mainly cause I couldn't find an HDMI converter, that would not cause gameplay delays.  I ended up ordering a RetroN 5 cause it has an HDMI output built into the system.

rating 3.0. The first one I purchased the door covering the controller ... The first one I purchased the door covering the controller ports would only open all the way 10% of time. rating 2.0. Trouble. This is my first experience with a clone console and going by the excellent reviews (PC Mag, Satoshi Matrix, StopDrop&Retro) I went in with high expectations. Maybe I was expecting too much.

The first unit I ordered was a silver and black model. Out of the box the unit had some cosmetic damage/flaws but otherwise looked intact. The first game I tried was A Link To The Past and my initial impressions were very good. Video quality was pristine through the S-Video port, audio sounded right on the money and the controllers, while slightly flimsy feeling, worked like a charm. The second game I tried didn't fair as well-- Super Mario World displayed all kinds of instability in the picture that seemed to be caused by overly bright scenes. This was confirmed when launching both Castlevania IV and TMNT Turtles in Time-- the Konami logo at boot up would flash and shake as the backdrop went white. I tested several more games and even tried different cables and even a different display and the issue persisted. I packed it up for return to Amazon.

Despite that issue I was still impressed enough with the overall package to try another unit. This time I decided to order the black/red model as it was a few bucks cheaper and I guessed it would look a little less cheap than the silver unit. I was right on that-- the 2nd unit arrived and looked much nicer than the silver model it had replaced. Indeed this unit had none of the cosmetic flaws my first model had and seemed to have some revisions as well: when peaking inside the cartridge ports I noticed the PCB was now green, my silver/black model had been blue. That wasn't all, the controllers appeared to be a newer model than the ones included with my silver/black unit. They felt a lot less cheap, now had little tension relievers at the top and, most significantly, now terminated into an actual SNES style plug instead of the 9-pin Genesis style plugs like the original silver/black controllers. I again connected the console to my display with high hopes... and again those hopes were immediately dashed. Right away I noticed the audio was off. It sounded as if the volume was turned up way too high but no matter what I set the volume to on my TV or stereo the audio still sounded strained and distorted. Game after game had the same exact issue with overly loud, distorted audio and to compound matters the new controllers were awful-- I mean truly bad. The D-pads no longer seemed to have the pivot of the originals and the face buttons and shoulders would get stuck easily. Again I packed it up for return to Amazon.

So now what? I really like the idea of this console but based on my experience so far I'd have to assume I'd be expecting a lot to get one without some kind of flaw. I know my sample size of two isn't exactly definitive but I don't think anyone would blame me for being suspicious of Retro-Bit's quality control at this point. I'm also disappointed that Retro-Bit replaced their crazy SNES/Genesis hybrid gamepads of the older system with these new SNES clone pads-- the original controllers were really, really good despite their somewhat chintzy feel.

rating 2.0. Not as advertised!!! First of all I do like the system but.. Then after testing it seems that the snes port one with the retrobit controllers has a mapping problem. (And yes i made sure the switch was in the right place) So had it send back for a replacement which is normal with electronics at times. Then i get the second one in and the same problem. Email retro bit with no response yet.

rating 5.0. Simple and cheap. This is a vital part of my playable collection. For the price, this thing rules. SNES was a little spotty for the first few tries, but it has seemed to become more reliable. I love this thing.

rating 3.0. Not for the purist. The Retro-Bit Super Retro Trio is a well made clone-console (especially compared to the competition), but it has its problems and ultimately will disapoint you if you are looking to duplicate the same output as an original NES.

Physically, the thing is solid and durable feeling with thick robusut plastic-work, though I found the insterting and taking-out of games to be too hard. It doesn't like to let go and yoyu will need to hold down the console when lifting out a game.

My biggest problem though is that the video and sound ouput was immediately off from the beginning. I was working side-by-side with a woprking NES and the RetroTrio's color was about 10% more yellowed and dark- not enough to ruin the actual playing experience, but distracting none the less. I tested this with about a dozen games and it was noticable with all. In about half the games (especially Super Mario Bros.) the sound output was also off- specifically tinny and, I don't know how else to describe it, but the more bass-driven beats sounded like *clicking.*

As someone who is tryingt to find a clone console that plays games JUST like an NES the combination of these flaws was unnacceptable to me.

rating 1.0. The SNES port stopped working after 2 days. First ... The SNES port stopped working after 2 days. First I lost audio, then it stopped working entirely. I tried multiple cartridges, all of which were cleaned and functioning. No issues with the Genesis or NES ports yet. This thing is cheaply made.

rating 1.0. Great concept...terrible execution Was so excited to get this 3-in-1 system. Went to a gaming store and bought games for all 3 systems. SNES game cartridges had to be wiggled forward to work because slot is too big. But at least it worked. The Sega Genesis part was awful. After only one time of use it stopped working completely. We tried all 8 of our Sega games, and got nothing. Now I have to waste my time returning the item, and then have to wait until they receive the item prior to being reimbursed. I'm going to try a different 3-in-1 system made by a different company, since I spent so much money on games. Great concept...terrible execution.

rating 1.0. Great idea, crappy product I bought this on October 26th as a Christmas gift for my 12 year old daughter who is in love with retro video games.  I installed the console onto her television today, only to find that the NES game slot is not working properly.  It will not respond to the controller.  So I went to return it today on Amazon, but they will not allow me to return it as there is only a 30 day return period even though the gift was not RECEIVED until yesterday.  I am annoyed on so many levels.

rating 2.0. The Super Nintendo part worked great The Super Nintendo part worked great,  but unfortunately the Sega Genesis part had sound but no picture. Since it was a Christmas gift, I can't really ask for it back to return it.  Hopefully the console is fixable.
rating 4.0. Four Stars I love video game history!

rating 5.0. Solid Console for True Vintage Gaming! This is a superb console for those with existing libraries of cartridges, and a wonderful opportunity for expanding your collection. Much more satisfying than using questionable roms on other platform emulators, it's legacy connectors allow you to use legacy controllers. Though the included controllers are, indeed, fairly well-built, they're not the best at running Genesis games. That is literally the only non-positive thing I could find with this ridiculously affordable workhorse console. NES, SNES, and Genesis carts function superbly, with immediate, fluid response and crisp graphics. I suggest the use of the RCA outputs and skip the S-Video. This console had rekindled my love affair with NES games, and introduced me into the 16-bit era that I had skipped when I turned to PC gaming in the early 1990s. I cannot recommend this console more highly!!

rating 4.0. I knew this was an issue with the competetor as they do not work like original hardware, but alas, no luck.  Otherwise everything works phenominally and the controllers are great!

rating 5.0. A Genesis, Super Nintendo, and Nintendo all in one!!! This system is great! We have had it for nearly 3 months now and play it almost every night and really enjoy it! I haven't got to test out the NES side yet, but the Genesis and SNES work fine. The controllers aren't half bad either! We have it hooked up to our 50" LCD TV via composite connections and the picture looks so much nicer than when I was a kid playing on an old 19" tube TV. We just bought a Super UFO 8 here on Amazon and run it on the Trio just fine! About the only downfall I can think of is this thing smells of something fierce. Like the manufacture didn't give them air-out time or something LOL. It is slowly going away and isn't that bad. Buy it, re-live your youth and finally beat those games you left behind as a kid!
