rating 4.0. good for the price, something to use for a bit gauntlet on nes wont work, it starts fine but the game it self wont play right. i tried the tengen and nes cart, same story. and two pegs came out removing an nes title. but i got a year out of it fine. plays all other titles ive used in the console. something to fill the void really till you get all the consoles back. works fine for a while though...if you can care for it. its very light, and playing sega on the snes controllers it comes with is rather uncomforable and confusing but does work. hope this helps.

rating 5.0. Perfect console for the die-hard retro gamers!!! Brand new gaming console, everything in the package is nicely wrapped and taped. The console itself plays all different types of games for the three types of cartridges (Sega Genesis, SNES, and NES). This machine simply works with every single one of those cartridges. I have a Mario Kart which is for the USA region, so I switch the region format to NTSC, and another Made In Japan Super Mario World cartridge, which didn't run under the NTSC mode, so I flipped the switch to NJ (which is NTSC for Japan), and it works!!! The two controllers are sturdy, simple, lightweight, but very well-made, and the buttons are responsive as you can possibly get, the games run very fluidly. Even the newly-made aftermarket "all-in-one" NES cartridges work perfectly on this console, trouble-free.

Just make sure that if you are using the controllers that come with this console, flip the controller mode switch to "Genesis", yeah I know, it could get confusing. The layout of the controllers are totally SNES style, but the actual jacks and ports for these two controllers work under Genesis format.

Another thing to pay attention is that the NES-SNES-GENESIS switch is a little bit floppy, especially when you move the switch all the way up to NES mode, I had to also push down the switch a little bit to make sure it is secure in the NES mode, otherwise both the indicators for NES and SNES will light up, meaning the switch is touching both modes and not secure in NES mode. But I'm sure it is only a minor QC issue, because I really can't find anything to complain about this nice console XD
rating 2.0. Two Stars Difficult to attach the cases and see the recognition

rating 2.0. Great idea, great nostalgia... underwhelming in execution Really great, exciting idea for a retro gaming product. Really reasonably priced, looks great and unfortunately just doesn't replicate the experience very well. Games look horrible on the screen, and the lag is brutal. However, again - well designed aesthetically and I think these folks heart is in the right place, but this console just doesn't work that well overall. Worth a try tho, for the very low price - HOWEVER, I would gladly pay double for a device that worked well.

rating 5.0. Awesome product It works awesome. It has played every single game i have put in it so far without fail. Controler is actually pretty nice and feels like decent quality. Only negative i have noticed is the front red door for the controller ports is a little flimsy and falls off but it snaps right back in. Sound and picture quality is awesome with the rca and s cord. Even on my 4K ultra HD TV.

rating 3.0. A few problems, minor and major It's okay, except for a few problems.
The controllers were fine, but now the buttons don't work. The NES port is janky; You can make the cartridge wiggle back and forth. The A/V cable is great, but the S-Video jack causes discoloration. The image attached clearly shows that the controllers came with some wearing.
All I have to say is that the console works, and that's all that matters to me.

rating 2.0. Good concept, but very flawed. This is my first experience in dealing with a clone system. I've been looking into a system like this for the sole reason of saving space with my original NES, SNES, and Genesis systems; seeing the positive reviews online, I decided to purchase the Retro-Bit Trio a little while ago and I'm not pleased. Does it save space? Yes. Does it work properly? No.

Getting this thing to work is next to impossible. For the record, I decided to test the following games: Castlevania 1 & 3 (NES), Link to the Past and Donkey Kong Country (SNES) and finally, Contra: Hard Cops and Double Dragon on the Genesis. My results are as follows;

For the NES, Castlevania seems to work on and off; sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't. The NES button on the console is VERY picky and usually gets stuck in the middle between the NES/SNES switch. When I do get the switches to work properly, the game itself I believe needs no introduction; however, the colours are much more disoriented as opposed to using the original system. Now I normally use the composite video for quality purposes, but it's pretty spotty as well. The S-Video looks awful. Castlevania 3 doesn't work at all on the system. Plain and simple - I've tried everything. And normally I'd say it's my cartridge but again, I've put it in my toploader and it works like a charm. When I get it to work on the Retro-Bit, I enter my name and the initial cutscene loads up with Trevor Belmont as a pixellated mess of incomprehensible texture. On top of that, after said cutscene, the game simply refuses to work. Fantastic start.

As per the SNES, the results were roughly the same for Link to the Past and Donkey Kong Country. Overall, I was pretty pleased with the results from these tests.

And finally we have the Genesis games; being Contra and Double Dragon. My only complaint here is that I've had Double Dragon crash CONSTANTLY on the Retro-Bit; and again, when I encounter a problem I will test the game on the original system to confirm the issue..and Double Dragon works flawlessly on the Genesis - which I have a model 2 version.

Finally, I tested the other ports for controller support, and again I am displeased. I have the same NES controller I've had since the 80s and it doesn't work in the Retro-Bit. The same goes for the SNES; the Genesis controllers work alright, but why don't the others? The controllers you get out of the box work, but I don't want to use those - if it has the ports for the original controllers, I expect them to work. Overall, I think I'll stick with the original systems or look into alternatives for clone consoles.

current_product_reviews: rating 2.0. Functional system, crap controllers.  Consequently, not worth the price. Despite two stars, I kept this system.  The base system performs well and reliably, but the controllers are complete garbage.  They were such complete garbage they started having failures after about an hour of use.  They failed in such a way that I thought the game cartridge I was using was actually defective, and initiated a return on it.  On a lark, recalling that I read a review about the poor controllers, I replaced them with 2 NES replacement controllers, which fixed the problem.  But this added another 50% to this system price, which is simply too expensive for what it is.  There's no reason to put these crap controllers in here, and nobody in their right mind would expect them to last.  There is a world of difference between actual NES or NES replacement controllers and these.
other_product_reviews: rating 5.0. Feels as good as OEM to these hands. Pretty nice controllers - feel as good to these hands as the original equipment NES controllers.  Cable is plenty thick and sturdy - if anything maybe a little too heavy.  Only used for about 4 days by 4 kids for an hour or two a day, but got plenty of rough handling during that time, and now in storage until the next trip to Grandma's house.  Also, these were used to replace cheapo controllers that came with a Famicom 3 in 1 8 bit gaming system that had NES, Sega, and SNES ports.  Worked great with that system in the NES ports, and Famicom's controllers are disposable junk that last about an hour, so if you're ordering a Famicom system, get some of these.

input:
rating 2.0. much better. Now my "recovery items" actually work and I ... This game has a cult following to say the least, but a cult it still a cult. Take that at its literal meaning. I thought I was getting an cyber-machina Japanese action game with the usual peculiarities. I am not sure what I got and I am likely to never know. I don't have a problem with hard games, but 5 minutes in the credits rolled after my "ship" crashed into a building in the middle of a cut scene. OK, you have my full attention now. It seems you have to complete the first level to auto-save. There was a mention of a different save system, but since I die on the second hit and have to restart the game, I haven't made it that far. 20 more times. OK, time to put the difficulty back to normal and once more.. that's it. There.. much better. Now my "recovery items" actually work and I can make progress. Past the first level. Onto an oil rig. Beat another boss. OK, now really big boss. Damn, I died. Restart tutorial. 1 hour 5 minutes in and still no save. Back to the literal square one. At this point, my hate for the unskippable intro cut scenes is so developed, I could never like the game and I don't have 2 hour chunks to hope I can make it through a level. Other users say the save scarcity lightens up after the that first massive level. I don't care. This isn't Dark Souls and making the tutorial your killing floor is the dumbest idea I have every seen. It's machina masochism and I am surprised the publisher went along with it. None of the professional reviews I read even mention the 1 hour plus non-save tutorial. The "glorious shifting camera that makes the character bug sized one moment and giant the next" [sic] is more likely to turn your stomach (or kill you) than create a memorable experience. Sorry, killing you with weird camera angles is not a game asset and it doesn't really belong in the tutorial level. That could have come later. At some point this game will be $10. Buy it and give it to someone you don't like. Tell them you beat it in two hours.

If you are interested in this game, take a serious look at user reviews spread out across the internet. Make sure you know what you're getting into, just watch out for the loyal fan base. This isn't a 5 start game by any means, although like anything is has a niche. Figure out if you are in that little crack or not. There clearly are people who like it, but this is not a game for a few hours of fun. It falls into a different category.

rating 5.0. Positive Evolution Towards Gaming When you first play Nier: Automata it isn't completely what you're going to expect. Judging by the gameplay footage, the pictures, etc., you expect just a hack and slash action adventure rpg game. Which it is, but also delivers so much more.

This game was loaded with goodies and cameos from the first nier game plus drakengard as well. Not only that, it offers an overview of characters giving a new stand on the game in some parts, dynamically changing the way you need to approach said spots. While the enemy variety can be kind of limiting, there is a certain charm about them that makes them, in a way, unique in the game. At some points I felt attached to the enemies more than the protagonists, 2B and 9S.

The customizing in this game is extensive as well as what some would consider overpowering, making you feel kind of like a god. But the difficulty is balanced well enough to where if you want more of a challenge, it will be there for you. That's another thing about customizing though. It will cater to your play-style however you feel comfortable. Which is enjoyable.

I've never played the first nier nor drakengard, but I have read about its amazing multiple ending segments that actually can continue the game further the more times you play it. And with Automata it was no exception. I beat the game once only to be given a message saying that there is more to be discovered. Tempting it I play a new game on the saved data only to get the same story, but in a different perspective by playing as 9S this time around. The gameplay between 2B and 9S is somewhat the same, but there is enough of a difference between the two such as hacking instead of a heavy attack with 9S that it actually felt like a new game.

This story goes so much further than any other linear story that I have played to date! Hell, I'll give it this; most open world games, including gta V, don't have this kind of pacing and sort of dynamic story arch with sub- quests. The one game I believe comes close to this would be witcher 3. And the best part of it is, Nier DOESNT NEED DLC TO CONTINUE ITS STORY (looking at you, Destiny).

So the only fault I felt Nier Automata had would honestly be it's lock on system. In my opinion, it is quite tedious to get the hand of it. But with a little practice that shortly become a minor nuisance.

So if you liked the first Nier, you're going to love Automata. If you've never played the first one, that's fine too because the game doesn't heavily lean on it considering that if you played the first one and got the "True" ending, then it'll all make sense. This game offers a thought provoking story, in depth lore, and immersion. Truly a game you should have in your archive.

rating 5.0. A brilliant, unrestrained work of art. On the surface, Nier: Automata might look like a standard action JRPG. If you read a summary of the basic premise and gameplay, you'll find that many of the usual tropes are present. You could be forgiven for thinking you'll be hacking and slashing through your standard heavy-handed plot and godlike entities by mashing a button or two for 20+ hours. On the surface, the game almost seems like it's trying to affirm those expectations.

Almost immediately the game starts to subvert those expectations piece by piece, deconstructing and twisting in on itself until you've abandoned any notion of predictability.

The first thing that surprised me about the game was the consistent fluidity of the combat, and how the mechanics seem to hold up even as the game throws new ideas and situations at you. The basic controls and rhythm remain the same as the game forces the perspective of a top-down shooter, or a 2D platformer. These experimental shifts never require you to mentally adapt or use different controls. I've never seen a game transform itself so frequently and effortlessly.

The story is also in a league of its own, as it starts with a very simple premise yet expands into something far more transgressive and poignant. It conveys its themes in a very surreal, postmodern style that blends tragedy with dark, offbeat humor, even going so far as experimenting with the fundamental structure of video games itself. (Disclaimer: Do not stop playing once the credits roll. You've barely scratched the surface.)

This is one of those games that you will think about long after you finish it. I can't recommend it enough.

rating 5.0. A Master Piece of a Game Nier Automata is a master piece of a video game and has become my all time most favorite game. The story is magnificent everything about it is done so very well, it fully fleshes out each and every single character along with its rich lore and lush environments it may seem that Nier: Automata has no flaws as it is so meticulously done. Though it is meticulously done everything has its faults and for me it is that the world is too small I would love for it to have much larger world maybe 4 to 5 times its current size so that I can explore more of this destroyed earth and go into the unknown only to wait and see what appears before me whether it be an Ancient Machine or a new undiscovered world. Another thing I guess you can say is a con is the weather of the game, though I know that the world has gone through a lot and has been destroyed by many thing and has become this dystopian society in a way where androids are constantly fighting against these machines in an never ending war but I would like it to change from daytime to night have it rain every now and then as this would add more immersion to the game and just give you those few moments of peace to stare out to see the wonderful scenery and just think about what awaits you on the other side. Other than those few little nit picky things I mentioned the one thing that stands outs above all else is the soundtrack. No mere words can describe how exquisite and how outstanding it is, each and every single moment of the game I was mesmerized by the music it has reached a level of greatness that has transcended our very minds and our very ears cannot fathom such beauty. If you love games with a amazing soundtrack, lore fulled worlds, amazing characters, and a magnificent story then this is the game for you.

rating 5.0. Amazing Story, Visceral Action Pros:
+ Roller Coaster Story
+ Beautiful Soundtrack
+ Tight Combat, (4 Weapon types, can wield two at a time and changes the player's attacks depending on which two weapon types)
+ Lots of side missions and collectibles

Cons:
- Might not be for everyone.

Personally, this game is a treasure and I have to start out by talking about the story. There are serious moments but also plenty of humorous moments that bring the story together in a nice, cohesive package, never feeling too simple and never getting too complex. This game also gives you a lot of freedom when it comes to the open world, if you want to blow through the main story in about 22 hours or so, you can. I elected to do a lot of the side quests and took my time enjoying the world, the characters and finding the hidden weapons scattered throughout (clocked in around 32 hours when I completed N:A). I also thought the English voice acting was really well done.

One thing I will point out is that this game has multiple endings, but they should be treated more like Acts in a play or musical. There are 5 main endings, endings A and B should be treated as Act I and endings C, D, and E should be treated as Act 2 (the story continues for each ending). You do not have to replay the game or collect any extras to see the additional endings, rather they are obtained in a linear fashion. This multiple ending approach might turn some people off, but I thought it was an different, interesting approach to the way the story is told.

The combat was also a huge selling point for me. You can wield any two combination of the 4 Weapon types at a time to mix and match different combat styles that suit your tastes. Wanna wield two Heavy Swords and demolish enemies in a weighty manner? Or my personal favorite of a Light Sword and a Spear? The choice is yours and it all feels incredibly well done and polished. You also get a ranged weapon in the form of your Pod, and in addition to bullets, you can fit him with different Pod Programs that let you perform special abilities such as a melee shield or a strong laser attack. There are also many different kinds of Upgrade Chips, which let you build your own character to focus more on offense (melee damage, critical chance, etc.), defense (increased health, lifesteal, etc.) or support (dodge distance, auto-heal, etc). There's a limitless amount of ways to build your pod and character around the playstyle you most enjoy, and for me that is a huge plus.

If you are a fan of action RPGs, this game is a must-play, and I highly recommend anyone looking for a good story and fast, fluid combat to give it a try.

rating 3.0. pretty well actually I was looking for another RPG fix to hold me over for Persona 5. This worked, pretty well actually. NieR: Automata was a very polarized game. The pros are incredible and unexpected, but the cons are equally severe and unexpected. Everything good about this would be 5-star quality and should make for a 5-star review. Everything bad about this are frustrating enough that I sat here not able to pick *any* score from 1 to 5 for a while before writing this review.

The Pros:
1. Absolutely incredible storyline. It manages to take you on a bizarre ride, while keeping a white knuckle grip on "reasonable sanity" that keeps cohesion in the story. It's an epic story, but it's a small and critical chapter in that story.

2. The way that story is told. No spoilers, but this is brilliantly unique. You'll see this same point again in the cons, but the way the story was told was awesome.

3. Immersion... The way NieR: Automata combines several game types, but still makes you feel like you're THERE fighting a giant boss. This isn't as unprecedented as the story itself, but it's good.

Cons:

1. The opening. I have never given up on an RPG, but this got me close. The opening is extremely difficult by normal ARPG standards (no, not difficult by Dark Souls standards), but gives you NO save point until you've fought 5 bosses. If you die, you better get to love that intro.

2. The difficulty. I'm not calling it hard. It's the contrary. The difficulty steps awkwardly. Easy is TOO easy (except one part near the end) unless you intentionally avoid the combat-assist entirely. For someone looking for an even moderately-casual ride, normal is WAY too much of a headache. This might be worsened by the fact that I lost 90 minutes of playtime to game overs in the "opening tutorial" alone.

current_product_reviews:
rating 3.0. Story? Yes. Trophy Hunting? Easy Peasy. Combat? *Shrugs* Environment: OMG Just be over already... So. I got the platinum trophy in this game. My gameplay time was 62 hours. Do I feel like I got my moneys worth with this game? Yes. If I could go back in time, would I skip over this title? Yes. One thing that I feel is the most important to highlight is something I wish I knew when I started. You may have heard that it requires completing it multiple times, which is true, but each time is from a different perspective and make the story so much better because of it. There is a huge warning I want to put out to people, it is near the end of the review, so those who like surprises don't have to read it. Let's get this review started.

Gameplay is vary varied. To a point where it doesn't seem like the game knew what it wants. It's mainly a 3D hack and slash combat exploration game. But there's 2D platforming, 3D platforming. 2.5D platforming. Twin Stick Shooter parts. Parts where you're in a mech suit flying. Parts where you're in the mech suit just normal fighting. None of these styles were terrible, but none were amazing either.

Difficulty is inconsistent. I started off on Normal because Very Hard is a one hit kill with permadeath that I didn't like the idea of. Normal was pretty easy. I didn't die up until the 3rd boss fight. And when I was dying, it wasn't because of skill, it was because the camera would get stuck in a weird position and I couldn't tell if it was by design or happenstance. This is also when I realized I wasn't enjoying the game, so I looked up if any of the trophies were difficulty related, and they aren't, so I put it on easy. And easy can be REALLY easy. There are upgrades you can get that make certain parts of combat run themselves. So you can do auto-evade, and auto fire, and auto attack, and auto weapon switch, and then all you need to do is just go from map marker to map marker, the game can play itself.

Story. It is complicated. I am still unpacking what I just played, and I will need to revisit some of it to fully understand it. This part is important. There are six endings that I got, there might be more. And two endings aren't really endings, but, yea. Take notes while playing.

For Trophy Hunters! You want an easy platinum trophy? Put the game on easy, make sure that you have the Auto chips enabled, and only do the main story missions. Don't get sidetracked by other stuff. Don't sell anything. Only buy the upgrades that let you equip more upgrades.You want to max the drop rate with upgrades. After you get the 4 main endings, there is an NPC in the game that will sell you trophies using in-game currency. Around 2 million in game currency units should be enough, which I had way more of just by playing normally. Do it right, and you can be complete in 10-15 hours.

For this next part, I will be talking about something as vague as I can, but I still feel the need to announce that it could be sonsidered a spoiler, so.

MASSIVE SPOILER AND GENERAL WARNING!

The game will delete your save data if you let it.There is one part after getting one of the endings where you get to watch the credits while playing the hacking mini game, and the game asks you if you want to help out your fellow gamers. It is all pretty straight forward. But the part that I did not know was that it will delete all three of your save game slots, not just the one you make the decision on.

rating 3.0. All in all, not worth buying if you game on PC I've read this mouse for a few months. I was a little disappointed that when plugging it in to the computer it only acted as a charger; no direct connection is made so can't really improve the response. It felt nice for being a plastic mouse and worked well enough. As time went on I noticed that some clicks wouldn't register, probably from the dongle being about a meter away, so the wireless communication isn't great. The battery life isn't great; it lasts for about 3 days of use before I have to charge it again. You can at least charge it and use it at the same time but you can't just leave it charging because it won't stop charging when done, can over-charge, and reduce the battery life even further. The listing seems to indicate some sort of control to have the LEDs be one solid color of the 7 but there isn't. It kind of just displays whatever 3 colors it feels like and it's not as pretty as the pictures make it look. It's kind of a "you get what you pay for" case but I almost feel like I just threw away money with this mouse because it's past the return period and it's not even adequate for PC gaming so I'll have to get a new one just to have a less frustrating experience and get the most out of my play time. Find a better mouse.

input: rating 5.0. Very small and portable, but at the same time ... Very small and portable, but at the same time has lots of space for games and extra accessories, as well as the Switch itself. rating 1.0. I bought this in October and gave it to my ... I bought this in October and gave it to my nephew for Christmas the handle is broke and the zipper broke today.  I know this is outside the return window but since it was a Christmas gift can you make an exception and return my money.
rating 5.0. Best for the price This is the best switch case I've seen for it's price.  Lots of thought went into every detail of this case, and is the ebst case you're getting for this cheap.

rating 5.0. Fits everything I need on the go! Great case for travel and everyday use. In the top compartment I can store my gel con guards, my smaller usb c charging cord and Apple 12w adapter, aux cord for my headphones and 2 joy con straps. It holds everything well and keeps a nice shape.
Perfect switch accessory!

rating 2.0. It messes up your JoyCons This product while convenient is not ideal for the Switch. Everytime that I use it my JoyCons are messed up for days following. I assume that is the because the case is too tight on them. I give it two stars because it does protect the Switch from dropping, and can transport almost all of the parts and games with ease.

rating 5.0. The best protection on a budget. This is a nice quality case, don't let the generic appearance fool you. I had the PDP case, which looks cooler, but hardly made to protect the console. Hestia's stitching is great, materials are strong, and the handle is very comfortable.

The top zipper storage is large enough to hold your Nintendo adapter, but it is a bit of a tight fit.

I would be a little concerned about the pressure of it against the screen, despite being between the padded game card flaps, if I expected any chance of someone tossing it onto a hard surface or it being dropped directly on the flat sides.

Considering that most cases don't fit the adapter in this compact size, I'm extremely happy and feel 100% protected when traveling now.
rating 1.0. Missing Missing inside strap to hold switch, rendering it useless.

rating 4.0. Great product overall This case appealed to me because it was cheaper than others but still had everything I was looking for in one. It seems sturdy enough to withstand falls and the Switch fits very snugly inside with or without a bumper on and stays in place nicely. My only complaint is that the little strap to strap the Switch in is weird and I don’t understand the Velcro placement.

Also, the packaging cautioned against an odor about the case and I definitely did notice this. The packaging suggested you open the case and leave it out and this did seem to help.

I was also impressed by this item and the seller that sold it because I got an email from the seller before my item even arrived, with a link to contact them should I have any problems. They didn’t bombard me with emails asking for a review, they just emailed me once to thank me for my order and let me know how to contact them with any problems. Customer service is a big factor when I’m considering 3rd party sellers and I feel the customer service I received from Hestia was exceptional. I will definitely keep them in mind for future purchases.

rating 4.0. Amazing quality but SMELLS - now rectified Good case keeps the switch safe and I like the case itself. However sadly the case smells really bad. It smells as if it's been smoke damaged.

As I mentioned the case is amazing quality. Not sure if they all smell this bad though, I don't like putting my switch in the case for any prolonged time as the switch smells too. I will wait a few days, maybe the smell will fade and be acceptable. Will update my review appropriately.

Edit: While mine did actually smell bad. Seller done every thing to rectify the situation. Sent a new/different product and it was a great product. Good seller don't hesitate to purchase from this seller.

rating 5.0. Looks great, feels great, and works great. Just what I needed. For such an affordable case, I had some doubts. However, I was pleasantly surprised by it's quality. The Switch fits perfectly and you can even have a case on it, which fits more snugly. You can fit a few smaller items in the zippered pouch, such as cables, microfiber cloth, earbuds, etc. However, don't expect to fit the original Nintendo charging cable (with the brick). It won't fit at all. Also, I was concerned about the zippered end doing damage and it is soft enough and positioned well that it isn't an issue.

Everything works great, looks great, and feels great. I don't worry about short drops, something pressing on the Switch, or about the contents. The only thing I haven't used are the game card holders as I use mostly digital games, so I can't say anything about that.

rating 5.0. Plenty of space I've been using this case for several months and it has now become my favorite switch case. It holds 20 cartridges, handles the switch even with a mumba tough case. And the upper compartment is spacious enough to fit my hori playstand, 2 extra joycons, 4 joycon straps and some screen cleaning wipes.
Definitely recommend this to those who want to carry extra joycons and a stand, or maybe a usb powerbank for charging.

Edit - 6 months of use:
I still stand by my original post. The case is holding up well and is my main transportation for the switch. It's nice to have my playstand and an extra pair of joycon when I go out. It holds my switch even with my case on it. One of the best and cases at this price. Other cases are either larger in physical size, more expensive or don't hold as much. Definitely recommend this case.

rating 5.0. love it! Just like the image it holds game ... Simple little case, love it! Just like the image it holds game cartridges and joy con groves to hold the switch in place. Good budget case it also came with a thank you letter I thought was neat and will buy again from this company 👍👍👍

rating 5.0. Good case - great value for the price Bought this for my son after he got a Switch for Christmas. The case is sturdy and the perfect size for his device. It’s convenient that it also holds games and some small accessories too. So far, we are very pleased. Great value for the price compared to other cases we looked at.

rating 5.0. I previously bought a case for like 35 because it had a stand to come with ... I previously bought a case for like 35 because it had a stand to come with it and Mario Kart on the go is big for me. The case was horrible, literally couldn't even fit the stand you bought it with and the Switch. A zipper popped off within a week and then sort of unrelated but the strap snapped as well. I realized I couldn't just expect to put a stand in the back of a case and it'd need just the tiniest bit more room.

Enter this case. When I tell you that this case is significantly better than any case you'll fine, I'm serious. It doesn't seem like it could be so much better, but the outer shell is tough with feet so that the case doesn't slide everywhere when you're messing with it, the storage area is fully secured behind mesh/zipper wall (many cases have partially open storage), and unlike the case I bought, this case EASILY holds the stand without feeling like it's much bigger. The strap has a rubber holding and is connected on 2 ends so it can handle the weight. And then they have a genius velcro set up so that unlike the dual elastic strap mechanism many cases use to hold the switch which tugs at your joystick every time you try to get it out, you just unlock the first velcro strap to open the case, and then theres a little velcro strap under the Switch you tug to easily remove it.

Other cases should be embarrassed to be asking for more than $5 if this case is $13 as it is when I bought it. That's how much better this case is. If you want a stand, my recommendation, just buy the stand. No need to look further when it comes to Switch cases unless you want one that can fit a dock and joy-con grip and the whole deal.
rating 4.0. Thumbs up I love the case but when it comes to putting joy cons in the zipper pouch you may struggle some and the analogs catch the net. Overall very nice
rating 2.0. Nice case but the only problem is that had a ... Nice case but the only problem is that had a really bad odor like a cigar , that is the reason I returned it. Never the odor went away

rating 5.0. Nice and roomy! The carry case did come with a distinct smell to it, which reminded me of cabbage that had gone bad.  It wasn't a deal breaker as the carry case itself was just as it was described in the product description. I love the velcro straps that allow you strap in the switch to the case itself and also the other strap on top of it to help you get it out when you're ready to play.  The zippered pocket is big enough to hold an extra set of joy-con, stand, and travel charger and still close easily.  As for the smell, I let it air out during the days where I didn't need to bring the switch with me and the smell disappeared.

rating 1.0. Will be buying a better case elsewhere Not really impressed. The treads are already coming apart and I just opened it. One under the zipper and another long one on the outside. When I place items inside that metal gets pushed out and can potentially reach my switch. Will be buying a better case elsewhere.

Once I opened the actual box this came in the smell over came the entire room. I figured it would go away but 3 days later the case stinks and the room its in smells too. I did put my switch in it and that fit great but once you add the power cable additional controllers and games you couldn't zip it up. rating 2.0. There's got to be something better than this it works, but it's not my ideal case. here's a couple of my issues:

- it doesn't fit a switch well when you have the fat joycon grips or a skin/case protector on the system. yes, you can squeeze it in there, but a better fit would be...better.
- there's no padding to absorb the shock if it's dropped.
- the game flaps will probably go unused since I'm downloading all my games (like I'm sure most people are). they kind of just get in the way.
- the net pocket at the top doesn't look like it will fit a stand and cable and thumb grips and my joycon SR/SL attachment things and earbuds very well. (standard, on-the-road stuff, right?)
- I doubt I'll get all that AND another pair of joycons in this thing.

so I mean, it works. but it's not well thought. and I don't think I'd be ok dropping this case with my system in it on the ground.

actually I'm docking another point just for that. i should be comfortable dropping this. 2*

rating 5.0. Great case! Fantastic case, very sturdy. The design is simple yet well thought out: the Velcro to secure the Switch is both easy to use, holds the device firmly in place, and is easy to remove (far easier/better than having to slide the Switch through elastic bands). Also, I definitely appreciate the attention to detail inside: the fact that this is one of the ONLY cases with a soft internal zipper is reason enough to buy out, I have friends who have had zippers scratch their joy-cons, but that is not an issue here at all.

The screen itself is protected by the flaps where the games are stored so its not going to get scratched but Im still staying on the side of caution for the charger and carrying it separately.

Overall the above issue is not a problem for me. I bought the case as a transport for the switch itself, and very rarely if ever would I travel with just the case anyway so the charger can be housed separately and this issue avoided.

Very happy with my purchase!

rating 5.0. Best Game Console Case yet! Best game case ever! Fits the switch nice and snug. I love the extra pockets where I put my other two joy cons and charging cable. Great bang for the buck, would recommend it to anyone!

The only Con -
I feel is the pocket portion (in the pic it shows the joy cons and power cable). It does seem to be enough room to hold all of it without any issues. I just feel uncomfortable putting in the power cable and such due to the bulkiness presses against the Switch screen (even though the Switch is protected by two layers of flaps that hold the game cartridges. when closing the case, you can feel bulge -
I would wish the case when expand further when closed. There is some expansion but i feel there could be more. I'm just afraid the contents of the pockets will cause too much pressure pressing up against the Switch screen.. hopefully my screenshots explain my theory.

Also - i wish the opening of the zipper was towards the top or bottom, its easier to slide things in - I can't get my stand to fit in this because of it heck, i would even take a diagonal opening

rating 4.0. Good case, but not large enough for stock charger The case is good, but I don't have a spare charger for the switch and the original one does not fit in the case.  I suppose that is on me for not reading the description close enough.  Its still a disappointment though.

rating 1.0. A shady company. Didn't come with the glass protector it originally displayed back when I bought the item. Not big enough for any usb adapter power brick that can power the Switch.

When I first left my review, the seller reached out to me and offered me both a refund and a free replacement case, a new one they were working on that was going to be larger. I obliged, and in return agreed to fix my review to a 5-star. The seller never sent me that replacement and I almost forgot all about it.

But I wanted to revise my review now, so that people know this seller is not one to be trusted. I'm ashamed that I sold out on my review for the possibility of a free case. But I would recommend that you put no trust in any reviews by this seller's products. How many other 5-star reviews were bought off?

rating 4.0. Buy it! If you're debating, just buy it. It's a very nice hard sided case, with plenty of room for games, extra controllers, and your charger. The box it came in says if it does smell to wait a few days for it to air out and it will go away.

rating 4.0. Smaller than it might look I want to give this 3.5 stars but can’t. I got this case after Christmas and was just looking for something I could carry some games my switch and a charger in. Given that’s all I had In mind for use I was A tad upset that it was a tight squeeze but it works for its purpose. rating 5.0. Great product and great customer service Great case. I feel totally safe keeping my Switch in it.  The company is very nice and are always there to answer questions should any arise. Would highly recommend!
rating 1.0. These this real bad smell on the case These this real bad smell on the case. It smells like its been by a thousand campfires, i think it's from the dye.

rating 3.0. Great case, awful smell This product is pretty great! The fit is nice and snug, but doesn’t seem to be too tight anywhere. There is a great flap that comes down to protect your screen and a Velcro piece that holds the switch in place. No matter which way you place your Switch in the case, there is room for the Joy-Con triggers / bumpers. The zipper is made of nylon but still uses a half-moon iron piece but so far, no damage to the Joy-Cons themselves.

If there is anything wrong with this case, it is the smell. When I first opened the box, an odor akin to old lettuce and burnt / smoky plastic was coming from inside. After reading other reviews, I have learned that the odor comes from the adhesive glue that the manufacturer uses to hold everything together. Hopefully, the manufacturer can find a less potent-smelling adhesive, in the meantime, this bad boy is sitting on my windowsill airing out.

current_product_reviews:
rating 5.0. Great case that protects and stores well ! I kept reading this case and another one were the best to get. After getting the runaround from the other one I looked at this one and I found that this had all the same features but it looked better too.

Been using it since christmas and it's a great case. took it on a trip and it worked out very well. Enough room for 4 joycons total, the wrist straps for all four, a samsung galaxy s9 charger and charging cable, the switch itself and if we owned more than one physical game, it would have housed all the game cartridges too. Nothing felt cramped or compromising, it really felt like a solid unit.

I want to point out though that the form-fitting indentations for the switch + attached joycons makes it nigh impossible to have any sort of switch body protector applied and stored with it in the case, which is massively disappointing, but you get trade-offs when you don't want to carry around a huge and bulky case.

I also wish a shoulder strap was an option, maybe hooks for it or something. sometimes it's a bit of a pain to only have the hand strap

input:
rating 2.0. They don't really fit. So when you go to put these on your Rift S Touch controllers, They're form fitted to the shape of the handle, except if you actually put them fully on, you permanently cover the side "grip" button. In essence, this doesn't fit. I have mine halfway on now, with empty space in the bottom part, constantly worried that if it shifts up just a little too much it's going to force me to be holding down a key mid-game.
Second, the material isn't comfortable at all. It feels like cheap plastic, not even rubber, so regardless of it having "grip" texture on the side, it's slippery and doesn't feel good at all on your hand. The only stars I want to give this are for the "AMVR GRIP"s on the side. They're basically just an elastic strap that works to strap your hand to the controller side. It lets me let go of my controller and not worry about it falling, or the stock wrist straps having come loose, etc. Those are really nice and I enjoy those. So I leave these on for that alone.

rating 1.0. The grips fit and feel nice but my only problem is that it blocks sensors. my only problem is that it seems to be blocking some sensors on my Quest 2. From looking at other reviews, others are also experiencing issues with these grips not being compatible in some way or another.
rating 1.0. Really sick of being mailed garbage. Ergonomic/attachments are important in VR and I am willing to pay well for it, these companies flooding the market with garbage need to be stopped.

rating 1.0. Not made for Oculus 2 Do not buy these grips for the Oculus Quest 2. You will be returning them and then waiting for a new pair to come in. I was having to “FORCE” these grips down the handles to get them to slide into place. There was no “fitting snuggly” — I felt as if I was going to break the handles if I kept trying to force these on! Easy to remove later?? NO— do not buy these, you will regret for the Oculus Quest 2.

rating 1.0. Does NOT fit Quest 2 controllers Shame on vendor.  These controllers in now ay fit the Quest 2 controllers  Not even close.  Can only get it 2/3 of the way inserted.. These do not fit the quest 2.  Do not purchase.  that said these look to be decent grips if only they would fit.  Am returning.  Will look elsewhere.

rating 3.0. Bad ergonomics, don't believe the hype Original review -
I've purchased these a week ago for my Rift S
The good reviews and wanting to be able to have index controllers-like feel, I've pulled the trigger on these (not literally speaking, because it's so uncomfortable to actually pull the trigger on my controllers with these things on!)
I have medium sized hands, and with both the medium or large setting - I'm having the same discomfort, which is not having my fingers aligning correctly to the Rift S controller's buttons

Also, after about an hour - my hands went numb due to limited blood circulation as a result of the straps

Will give this another shot but most likely will return these, unless I'm able to find a sweet spot that works.

Revised review -
I've given this another shot. It's not as bad.
I don't think it's perfect, but using it diagonally on my hands, I seem to get this to fit snuggly and have comfortable access to all the buttons and triggers

rating 1.0. Far from the ideal solution Hard to fit and a lot of preassure is needed. It is not made for quest 2 controllers. You can use it but it is not the ideal solution.

rating 5.0. Vastly superior to Kiwi knuckle straps BLUF: I tried this and Kiwi's as affordable knuckle-style controller straps. These were a better fit and better value. Highly recommended as the sweet spot of price to performance as of Feb 2020.

PROS:
-Straps are adjustable to hand size. S-M-L.
-Design holds the controller in the right position in my palm so my thumbs and fingers easily reach the trigger and buttons (Not the case with Kiwi knuckle straps).
-Set includes red, blue, and 2 black straps. I found color coding my right controller (red) and left (blue) was convenient.
-Straps have an additional silicone material on the inside for additional grip to my hand, feels very secure.
-Strap tension system is adjustable one-handed (Not the case with Kiwi knuckle strips).
-Strap design permits using the OEM wrist straps. When I want a free hand, I can let the controller dangle.
-TPU cup adds to controller size (length and width). Fills my hand better.

CONS:
-TPU cup adds to controller size (length and width). Won't fit in most travel cases now.
-The design of the TPU cup and velcro strap is inconvenient for battery swaps (Kiwi was superior in this regard).
-Set includes a silicone nose gasket (?) or flap (?) that helps reduce light bleed around the nose. I was not able to make it work with the OEM interface. Maybe it's designed for AMVR's interface?

It feels horrible and so should this company. I would give this company 0 stars, but I dont think they will exist after 2020 😢😢😢

rating 2.0. Not a good solution. Doesn't fit the new touch controllers well.  Seems more designed for the older Rift controllers.  Makes it odd to hold the controller.  Bands line up over the finders just past the knuckles. Light blocker and lens cover works fine.

rating 4.0. Very Functional, not entirely perfect While its functionality was immediately noticed after putting it on, its not quite perfect - but all in all very recommended and good for its price.

The good:
* Drastically improve game-play, and reduces wrist fatigue, since you no longer need to hold the small controllers so tightly.
* It also prevents the battery Cover to pop off, which is really annoying when playing intensive games like beat saber.
* Has overall premium feel - the Silicone is noticeably of high quality, and it doesn't catch sweat.
* Comes with extra straps, extra Velcros, and the lens protection cover which is very useful (still havent found how to use the nose pad)
* Very easy to disassemble and assemble - although the Velcros do get stuck on the straps which is annoying but negligible.

My complaints:
* if you have large hands, the way the strap wraps around the back of your palm might not feel too natural,
It feels like its tugging a bit on from the bottom.
* the bottom part doesn't align well with the pinky and ring fingers, the grooves feel kind of out of place.
* leaves a bit of an empty space from the bottom, which in turns leaves space to push it inwards sometimes. this is not a big deal since the silicone is rather sturdy - personally i dont like the empty space.

rating 2.0. Awkward and uncomfortable I can see why some people might like this. For me, it was just a really awkward fit that made it difficult to actually access the buttons comfortably.  Not a fan of it.  Regret purchasing them.

rating 5.0. AMVR is the Gold Standard for VR accessories. The best grips out there for the Quest and Rift S. These grips for the Oculus Quest and Rift S are a must-buy! I have tried cheaper silicone/rubber versions and did not have a good experience. However, with the AMVR Touch controller grips, I can play comfortably without being annoyed at the short length of the handle on the Touch controller and have the bonus ability to open and shut my hand at will. This should be your first purchase after getting an Oculus Quest or Rift S, especially if you have large hands like myself. I can't imagine playing fast-paced games like Robo Recall, Creed, Beat Saber, etc. without these again. It's a very quick experience placing these on with the provided velcro straps. And the color options they give you to mix and match the band around the top part of your hand is a very nice touch (red, blue, white). There are competitors out there with similar options, but no question this is the best I've seen thus far for the money and overall experience.

rating 4.0. QUEST 2 - Functional Grips that *Mostly* Fit First things first, I bought these AMVR Grips to use with the Oculus Quest 2. I have spent the past week using them, and have to say I am happy with my purchase. While playing games on the Quest 2, especially those where you have to throw something (EchoVR), I found myself gripping the controllers too hard and sliding the battery cover out. This happened many times within the first week of having the Quest 2, and I was getting tired of it. I finally stumbled across these AMVR Grips, and they have been great. In this review, I will talk about the design (specifically for Oculus Quest 2), how they have been working for me, and my recommendations.

First, the design. In the pictures, these grips look like a soft, grippy, silicone-like substance. I probably should have read some more reviews or looked more into the grips, as they are actually a harder plastic. But, that is definitely not a bad thing. I cannot see these breaking any time soon, and it also gives me greater peace-of-mind because I feel like they will better protect my Touch Controllers. I also did not really care about the knuckle straps when buying the grips, but, I have to say, that is my favorite part of these grips. They are comfortable, they look stylish, and they keep the controllers firmly in your hand. I do not use the regular straps anymore because I highly trust the knuckle straps. For those wondering, there is a hole in the bottom of the grip to put the wrist straps through, so you do not have to remove them.

There are two negative aspects of the design, however, one of which is specifically because I am using them with the Quest 2. First, the top part of the knuckle strap (along the ring) is right next to my joycons, causing my thumb to brush against it whenever I move the joycon. No, this is not a big deal at all, but it is just there and I wanted to point it out. Next, this part is specifically for the QUEST 2 - While these grips fit, they do not fit perfectly. It was a little bit of a hassle trying to put these grips on because they seem just a bit too small. I understand this completely - I do not believe these grips were made specifically for the Quest 2 in mind. They do, however, fit just enough. As others have said, the bottom of the grip does not make contact with the bottom of the controller, but, in all reality, that does not matter too much. I am dreading having to take these off to change the batteries.

As I have previously said, I really enjoy these grips. They feel great playing FPS games, such as Onward and Population: One, and I do not feel like my gameplay is any different from before. When playing games like EchoVR, I do not worry anymore about sliding the battery cover off every time I throw the frisbee. The knuckle straps are comfortable, and I know that the controllers will not come flying out of my hands. Even though the grips are made of a hard plastic, they are comfortable enough to hold. The actual grippy part on the sides are also nice, and they do help wick away sweat.

Finally, I would recommend purchasing these grips. They are not too difficult to install, look great, and perform just as advertised. Even though the two design issues I stated earlier are annoyances, they should not deter you from buying these. I am giving these grips 4/5 stars because of the design issues. Even though they are marketed for the Oculus Quest 2, they do not fit perfectly, but fit just enough.

rating 5.0. A+ For Everything Let me start off by saying that I've been using the Mamut Grips (similar style grips) since I got my quest a few months ago. And I enjoyed them a lot. HOWEVER, since I have tried these AMVR Grips out, the Mamut Grips aren't that good anymore. You compromised a little comfort with the Mamut Grips. They also had these shoelace style that wrapped around your hand. Now the AMVR Grips are much more comfortable in the hand. And the elastic band instead of shoelaces is a big improvement too. Much more comfortable and easier to adjust. You only have one thing to pull to adjust it and you can do it fairly quickly. The Mamut Grips have the 2 laces. So you have to either grip both laces very firmly with a controller in your hand (which wasn't easy), or pull one lace at a time until it was right enough. Anyway, these grips were super simple to install and are a great quest accessory. These are now permanently part of my Quest setup. Definitely give these a try!

Ultimately there are Three problems, two are the fault of the manufacturer: 1) The strap that should go over the back of your hand really only goes over your knuckles or is in front of your knuckles unless you hold the controllers in an odd way. So really there's not much benefit. 2) The grip is not very grippy, it's smooth-ish plastic, not as smooth as the bare controller, but nothing like a rubber feeling. 3) When the grips are on, the controllers don't fit into most cases on the market. Most cases have very snug cut-outs for the controllers and these grips extend both length and girth.

rating 2.0. Poor Ergonomics w/ Design Flaws I'm just going to break the ice and be the small percent that hates this product. To add some info, I used silicone grips previously until they started to stretch and get loose on my oculus rift s controllers.

First off if you have small-ish hands, dont buy these. As you can see in the photos, my hands dont line up at all with the "ergo pattern". The bottom is just too far down to even hold the controller the way it's intended with these grips which brings me to my next point. I would presume 90% of people's ring fingers are longer than their pinkies... why is the ergo pattern made as if your pinky is longer? It feels terrible. The only thing I can give a thumbs up on is the straps and build quality. The spring clips are a little janky but the grips themselves are solid. The straps are also just perfect as far as design goes. With all that being said, I did triple check that I put these grips on my controllers properly and also consulted an avid VR user, also a friend, who lives by these and loves them. After trying to use these for a few hours, I find myself enjoying the base controller way more.

TLDR: Poor ergonomic design that drastically hinders comfort, not made for small-ish hands, A+ sturdiness, A+ straps. I will be returning these.

rating 1.0. Does NOT fit Quest 2 correctly I'm just here to echo other reviewers by saying that these are NOT for 3rd gen Touch controllers (Oculus Quest 2). The Quest 2 controllers have a slightly bigger and different shape. They also drastically change your grip position on the controller and not in a good way; the ergonomics are bad. If you've never used grips before, you might think they're fine, but if you've held a controller with grips that were actually designed for that controller, they're awful. rating 4.0. Updated Design Solves a Few Older Issues People knocking the product for strap slip during usage have been addressed. The updated elastic straps now have rubber stripes across them where they slide through the spring "lock" on the bottom of the controller, which helps some, but they will still slip unless you use the additional pair of spring locks that are now included, as described in the updated instructions. With dual spring locks on each strap, they do not slip at all now...at least not before I felt I was going to rip the strap. With a single spring lock they do slip a little if you tug enough.

Otherwise this is the best grip design currently on the market IMO. I have tried several, was previously using the Kiwi straps, but they all jam my long-ish fingers/thumb up onto the frustratingly short controller so that it's difficult to use my thumb to move the thumbsticks.

These grips add a bit of extension to the bottom, although I would really appreciate even more of an extended grip, because these still push my thumb up a bit more than I'd like. Better than any other strap setup so far though. I may try inserting a bit of a spacer between the grip and handle so that they are forced down but should still stay on due to the velcro strap wrapped around the ring up top.

I would also like to see the tab with all of the slots for the straps located further back toward the wrist. As it is, it still tries to lay the strap down my knuckles, which is of course the high point of a closed hand, so then it drops off of the front of the knuckles toward my finger tips, and I have to force it back over the knuckles to lay across the back of my hand like I feel it should.

One suggestion for locating the straps: I would argue that the directions indicating which holes to use based on hand size are reversed. If you have larger hands/longer fingers/thumbs, then you will need the strap located in the lower hole to position the controller further up in your grip, and keep your thumb from hanging over the top of the joysticks. Likewise, if you have smaller hands/shorter fingers/thumbs, you will want to use the upper hole to keep the controller further down in your hand, allowing your thumb to reach the joystick without stretching.

On a final note, to the one negative reviewer complaining of things like the controller potentially flying out of his hand, you are either not capable of following instructions, or an obvious shill for a competing product. If the velcro strap is installed correctly, there is no physical way for the controller to slide out of the grips.

rating 2.0. Great idea, but two big downfalls. They do what they're meant to do. They hold the backs of your hands so that you can let go of your controller without it leaving your palms-- like the Valve Index. Unfortunately, that's all they've got going for them.
The grips hold your hands right beneath your knuckles, and unfortunately, because of the size of the touch controllers, the tips of your thumbs go way over the thumbsticks and buttons, so you can't press them as easy as you normally would (unless you have really short thumbs). Instead of pressing the buttons with your thumb tips, you'll be pressing them with the creases of your thumbs.
The grips also block the part of the controller that tracks your ring and pinky fingers, giving you a permanent finger gun for as long as you have the grips on.
These things are also pretty difficult to assemble the first time, but after that, they're pretty easy. And I say "the first time" because you're going to be taking them off A LOT.
If you're at all experienced with the Rift S or Quest, you'd know what the touch controllers practically eat batteries. If you play VR about 3-4 hours a day for two or three days, you'll be changing your batteries once a week. That being said, the compartment on the controller in which you change the batteries is blocked by the AMVR grips, and every time you need to replace the batteries, you have to completely take off the grips to get to the compartment. This is something that can't be avoided, both on the manufacturer's part and the consumer's part. It's just something that you'll have to deal with if you buy these grips.
So overall, these things do their job, but there's a LOT of things that you'd have to either get used to or see past in order to get a truly enjoyable experience out of playing VR with these grips on.

rating 2.0. Bands Don't Stay Tight I bought these after seeing a good review and hoped they would improve the ergonomics of my Oculus Quest controllers. Installation was a little difficult due to the poor instructions; I had to find a video online to make sure I was installing them correctly. I have medium-size hands, and the extra bulk of these repositions my hand slightly so some of the controller buttons are not as easy to reach. The biggest problem I have with them though is that the bands that wrap around the back of your hand are not securely held in place. They feel good when you first put them on and tighten them up, but over the course of a few minutes they slowly get loose and then you have to fumble around trying to tighten them back up. It's really annoying. If they improved the clamp mechanism so the bands stayed put, I would give this four stars.

rating 2.0. Not great for the Quest 2 Yes, the grips can technically be used on the Quest 2, however I had a few issues with it after about a week of testing. The grips are too small and do not fully slide onto the controllers, which causes them to sit lower on the hand than intended. Pushing the grips in where they're snug but not tight works okay, but I find that they slide a bit when throwing objects quickly. Pushing the grips on tightly pushes against the battery door, which (for me) caused the door to slide off when removing the grips. I would be worried about accidentally breaking part of the door using the grips long term.

Otherwise, the comfort is okay. The elastic straps feel nice on the hands, but I struggled to get them secure. Tightening the straps to a snug point still causes the controllers to wobble, so throwing isn't as comfortable as I'd like. Tightening the straps more helps but can cut off circulation in the hand. I still liked using the grips (and missed them when taking them off,) but will try a more secure version designed for the Quest 2.

rating 3.0. Good when it works, but could give more headaches then solutions. Highs: Controller feels sturdy and looks sturdy

Controller fits well for smaller/middle sized hands
Joy sticks are light to touch
XYBA buttons are somewhat sturdy yet light

Lows: D pad is mushy, making it hard to play fighting games with
Getting gamepad to work could be problematic and confusing
R1+L1 triggers feel flimsy

Questionable's: I had a problem where my left stick trigger wouldn't hit peak axis points to its top left and bottom left so when certain games require it to reach a certain peak it doesn't register. Like from walking to running. The left stick would just walk and not run. i'm not sure if its just a malfunction on the sticks but I looked at the calibration and confirmed that was the problem. I had to increase sensitivity through emulation for it to work properly.

Tips: When trying to get it to connect to (PC) try enabling the controller to the right setting
before connecting the usb to your PC. I had to hold the home button+X and let the red
LED blink before connecting, then my Windows 7 recognized it.

Conclusion: The gamepad is good for the money and works well when it does. I haven't tried the bluetooth only because I
didn't see the point for me at least, because of the problems it has for me is a no no. But, it shows so much
promise I hope they would better implement those areas I've said and it would easily crush its opponents in its
field.

rating 5.0. Best Lego game on the 3DS! Ok, so my 5 year old son loves Lego games.  He plays Lego Indiana Jones on the Xbox but usually has trouble with some of the puzzles so I usually join him to help beat the levels.  I eventually bought him Lego Indiana Jones 2 DS version but forget it, controls were clunky and puzzles were harder.  Then I saw this game was going to be released soon so I kept in on my radar.  I eventually downloaded the demo and thought it played very well so I decided it would be worth the purchase.  My son has been playing it and I can gladly say he is enjoying.  The controls are smooth and he's is plowing through the levels with ease.  The puzzle sequences are so well put together to where they're not too hard but yet it gives him a challenge.  It almost walks him through it.  For example: When he completes a puzzle, coins will appear and show him where to jump or which direction to go.  The level design is great along with the gameplay.  I do think the handheld version is geared more towards the younger crowd but it doesn't mean its not a good game because it is.  I often have to stop myself from snatching the 3DS from my son.  :)

rating 5.0. Upgrade from the P21's!!! I had purchased the P21's which only worked for the PS3 back in Christmas time last year and LOVED them.  I also have an XBOX 360 and was a little upset that they were coming out with a newer version (PX21's) that also worked for the XBOX, especially after paying the same price.  But I guess I should have done more research.  Anyways, I ended up selling my P21's and put that $ down towards buying these.  And I'm not dissapointed one bit.  They worked perfectly on both systems and gotta say that the sound was somewhat better on these.  Seemed like the sound cues were more defined on the PX21's than on my old headset.  Another plus the longer cords.  Now you can actually sit a distance away without any tugging.  All in all, I'm pretty happy with the upgrade.

rating 5.0. Great Game (helpful Reviewer) As far as first person shooters go, this is top notch.
Story: Keeps you engaged the whole time and on a roller coaster of action throughout the game. It is thrilling at parts where you don't know what will be coming at you next. (invisible elites, etc.) This is also expanding the Halo story line since the war is over after the third installment, they try to continue the rivalry with the covenant through a rebel faction and introduce forerunners to player to bring more intrigue.

Replay: Very high: Not only is the story replay-able with your friends, to keep it interesting, the multi-player has tons of options that can afford hours of great fun. If you are online you can continue the story with Spartan-ops, which is like a whole new game. I loved this new addition, even if they got rid of firefight, this was really great to see well made cinematic episodes.

I have heard many people attack this game because it does not live up to other such expectations previously thought of, but you are poorly mistaken because this is a game that can stand on its own. Yes it helps a lot to know the previous story line, but this introduces one of its own and is a great game. I picked up this one as a second buy because I wanted to have LAN parties with friends and get all the new maps/skins for 25 dollars.

rating 5.0. LOVE THE WAY THE GAME LOOKED AND HOW THE GAME ... A LITTLE BIT MORE GOING ON. A FEW DLC'S MADE IT A LITTLE DIFFERENT. LOVE THE WAY THE GAME LOOKED AND HOW THE GAME MOVED.GOOD GAME.
rating 1.0. broken case havent played the game yet because im gonna get it replaced. the inside of the game case it broken. probably fedexs fault

rating 3.0. Kind of Boring This is the same game you've played before, as far as campaign mode goes. It's actually disappointingly boring. I think the full title of this game should be "Halo 4: Looking for Ammo." You pretty much have to use an entire clip to take out one alien, and then you spend the next five minutes back tracking to see if there were any guns laying around. It's not much fun looking around to see which plasma pistol has the most battery life left. They even made driving vehicles less fun. How do you make flying around in a spaceship with lasers not fun? Also, a lot of people have said how spot-on the sound design was, but the Warthog sound effects are laughably bad. It sounds like they did an engine swap with an old go cart. The graphics are nice though. People look pretty realistic, so that's kind of neat. Anyway, this is essentially the same as Halo 1 in terms of single player game play.Nothing new or innovative except that it looks prettier.

rating 3.0. A different brand of Halo. How not to make a sequel to an iconic franchise. vol. 1

Presentation: 7/10 How do you continue the story to a highly successful franchise that over a ten year span, has revolutionized it's genre by creating iconic characters and innovative gameplay? You just write up a sequel. Halo 4 answers that age old question "What happens next?" and does it while leaving us with more questions than answers. Questioning not just the reason for Halo 4 but the very future of the Halo franchise.

Gameplay: 8/10 Halo 4 is using the Reach engine and it's apparent the minute the Master Chief awakes from his 5 year nap. There is no duel wielding, fall damage is back, and now sprinting is a natural ability. The game justifies these changes by telling us right away that Cortana, the main characters blue A.I. friend, has updated his suit. This explains the new HUD but why our main characters iconic armor looks radically different is never explained. Purists will say that everything is explained in the expanded universe of books, and other content but it is no explanation is present in the game. Needles to say this alienates a good chunk of the audience that has been fallowing the games and only the games from Combat Evolved to Reach. It just doesn't make sense to the casual player.

In short, the way the game is played feels like a step sideways. There are tough new enemies that feel a bit too tough at times, and never enough ammo. While advances borrowed from today's popular shooters like better hit detection and grenade awareness meters are a nice touch, they still feel completely out of place in this franchise.

Graphics: 8/10 The next Halo game is going to need a new engine. I have no faith that the Reach engine can be polished any further as it's presented as mixed bag of beautiful lighting and texture effects bogged down by pixilated details and graphical anomaly's. You will be presented with a picturesque landscape one moment that almost looks next-gen just to kill an Elite the next moment and realize that up close, these enemies look about as good as they did in Halo 3. That, and the bland barren surfaces of everything forerunner has worn out it's welcome. These are the finer points that bring this other wise beautiful, if not aged, engine down. Oh, I have to mention the pre-rendered cut-scenes. They hold the best graphics I have ever seen in anything, ever. Why they didn't just shoot live action is beyond me because it looks just as good. While they are fun to watch, they are so cryptic in story that you end up not really knowing what you have seen by the end, other than great graphics.

Sound: 9/10 This is where Halo 4 got it right. I'm talking about the sound effects in particular. Every weapon sounds as a weapon should. Powerful and dangerous. Your armor sounds heavy, vehicles have engines now, I mean they sound the way they would sound in reality and in general the sound effects are top notch and better than they have been in any Halo title to date.

I can't say the same for the music. While it is fully orchestrated, original, and good quality, I found it to be forgettable. I have played Halo 4 for just over 40 hours, through the campaign on Legendary three times and I can't hum a single tune from the game. The music at times feels just plain out of place. My best example is the main menu where if I close my eyes I'm sure I'm about to press start on a Final Fantasy title screen.

Value: 8/10 After the 6-8 hour Campaign, you have multiplayer and Spartan Ops which is a feature that essentially replaces Firefight and tells a story that takes place 6 months after the events of Halo 4. That was a mistake on 343's part. It is not customizable, very repetitive, and tells a story that much like the campaign, leaves with more questions than answers. The multiplayer borrows alot form COD. Their is no rhyme or reason to randomly spawned weapons on the battlefield and rather depends on a scorestreak/loadout system. This is not your daddies Halo. I personally feel that while this style is functional, it detracts from the Halo experience. Frankly, it makes me want to go play COD because COD does it better. I am not happy to be saying this. Trust me.

I guess I have to mention Theatre and Forge as they are both in the game but the file share system is all but broken (and never got fixed) so map making is pointless and the theatre only records your most recent multiplayer match. No campaign or Spartan Ops missions are recorded which is a shame when I've grown so used to doing something awesome in campaign and watching/recording it to put in my file share in past games. There are no skulls to collect, terminals you can only view by going to a separate application, Halo Waypoint and multiplayer modes that seem to lack focus in all the places that count.

Overall: 8/10 I'm stuck wondering what went wrong. I mean I guess Halo 3 never needed a sequel but 343i promised so much. They said they had former Bungie employees working for them and that they were planning a new trilogy with new enemies. Coming off of Halo: Reach and Modern Warfare 3 I'm presented with an amalgamation of the two. I'm confused and bewildered that for once "Halo" on the game case doesn't translate to "Buy this game!". I'm faced with the conclusion that for me, Halo was over at Reach.

I was still very into Goldeneye 007 when the first Halo came out but I gradually grew to really enjoy Team Snipers on Zanzibar on a saturday night with friends as I did with Pistols Only on Facility just a few short years prior. In Bungie's Halo games we felt powerful but challenged, small but important. Halo 4 makes us just another Spartan in a sea of Spartans and where Halo: Reach proved that idea could work, Halo 4 just feels like another modern FPS in a sea of modern shooters. It's by no means, a broken game, at it's best it's a solid Halo experience. Perhaps the last one. Halo 4s biggest sin is in that it takes risks where it should play it safe, and plays it safe where innovation and expansion are expected.
