rating 5.0. Well-Polished, Immersive Experience Amazing opening sequence that really draws you into the story. Naughty Dog has always been great at telling a story, and The Last of Us is no exception. The game, as a whole, is great. Premise is believable and gripping, voice acting and dialogue are fantastic, graphics superb, AI is smart, limited weapons/ammo make it challenging, and moments in the game create real tension and creepiness. Story-wise, it opens with a bang, gets cloudy in the middle, and then finishes interestingly enough to satisfy. The Last of Us is certainly a top-notch release and among the best PS3 games.

If you enjoyed [[ASIN:B004FS8LYK Tomb Raider]] and [[ASIN:B005C2D1YI Dishonored]], you'll enjoy this title ... but you will see a lot of reused ideas/mechanics that are sometimes not quite as good as found in either title. Specifically, Tomb Raider's maneuvering (including camera follow/lighting), platforming, variety of gameplay, weapon selection, and firing mechanisms are better than The Last of Us. Dishonored's stealth, silent takedowns, and talent upgrades are a notch above this title, too. With that said The Last of Us is a great combination of the two with a good story that can seem a little drawn out at times -- get to this checkpoint, oh no wait, go to another, oh wait, one more checkpoint. I guess that's how post-apocalyptic travelers would feel, but it certainly slows down the game without really furthering the story.

I also expected more variety in gameplay. One of my favorite parts of Uncharted is when you have to run through a part of the level and make quick decisions. I didn't feel like there was enough of that in The Last of Us. For the most part I was able to deliberately plan ahead before taking each scenario. Maybe another shortfall is that the companion/sidekick character gets in the way a lot, especially when hiding behind objects or going in/out of doorways. Just minor annoyances, really, which do not take away too much from the game.

I have not run into the problem of saving/loading corruption, but my PS3 did freeze up early into the Winter chapter -- luckily, the autosave worked and resumed correctly. Other than an odd splt-second blue screen prior to a cut scene, it's the only problem I ran across. I haven't tried the multiplayer, but I hear great things about it. My favorite PS3 games have been Tomb Raider, the Uncharted series, and Dishonored. Now I can add The Last of Us to the list and look forward to a new, great series from Naughty Dog.

rating 5.0. Valid hype No intention to do an in-depth review. I just want to say that I finished The Last Of Us last night, took me 21 hours, and I thought it was every bit as good as 'they' say. I've never been a huge Naughty Dog fan, frankly I thought the Uncharted games were mildly enjoyable and the gross fanboy hyperbole about them made me roll my eyes pretty hard. But from the first time I saw TLOU over a year ago, it had my attention. I never really pored over the previews, so it was all very fresh to me. Somehow it never really occurred to me that this was going to be survival horror. In recent years I've thought of Dead Space as the last bastion of something even remotely approaching classic survival horror (though it gives you way too much ammo and health items to truly qualify) but now I must hand the championship belt to TLOU. Resident Evil was George Romero style survival horror. Silent Hill was Stephen King/David Lynch style. Dead Space was Ridley Scott/John Carpenter style. And now The Last of Us is Cormac McCarthy/Michael Crichton style survivor horror. It never allows you to amass enough resources to think you can handle absolutely anything that comes your way, yet it's never overly frustrating. It maintains the perfect level of tension. The crafting system forces you to choose between making more first aid kits or molotov cocktails (which are pretty damn handy.) The stealth game play, crouching and sneaking and taking down foes unawares is every bit as fun and satisfying as I thought it would be when I saw the E3 demo last year. The story and characters shine. Troy Baker has surpassed Nolan North in the Is There Any Game This Guy Is *Not* In? category, but he does a terrific job as Joel. One of the few things that I didn't like in the early trailers was that the voice actress who plays Ellie just sounds too B-level, too cartoony (sure enough when I looked her up on IMDB I quickly confirmed she does a lot of cartoon kids shows) but in the finished product she does an excellent job. There are a good number of characters in this game, and I can't think of a weak link in the bunch. The hordes of NPC enemies all seem to be voiced by the same three or four actors, and that can be a tad distracting after a while. By the end of the game I'm pretty sure I'd murdered Fred Tatasciore at least 50 times.

The only criticism I can think of is that the load and save functions are kinda broken. When you turn on the PS3 and boot up the game to start a play session, you might want to have something to read or otherwise keep you occupied, because it takes forever. I'm not talking about the first time you play i.e. one-time system file install, I'm talking about any time you boot. And if you dare to so much as open the save/load menu, you have to sit and wait while it spends an eternity auto-saving, just in case. That's my one and only complaint. At first I thought maybe it was just me, even though I have a PS3 Slim 500GB that's less than two months old and have had no loading issues with any other games... but I've since confirmed with friends that it's the same for them.

Personally I'm not interested in a sequel to this game, I think it's the perfect example of a game that should be allowed to simply stand by itself. But I'll keep a closer and less jaundiced eye on Naughty Dog from now on.

UPDATE: Jan. 26, 2015 - I played through TLOU a few times when it came out, and then about six months ago I played it again when TLOU Remastered was released for PS4. A few days ago I felt like playing it again, and I got all obsessive and started watching Neil Druckmann interviews and reading various people's in-depth analyses. Two things I want to say- I totally agree that the A.I. in the game is clearly inferior to what was shown in the early demos, but I didn't notice that until it was pointed out. I've seen some videos of the A.I. performing some pretty bizarre broken behaviors, but I never saw any of them in any of my play throughs. It seems equally clear to me that the A.I. flaws are generally only apparent if you go out of your way to cause them, as opposed to simply playing the game in the spirit in which it was intended. The second thing I want to say is that I changed my mind about a sequel. I still think another game starring Joel and Ellie would be pointless, but I can totally see a new game with an older Ellie in the dominant role, with a new character (or characters) in tow. Based on what I've heard him say on the matter, it sounds like Druckmann is skeptical that there would be any need for a sequel, and part of me is proud that he agrees with my original 2013 assessment! But the 2015 me hopes he finds some good fertile sequel soil.

rating 5.0. Smart, fun, and rewarding gameplay Tropico is a "build your own island" SimCity-type game with a distinct tongue-in-cheek slant to its delivery. You take charge of a small tropical island as El Presidente, and it's up to you to figure out just how you're going to keep things running smoothly. Will you support the island economy through mining? Farming? Tourism? Will you strive to keep your citizens happy by offering them tax cuts and free housing, or will you just outlaw elections and surround your office with military bases in an attempt to thwart the monthly assassination attempts.

The game's visuals are amazing. Every little detail is animated, and a great deal of time can be spent just "flying" over your island watching people go about their day. You can highlight any citizen and see their job, education, family tree, beliefs, and even their thought process. You may find several people upset that there are no luxury stores around, which lets you know to build a shopping mall that caters to high-end customers. You can also, if so desired, mark a random rebel or criminal to be arrested, assassinated or just to "have an accident".

As beautiful as the game is, its real appeal is deep within the mechanics that keeps everything running. Everything in Tropico is connected in one way or another. Building logging camps is a quick way to boost the economy and provide jobs, but angers environmentalists. Shallow displays of patriotism impresses the uneducated, but bothers the intellectuals. Quite a few times, you'll find that your island dwellers need healthcare and education, but the buildings will remain unoccupied until you find qualified staff to run them. Building a solid base early on is essential to a productive island later on.

The music is catchy and infectious, but is way too short. The soundtrack is an earworm of the worst kind, and it's gotten to the point where I had to mute the music and start playing my own. I really wish there was additional music available for download, because it'd be a necessary purchase after just a few days of gameplay.

rating 5.0. Great Value I have been playing the game for around 5-6 hours now. Game is not that hard, but I am guessing it will be getting a bit more difficult as I play more. It is a really good value for $21 since I got the additional Modern Time add-on. If you enjoy city building games like SimCity then I think you will like this game. It also has a bit of a sense of humor and I like the tropical theme. There are also many extra missions to do along with the campaign. Plus there is a "Sandbox Mode" where you can create your own custom scenario and see if you can win in.

rating 2.0. Many serious bugs; maybe buy something else Tropico 4 is quite fun. It has a wry sense of humor that is surprisingly well informed about the lamentable aspects of Latin American and Caribbean history. The gameplay is interesting and fairly addicting. But that just makes it difficult to tolerate the myriad serious bugs. The main campaign is relatively stable, although you will get assigned tasks that you cannot complete because they require things only available in the expansion pack. Don't try to demolish a salt mine and rebuild it--too risky. Play around too much while placing roads, and it will lock up. The Modern Times expansion pack could have been a nice addition. For the most part it just provides better versions of existing buildings. That would be more fun if you could still use the previous versions of buildings, but as each new building is introduced over time, you can no longer build the old versions.
Modern Times apparently never went through beta testing. It locks up frequently; if you try a game in "sandbox" mode, after couple hours lockups become incessant and corrupted saved games are common. Using either the expansion or the original game, it becomes a slideshow after a while. Apparently the Xbox can't keep up with the simulation and graphic demands, but there's no way to reduce the visual complexity. Playing at a fast speed is asking for it to freeze. From what I gather, the developer has no interest in fixing anything.

input:
rating 5.0. More Than Just an Update: An Overhaul I see a lot of reviewers dismissing MW3 as just a small update, the Madden 12 of FPS so to speak. In my opinion, this is not the case. The fundamentals of the multiplayer have been changed and the story that was started in COD4: MW has finally been resolved.

While the maps seem similar, the weapons are mostly recycled and the graphics haven't gotten an update there are a lot of changes that make this a completely different feel from MW2. The kill streak rewards are the biggest change, and not only are they different, they've completely changed the way a match can play out.

There are now 3 different types of kill streaks: Assault, Support and Specialist. Assault is the standard; you kill, you build, resets when you die. The other two are completely new and have totally changed the feel of the multiplayer experience. Support counts kills over the course of the MATCH. Death no longer stops your kill streak growth bonuses, meaning that you can earn the top tier kill bonus on every match (if you accumulate enough kills). People are no longer punished for a cheap death or a stupid mistake, and can help change the course of the match for their teammates without camping off in the corner hoping people stumble by.

The third type, Specialist, is even more unique. It unlocks perks as you kill. This means that after a few kills you can have MORE perks active instead of only three, making you a far more formidable foe (so long as you know how to USE the perks). Once people figure out the sheer options they have for multiplayer, believe me, you'll be noticing a serious difference.

As for the single player narrative. It's semi-interesting but is a bit rushed. You're after Markarov, he's a bad guy, etc. etc. I just wanted to see the end of the campaign and I got it. Hooray.

The final part of the game, Spec Ops, is more fun than it was in MW2. The missions are a bit more varied and the new Horde mode is a ton of fun (especially if you enjoyed the Gears of War horde mode). It has a lot of varied enemies, from dogs to vehicles and has its own in-game system for upgrades, it's fun, trust me.

SUMMARY - I think this is a worthwhile purchase. There are enough changes throughout to make it into a new take on an old formula. The killstreak changes alone make it so I'll never be going back to MW2 multiplayer. The burning question: is it better than Battlefield 3? It's DIFFERENT. I love Battlefield 3 but Modern Warfare multiplayer isn't designed to be a direct competitor. It's claustrophobic, over the top, and fast. You don't run across the map to kill someone, they're right around the corner.

rating 5.0. King Of First-Person Shooters At 5-7 hours on Normal difficulty, Modern Warfare 3's single player is just as short as you've come to expect - though, with the degree of insanity unfolding from minute to minute, you shouldn't feel cheated. Minus the usual breathless cliffhanger, the ending is a bit anticlimactic - though the final fade-to-black scene is close to perfect.

Pros: Some of the best campaign set pieces in the series. Survival mode and co-op in general is great. Multiplayer changes are well considered and the new game modes work well.

Cons: The last two campaign levels are weak. The impact of Pointstreaks and strike packages will depend entirely on your own skill level.

Conclusion: As substantial as they are the changes won't silence committed critics, but for accessible, content-rich action this is still the best first person shooter of the year.

---
As a diehard Call of Duty'r (I actually wrote for the official unofficial website when the series began), I had to get this. I've played them all, prestiged more than I'd care to admit, but... I'm considering selling this after only a few hours of gameplay.

I no longer own MW2, but I'd wager a guess that the graphics and audio were both better in it. I can't verify and am likely wrong, but the fact of the matter is that it's even a question.

Further regression has occurred in the menu system (my options seem more limited and the UI graphics are straight out of 1998) and the unlock system (I might need a PhD to figure this one out).

The one place I did see any progression is in game speed, which is cool from a tech standpoint, but the gameplay is frantic as hell... it's almost maddening.

The recipe was near perfect with MW2. Whatever they've done with it since, they need to reverse it, keep things simple, and begin making quality games again rather than becoming the Madden of the FPS world with yearly map packs sold as full games.

Skip it and join me on the [[ASIN:B003O6G5TW Battlefield]].

rating 5.0. Let's Rate This for What it Is.. In all honesty, yes, this is very much like the previous COD games; and it baffles me that this fact surprises people. The COD franchise has proven that this is a basic system that works and sells well: Strong multiplayer that has endless replayability that consistently rewards gameplay and a decently fun single player to use when your internet is out.

The fact that people are so taken aback by this and are giving this game low ratings is ridiculous. Yes, this is a typical "new COD" game, but you already knew that. What this game does improve on IMO is that the graphics seem to be an upgrade from Black Ops, and also there is also revamped multiplayer system that has even more different ways to level up and unlock new goodies.

In closing, if you like COD, you will be glad you bought this game. If you didn't like previous COD games, why did you buy this game? After all and for example, I don't like Grand Theft Auto games; why would I want to buy the new one and then complain about it?

rating 2.0. The continual Recreation Being a gamer who has played Call of Duty since its original release I was really expecting a lot out of MW3. After Black Ops came out I said to myself their is no possible way for them to create a worse version of Call of Duty. Well, if you are looking for a version that is a terrible re-creating of MW2 this is your game. The feel of the game is identical to MW2(which I loved) but the maps are absolutely horrific. The layout to most maps only allows a quick, past paced run and gun game play. And since every single map is more or less a maze, you have 90 percent of the players in the game camping corners. Every gun sounds fairly similar, there is no variation. The graphics have not been upgraded in anyway. I don't know if it is unfair to compare the graphics, sound and game play to Battlefield. But spend you money on Battlefield 3, not MW3.

rating 2.0. MW2 basically w/Minor Changes. This game is good. I love the Call of Duty franchise and I love the Battlefield Franchise. I've been playing both since I was a kid.

I can say that both games are good. They are fun.. much fun. Both have their pros and cons. I'm not a hater on CoD or Battlefield.

The thing is with MW3.. It's really just MW2.... nothing changed really with the menu, nothing changed with the guns, nothing changed with the graphics really, nothing changed with the sound effects. It is all the same!!! If you can remember the sound effects of the flashbang and concussion bang and frag grenade dropping, it will ring to your ear as MW2.

It's boring kind of. I was playing it and I felt like I was playing Modern Warfare 2 just with different maps and kill streak settings.

Modern Warfare 3... you are fun. but you aren't new. It's still the same. I think the Modern Warfare era needs to come to an end after this one or the next one.... and I hope if they do make a 4th MW... it's no where near the same as these last 3. If Call of Duty does continue... please make some kind of Colonial game or go back into history of the Civil War, try something new? I have no idea, but the idea of World War I and II is really to old to keep making games around and the Modern era of war fighting games is kind of boring now because its something we see every day on the news or t.v. or in movies.

I'm disappointed in the game pretty much you can say. It's a big big let down from all the hype in my opinion. It's just Modern Warfare 2. ..thats all it is. Just with a few minor changes and small upgrades that aren't noticeable really.

rating 4.0. Campaign is fantastci Just finished up the single player before going to bed last night. This title shows what comes from the experience of doing multiple highly scripted set-piece campaigns, and that is quality. The polish is evident, and while challenging none of my (frequent) deaths felt arbitrary or undeserved.

The story has enough explosions and bombast to make Michael Bay weep with joy, and is full of "F*&$ YEAH!" moments. The pace is intense, significantly more so than the prior games. Also, Infinity Ward & Sledgehammer are some of the few developers out there who seem to understand what it takes to do a compelling and fun chase sequence in a video game without it feeling artificial or forced.

Without spoiling anything, I can tell you that the ending is very satisfying, and well worth the journey.

I'll begin playing the multiplayer and Spec-Ops modes tonight.

rating 5.0. Simply an awesome game! It seems to me that a lot of people just misunderstanding this game. It is an extension of MW2, it is similar to MW2 but it isn't MW2. In my opinion it is because of the success of the previous version of the game. If it isn't broken why fix it? The game's graphics are fantastic, the experience of multiplayer is equally amazing. I am sure that just like with any other games, the bugs will be fixed-if any-, and future updates will make every doubter happy. It is a big bump from the previous version. I feel that a complete overhaul of the game and a drastic change of the dynamic would have ruined the franchise. It is great as is. A highly enjoyable game and a lot of fun for next 12 months until a new one comes out.
So, if you liked MW2 you will love this as well. It will not let you down. If you are a fan of Call Of Duty just ignore the negative comments and go enjoy this fantastic game.

rating 4.0. Still shows it's the best in a crowded field First let me state that if you have played any of the CoD games going back to CoD 4: Modern Warfare, then the game will still be quite familiar to you, there isn't a huge change (thankfully) in the way the game feels or plays. The one reason why the Call of Duty series is still the best in the FPS market is because on one thing....feel. The game still has the best feel as far as control, contact with the ground and game world, and behavior of the gun. While the Battlefield series has its fans, it still is far too floaty and there is way too much sway and play with the way firearms behave.

MW3 still shows that the Call of Duty franchise is the one to beat.

rating 2.0. MW3=MW2 + $60 + Worse Maps For a quick summary of the game, if your willing to pay $60 for Modern Warfare 2, being Nov 2011, then you should consider this game. I agree with many other reviews about how they had made this game all about close combat, for multilayer at least. I enjoy sniping occasionally but it is impossible to utilize a scope in this game. The maps feel clutter, as if they just Activision just threw in random crap all over the place, and there is hardly a time when there is more than 30 yards of free space in one direction. This makes sniping annoying and forces the use of close combat. After playing multiplayer for a couple of hours I, not surprisingly, found that shotguns worked best in every level.

The only thing new about this game other than the cluttery maps is a new mutliplayer game mode called Kill Confirmed. In this mode after you kill someone you have to get their dog tag, which is over their body to be added to your teams score. You can also retrieve you teammates dog tags also so the rival team doesn't get the kill.

I would rather buy MW2 for $20 than MW3 for $60.

rating 5.0. Best campaign in the series Don't believe everything you read. Simply try the game out for yourself and make up your own mind. MW3's campaign was the most exciting campaign in the Modern Warfare series. While the characters are the same (it is a trilogy), the gameplay mechanics and styles varied on every level. You were a ground soldier, sniper, pilot, AC-130 gunner, helicopter gunner, etc. alternating each mission, so it never got stale. I highly recommend it. I'm not even going to go into multiplayer as you know the the basics of that. With nearly 20 modes and over a dozen new maps, you will be playing this for a long time.

rating 3.0. Great game, just not totally awesome like MW2 Maybe I'm just so numb to these games now but I didn't really see/feel any big changes to the way the game was played. And it's a pretty easy game to play through in story mode, even on Veteran. Took me about 6 hours to beat it on Veteran. I'm looking more forward to the multiplayer more than anything else though, which is the primary reason why I bought the game.

The game continues with the storyline from the last MW which was absolutely amazing. I loved the story, the epic airport play, etc. and this release is just a continuation of the story.

If you're not looking for big changes from MW2, this is the game for you. Otherwise, you might be somewhat disappointed in it.

3/5 for recycling old stuff.

rating 5.0. Initial Review I've only played this game for a few hours, so please excuse the lack of in-depth knowledge that I will ascertain from playing furthermore. I am just writing an initial review for the game based on what I have played so far.

So far I like what I see. I honestly didn't expect too much more then MW2 because common, how advanced can the graphics get and how "futuristic" can you get with a game thats entitled MODERN WARFARE. If you get any more into it you might as well be going sci-fi because the weapons of today haven't changed much since the last game.

The graphics are good, and maybe a tad bit better then MW2, but honestly how much further can we go without a brand new gaming platform? I don't think there's any more "HD" you can go without playing this on a PS3, and thats even pushing it. If you want something that is going to blow you completely away in regards to graphics then wait for the next "gaming consol of tomorrow" because honestly we are at the platforms graphic capacity (NOT the WiiU because that won't be graphic intensive).

The campaign is a continuation of the first two games, so if you haven't played them then you probably should. I got lucky and jumped into the 2nd modern warfare without playing the 1st, but was still a tad lost; but they really don't try to brief you on anything that happened before hand in this game. Its either you know or you don't. So far playing on regular it isn't that difficult, but I'm only doing it on regular so I can probably bet that when I up the difficulty, it will most certainly become harder. I do enjoy how your team mates kill the enemies and really do try to push the objective instead of "blindly firing" (and by this i mean attempting to fire at the enemy but always missing) as they have in past games.

The guns are pretty much the same with a few new guns and additions. There's a lot of complaints about this but honestly what do you expect? It's like I said before, if they tried to make up a gun it would cross the line of being a modern game about modern warfare into sci-fi and then you would blast it even further so I like where it's at now. The guns are more customizible and now you can even level up your weapons so it encourages you to favor one weapon over others. This can be good or bad but I prefer it because I like to master one gun and then move to the next.

Multiplayer always has its quirks, but thats common over almost every game out there (Halo, MW, MW1 etc). Right now people that have no life are the highest levels, but for the common joe like me with a job and a life only play when we want, and seemed to get paird up with equals, and even if you are jesus of the video game world, you'll start off as the lowest level until you rank up so just wait a week or so and people will be probably about at their rankings as they were in MW2. That Jesus of the video game who owned you last week will be a completely different level by then and you'll probably never face him again, its all part of playing a brand new game right at launch.

It's fairly easy to level up but i did find it a little difficult with the three profiles you are given at the start because they are so limited. It's really not a big deal though and you'll quickly be able to customize your own profile once you level up which they have made pretty easy.

For those that complain that there are little children playing cussing, singing and playing loud music...you'll never get away from it. There will always be an annoying little brat that thinks he's better then you, calling you a fa* and probably touching themselves. Its the nature of the game and really any game and shouldn't be factored into a review of the game itself. If you don't want to be bothered by that sort of thing then don't play multiplayer and just play with your friends on split screen.

I will update this as I find out and play more of the game, cause honestly there's not much I can put having only played about 4-5 hours worth after work. Depending what I find out I will certainly keep the score or downgrade, so keep a look out this is only a preliminary review.

*EDIT*
So I've played quite a bit of this game now and I do not wish to change any of my previous statements. This game is fun, the graphics are of good quality and basically everything else I mentioned. Have fun!

rating 5.0. I'm finally proud to play Call of Duty again Having joined the Call of Duty franchise after Call of Duty 2, I must say that I did not become a true die-hard fan until Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare came out. If your only experience with Call of Duty is from Call of Duty: World at War, Modern Warfare 2, or Black Ops, I urge you to turn them all in to Game Stop and purchase Call of Duty 4. Up until about yesterday when the UPS guy dropped off MW3, I would have contended that Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare was the absolute greatest FPS in gaming history, if not the best overall game.

My dismay first appeared with Treyarch's World at War. The anticipation for me for this game was huge. I was incredibly excited. After playing it, however, I realized that it was nowhere near as good as Call of Duty 4. You see, what I didn't realize was that the only thing I wanted was perhaps a graphically updated, WWII skinned Call of Duty 4 with the same guns, perks, and killstreaks simply modified to fit the WWII theme. That did not happen, as Treyarch made their own game with their own spin on Call of Duty -- a spin I hated, perhaps only because it was in comparison with the greatest FPS in gaming history.

After being disappointed by World at War, I went back to Call of Duty 4. That was until MW2 was released. Again, I was incredibly excited about this game. After playing it -- while I must admit that I certainly enjoyed it -- I again realized that all I wanted was a graphically updated Call of Duty 4 with perhaps a few small changes. It must be noted that there were also dozens of problems and flaws in the game that were never resolved, making the game horrible imbalanced.

After that, Black Ops. Due to being produced by Treyarch, I did not even care. I bought it a few days after release simply because a few buddies of mine intended on playing. I got to max level in multiplayer (did not prestige) and went back to Call of Duty 4.

And finally, MW3. At first I didn't care. After reading more into it, I became excited again. My excitement has paid off. I am extremely happy with this game. Weapons, perks, maps... the only thing that could be potentially better would be an updated version in graphics from Call of Duty 4. Even that would fail to have new features like strike packages and weapon leveling, which is a marvelous idea. To the naysayers that call this game a "map pack" of the previous games -- how could they possibly be pleased? We have already seen developers change that which is flawless. Twice. World at War, Black Ops. Terrible. MW3 is exactly what I was hoping for with MW2. It's very similar MW2 minus the garbage that ruined MW2 -- the garbage that made CoD 4 better. Look at other franchises, such as Battlefield. They release new games that are no different from the previous. If I hadn't been a BF fan, I would have no idea that there was a difference between BF3 and BFBC2. They both play exactly the same and look almost identical.

Overall, I'm saddened that Black Ops and World at War fans have purchased this game. They wanted games similar to that - that try and be original and fix what is not broken. That is why there are so many 1 star ratings. MW3 is perfect in every way, shape, and form. True fans of Call of Duty 4 would understand this. Everyone else... they're rating a game 1 star simply because it stays true to its roots.

rating 1.0. Infinity Ward Rides its Wave of Success Long story short, this game is a step back from Modern Warfare 2.  I only played the game to finish up the MW3 story line.  The story line is okay, it went a little off the rails since MW2, but not quite as bad.  There were some emotional moments, and the ending is clear-cut and awesome.  Survival mode was a good update, and it's a lot of fun.  However, for a former $60 price tag, I would've never spent that much money on this game.  If you're playing this for the story, it's worth a rental.  If you're playing for the multiplayer alone, don't give your money to Activision or Infinity Ward.  Multiplayer is broken, and I don't mean a Black Ops "unbalanced guns, crappy server, and campers" broken, I mean a "holy crap, I fired an entire clip into that guy, he didn't die, and when I looked at the killcam, his screen didn't even turn red" broken.  If you hated the community in MW2, and even worse in Black Ops, this is the worse community yet. People exploit the lag compensation system by intentionally making their internet connection better by siphoning off yours, to gain an advantage.  People also do what by far is the most infuriating thing in COD history: dropshotting, or crouching down at Infinity Ward's super fast speed, and spamming bullets of a super fast firing weapon to give you no chance.  It's infuriating.  I've only played for a total of one consecutive day, and I eventually got fed up with this rotten community.  I seriously encourage you to do greater research on future COD games and decide whether its worth sixty dollars a year for these games.

rating 3.0. Eh This game took all the great things about Modern Warfare 2, and didn't really do anything with them. It also took all of the annoying things about Modern Warfare 2, and made them worse.

rating 5.0. Great Game! Short...but sweet! I got this game right when it came out off of Amazon by mail. The campaign is visually stunning with the landscapes in New York, Paris, and on a plane as it crash lands! The whole feel of the game is smoother compared to its predecessors in terms of weapon use and overall movement within the game. The graphics are basically the same with maybe minor improvement to Modern Warfare 2 which was acclaimed for it's top of the line graphics upgrade. Length of the campaign mode was just about as short if not shorter than it's predecessor. In all it probably took me 5 hours tops on Hardened Mode(Hardest!), but what it lacked in length it made up for with action packed drama Michael Bay couldn't match. Multiplayer, which is the main reason for buying this game, is revamped in terms of some weapons and perks, but is mostly a updated version of MW2. Note this isn't a bad thing though, if they changed it too much people I'm sure we'd get something resembling the failure of Call of Duty: Blackops multiplayer(there I said it!). Oh, and these new maps don't offer much in terms of sniping. They are all pretty much grounded or exposed sniping positions with a few exceptions, but I figured I'd note it. All and all I'm very satisfied with this game overall. Great end to an exciting series!!! Ooh-rah!!!

rating 5.0. Awesome game! If you've ever played a previous Call of Duty game and like it, this is not going to make you feel any different.  Fun campaign and awesome multiplayer that could keep you entertained forever.  Easily worth my money.

rating 4.0. Hey guys, this IS supposed to be a sequel... This game is as fun as the last 2. It is the same but I DONT MIND THAT. Graphics are a step better I think but maybe I'm seeing things. Action is fast and furious and forces you to really look at the battlefield to progress through the map behind objects and whatnot. I got a great deal on this game from Amazon so I didnt pay the 60 bucks only 31.99 thanks to a promotional credit. If I had to pay 60, I wouldve waited.

The only Con Ive got for this game is the campaign length. 5 hours? Come on not all of us spend all night on xbox live we've got to go to work in the AM lol! I play most games for the campaigns and these COD games are way to short. Should be at least 8 hours.

Overall great game maybe not worth the 60 bucks. If I was really into multiplayer it would have been well worth the 60 bucks.

rating 4.0. Up to Par MW3 does not disappoint. It is not ground breaking in anyway, it is in line with the COD tradition. Single player campaign is good. A little short in my opinion. Played on veteran and finished in about 8 hours. Good story. Black ops story was better. Veteran game play isn't what it used to be, used to be frustrating and extremely difficult and took weeks to finish due to frustration :) Now it is too easy. I liked the frustration and the joy of finishing.

Multiplayer is also not ground breaking, it is still just as good as other CODs. Great maps, good guns. Fun to play. Gives the game a HIGH replay value. You won;t get tired of playing MW3 with all the map packs and other extras planned for this year.

Worth a buy if you are a COD fan.

I only gave it 4 stars for an overall rating due to the short single player campaign, because I look forward to the single player. But over all the game has a ton of other things to do besides single player.

rating 5.0. Just buy it... MW2 was in my opinion the best war game ever, better then Black Ops. This game is MW2 but with New Guns, Maps, Perks have modified and are better, It's a sweet game and all you fools bashing it because it has not changed need to chill. Why fix what is not broken? It's a war game wars don't change just the weopons and locations do, that is what this game does. IT"S AWESOME! BUY IT!

current_product_reviews:
rating 4.0. You get what you pay for Not speaking in terms of value, but in that you are getting a 6-7 hour linear campaign in the vein of the Call of Duty moniker. To think it would be otherwise is basically naivete on the part of the consumer. It's always the populist mindset to root against the 800 pound gorilla in room...Yankees, Patriots, Lakers...and in video games, Call of Duty. But the product they put out is usually pretty consistent and predictable, albeit, they're basically blueprints of prior iterations. I'll just give you some of my perspective of Infinity Ward's versions starting from COD4. I only play single player so I'll give a review on the campaign only. I know, it's shocking to think people still play a game for the single player so to all those who say games like these should solely put out a multiplayer product, they would lose the revenue stream from players like myself.

COD4, in my opinion, was and maybe still is the best fps single-player on the market. It revolutionized the genre when it came out. It was short and linear, but was memorable due to its set-pieces (first time you fell out of the helicopter, the death of a protagonist from a nuclear explosion, the final shot at the end) and innovative missions (sniper, AC-130 and the extra spec op mission). Most games these days, you forget about entirely once you eject the disc. COD4's staying power is due to the fact the game stayed with you long after playing it.

MW2's campaign was a not unexpectedly a flaming turd. It was impossible to surpass its predecessor so they basically tried to shock you by bringing the war to U.S. soil and adding the stupid "No Russian" scene. They killed off so many protagonists that it became laughable and the scene from the Rock was just over the top. Unfortunately, shock value is not lasting and this game was instantly forgettable.

So what to do with the end of the trilogy? Of course you return to what brought you here and that's exactly what this feels like--COD4 re-imagined with parts of MW2. You get some sniper missions, an AC-130 mission, killed off another protagonist, dropped the Eiffel tower (rather flippantly too). Then they peppered in some parts from MW2 like the seal team mission, breaching rooms and the scene from Rio, but it's in another 3rd world country this time. And tried to shock you with civilian casualties again. This sounds like a lot of content within a short time-frame and it really is. I enjoyed it quite a lot and would say if you play a lot of single player games, then this one is right up there. This is probably a better game than COD4, but since it came 4 years after, I docked it a star for lack of originality.

It's a shame that all the 10 year old fanboys came out to review MW3 and Battlefield 3, thus skewing the overall score for each. If you're deciding between the two campaigns, this is the far superior one. (Didn't realize how long this got. Sorry for the length.)
other_product_reviews:
rating 4.0. Fun even though lacking innovation Campaign review:
The Gears of War series, to me, has been the only must-have exclusive on the 360 (never been a Halo fan). GOW3 is the reason why I bought a slim when my original 360 red ringed over a year ago. So it pains me to say that the campaign in 3 is rather a letdown. Gears 1 burst on the scene with such in-your-face testosterone action and innovation that it set the standard for 3rd person cover based combat. It introduced weapons like the lancer and hammer of dawn that will live on in gaming history. Gears 2 refined the gameplay of its predecessor and added a sense of scale to the war--it did what a sequel should--improved on an existing formula, but lacked in freshness. Now we are at Gears 3, the end of a trilogy, and it feels as such. Gameplay is a little more improved, but all innovation has been sucked out of the franchise. They've added new weapons but the lancer and hammer of dawn are still the showcase pieces. New enemies seem downright out of place in this game. They've lowered the testosterone level in this game by adding sentimental moments, which I feel is a mistake. The story is the weakest part of the game--very shallow with the majority of the game being fetch quests for fuel and equipment. Graphics are very good as you would expect, but feels too much like their last release "Bulletstorm", which left a bad taste in my mouth. It seems that they've dumbed down the series with each iteration, so play this on the most difficult setting.

With all this said, it is still a good game because you know what you're getting. You also get 4 player campaign co-op which is great for the co-op players amongst us. Of course you also have a huge multiplayer community featuring Horde 2.0, which was my only reason for going online in Gears 2. All in all, this is the game that I'll be returning to for the next few months so it was a great purchase for me. If you don't game online and only want to see how the story ends, just rent it or watch it on youtube.

rating 3.0. Just average unfortunately I'll start off by saying I loved Heavenly Sword. The story was good, all the characters were interesting, the fighting mechanic was different and the sixaxis moments gave it variety. I didn't mind that it was a short game because there was a lot of content packed in that time-frame.

With Enslaved, you have yet another 3rd person hacknslash that adds nothing new...I'd say they actually took a step backwards with the gameplay mechanic. The levels broken down are just repetitive "clear the area of enemies" and "climb on platform a to b to c" to advance to the next level where you do it all again. The point of view is too closed in, so you lose sight of enemies at times. The combat consists of button mashing, maybe dodge here and there. There are basically no puzzles in the game because each foot-hold is lit up so you know where to advance. But with all this said, an interesting story and colorful characters would have saved the game, and it has neither. I won't spoil the story, but the characters consist of an MMA fighter and a prototypical heroine. Some have said the facial expressions are amazing and that is true, but if that's the selling point for this game, then you may as well watch a movie, because I assure you that the facial expressions are much better on that medium, aside from the Twilight movies.

Bottom line...rent or buy/re-sell if possible.

rating 4.0. Single player review Based on all the average reviews, I wasn't expecting much, but the campaign in this game is surprisingly good.  The only thing wrong is that it is too short.  I usually have no gripes about a game's length because I actually prefer to be done with a game in 6-8 hrs.  I was done with the campaign in 2 sittings, probably 4-5 hrs in length.  The game plays more like the Call of Duty than the Bad Company series.  I'll probably pick it up once again when it's sub $20.

rating 5.0. Great Bargain Game Make no mistake, this doesn't feel like a low-budget game.  It has high production values equivalent to Resident Evil 5.  It's a shame that the game sold so poorly that they had to heavily discount it in order to move it...which is great for us consumers.  It plays very similar to the first, but the emphasis on co-op play is what makes this game stand out--you can play online with 3 others in defeating the ridiculous monsters in this game.  The game is a typical 3rd person shooter but the pace at which you move is different from what people are used to, which is probably why it didn't do too well.  This isn't a twitch shooter in the Call of Duty fashion.  When you first play it, you will immediately look for the run button because you move so sluggishly.  There isn't an aim button either which I think was a huge decision blunder.  You can zoom in and out by using the up and down on the D-pad, but that only changes the point of view and it is just not intuitive.  I recall reading the Rage interview in the latest Playstation magazine and one of the designers answered when asked about the games controls that if such and such millions of gamers bought Modern Warfare 2 and they're accustomed to the "X" button as jump, then you better make your "X" button jump (or something to that effect).  Well, if you play this game, you had better adjust to the control scheme which there are several options to choose from, but all aren't good.  If you go into the game with these caveats, you will more or less wind up enjoying it.

rating 4.0. It's a PS3, what else were you expecting Let's be honest. You're going to either buy one or not because you want to and not because of these reviews. I'll just note some items of interest from the perspective of a former xbox 360 owner.

*It's very compact and relatively quiet.
*My controller doesn't always sync up when I start it, which is kind of annoying.
*The interface is not too user friendly, this is still a video game console, right?
*The controller is smaller and layout is different.
*PS3 doesn't have a large community so it's more for a solitary experience.

I got this after my 360 red-ringed for the 2nd time, this time out of warranty. I love using the PS3 so far, but just know there are gremlins with this system because of all the technology that is packed in there. I have a feeling that it is not as durable for the long haul, but neither is the 360. (My friend actually bought 2 older PS3's for $600 each and those gave out in a little over a year.) I wish they would just make a console for gaming purposes like the older days...the ones where you could bash into or throw the controllers across the room or rip the cartridges out and not worry about whether it is going to work again or not, because they always did. But then again, the games these days are so dumbed down, that you'll rarely feel the need to.

rating 5.0. A hidden gem, not for casual gamers I have nothing new to add to the two review here--they are spot on. If you are a hard-core shoot em up fan, you already know about this game and its developer; if you are a casual gamer, you should stay away; but if you are somewhere in the middle and are interested in a challenging shmup, then I implore you to give this one a try. But make no mistake, you will die a lot because this game is difficult. I've played gradius, life force, raiden, and others back in the day, and I would consider those kiddie games compared to this. I've been playing for about an hour now and haven't gotten past the 2nd level--there are 5 total. The game rewards you with continues the more hours you play it, so you will have to play a lot of hours if you want to beat the game. This is one of those games that you can take pride in finishing if you ever finish it. Check out the numerous videos online and you will gain an appreciation for the game and the skills of the gamers.

A few notes:
*the graphics and controls are excellent, they did an excellent job porting it
*playing with the analog thumb stick was fine for me
*you die with one hit in this game, it is unforgiving towards mistakes, but you can absorb enemy bullets if you align the polarity of your ship and it is this simple premise that makes the game so elegant in design
*this game is fair; if you die, it is because of the player so you won't feel cheated and throw your controller across the room
*you will see a lot of bullets on the screen so if you're not good at dodging bullets, you should try something else

rating 1.0. Are you kidding me?!? OK. I guess I was trying to get a way with a cheap battery and the cable. But should have known.... Guess it worked for some, but it didn't for me.
First, the cable did not work well. I connected directly to my controller, and I didn't see power coming into my controller. So I pressed the connector hard into my controller, and only then I saw power coming into my controller. But if I move the controller, then it would lose power again. It seemed like the connector to the controller portion didn't quite fit correctly, and power pins in teh connector was not making a good connection.

Second, the battery never charged up. Period. After going through the pain with the cable, I connected the cable, put it on the table carefully, so that the controller was getting the power. Went to sleep, and the next day, I checked the battery. The controller got the power from the XBOX the whole night, but when I tried the battery without the cable, no charge (actually the light blinked for 1-2 seconds, then went dimming and died).

I was so pissed, since I wasted so many hours, trying to make it work. I returned it (although it was really cheap, I wanted my money back - no free money for this garbage).
So I went to Gamestop and bought a used pack for three times the price I paid for this garbage... and happy with it.

rating 5.0. Really short cord but still an amazing deal. My legit Xbox charge pack and cord broke out of the blue, the cord because I always had it plugged in and the battery pack because... Hmm, I don't know because i never used it on charge....

Anyways, I was looking for a low cost solution and this is exactly that, the quality of this product seems fine but there are two things I've noticed...

1. The cord is very short, 6 feet or so. Which means its fine for charging next to the xbox or if you sit close to your tv, but don't expect anything close to the kind of range that the "real" charge cable provides... = This is still worth it for the price.

2. The battery fits very snug into the controller. Unlike my "real" charge pack that could pop out easily when I push the button, this charge pack feels like it would stay in place fairly well even without the button. This is a plus for people that leave the battery in all the time, but it might be annoying if you like to use an external charger every night.

Other than those to things this charge pack is perfect. I'd buy this time and time again over spending full price for the "real" xbox ripoff brand, and from what I can tell, it holds a charge even better than the "real" power pack.

Hope this review helped!

input: rating 2.0. Seriously Disapointed For the First Time Since 2000 Been playing the Sims since Sims 1, even paid to play Sims Online for nearly 4yrs... I really don't see any improvement with this most recent incarnation of the Sims games.  For $80 we don't even get a guide book?  Keep your special 'gift' of a little book of art and the Sims music CD (yeah that's going to get alot of use) and give us a damn guide book for free instead of charging another $20 for it.  So what do we get.. new and improved .. well.. nothing.  Sure there are some new items but the interactions are boring and limited.  They take away an amazing open world play system and give us 'mini-hoods' within worlds.  If you consider a 'hood' a couple of houses then ok you have a few 'hoods' per world.  No more buying books or food.. that I can find anyway.  And even with the 'open (yeah right) travel in the 'mini-hoods' you still can't even go into or out of a public building without an in game loading screen.  Yeah they are only maybe 15 or 20 secs long but the point is why do we need to load the 'bar' when it literally consists of one bar, a fireplace and some tables and chairs.  I had all EP's from Sims 3 and sure you had lag once and awhile but I was still able to play on mostly high settings.  Sims 4 brags that it was bringing us such new and fantastic 'next gen' stuff... where the hell is it??  They even screwed up the customization of Sims and items.  Love that shirt that only comes in blue, white and brown.. but want to wear it with the cool skirt.. so sorry that only comes in green stripe and purple.  No color wheel.. very VERY limiting.  You can't even see the limited customizations because everything is in a tiny little thumb screen shot, if you don't like it you have to go back to the purchase tab. I'm sorry but I've invested years and probably thousands of dollars with all the series and EP's for each, Sims Online and upgrades with comps and graphics cards, so to be tossed this over priced bare bones 'it's better because they have emotion pop ups' version.. sucks. I'm not seeing the 'more' promised.

rating 1.0. Not worth the money The only thing I enjoy about this game is that the Sims look less cartoonish than the Sims 3.

Things I dislike:
-The neighborhoods are so tiny. I ended up deleting everything just I could have spots to build houses.
-No way to bulldoze lots from the neighbor hood screen, I had to enter each lot to bulldoze it.
-Edge scrolling hardly ever works for me. When it does work it's slow and laggy.
-Not enough color choices for furniture. I spent more time looking for pieces I liked that had other pieces to match than I did actually playing the game. I don't need to be able to color everything in any design but I felt like there were not enough choices.
-The only emotions my sims actually experienced were "Fine" and "Sleeping". No matter what I did they stayed the same.

rating 3.0. Alright but still a lot of room for growth I'm amending a previous review because of a patch that EA released on day one that fixes several issues. So I'm breaking this review down into pros and cons.

Pros:

-Much improved gameplay performance, load times, and build mode. Gameplay is fluid and easy to get the hang of. Sims have more depth than they did in previous iterations.

Cons:

-Tutorials explode on your screen prompting you to do everything under the sun when all you want to do is play the game; useful feature for beginners new to the series, terribly annoying for veterans who just want to get on with it (no toggle option). CAS and build/buy has very limited color schemes, matching sets of furniture can be a terrible nightmare at times. Cheats suck, motherload and refill motives only useful ones, no "unlock career rewards" no "change relationship status", nothing. This game lacks a LOT of content from The Sims 3 and The Sims 2, though I expect most of that to be filled with expansions.

Overall: Decent game but unfinished. EA should have waited prior to release.

rating 5.0. Another great Sims game! I have own every Sims game in the franchise, each one better than the last, and this is no exception. The Sims 3 was my absolute favorite because it seemed so realistic, but The Sims 3 was extremely laggy, I have extremely high specs on my computer and it still was laggy even when I lowered the in game settings. The Sims 4 runs so smooth! I've been playing it for over 4 hours and haven't had any kind of lag at all. There is even a 'laptop mode' in the in-game settings! While I'm a little upset about the things missing in the game, which was announced months ago what wasn't going to be in the game, I still think it's a great game. I really love the fact that in The Sims 4, your sims can multitask (they can eat and watch tv at the same time, they can work out on a machine and watch the cooking channel and level their cooking skill up. The emotion aspect of the game is a nice touch and it really effects the sims mood and their actions. The get special interactions depending on the emotion they are feeling. People are saying they are disappointed in the lack of content included in the base game, but this is the most content that has even been released in a base game. They have made it cc (custom content) and mod friendly, which is awesome because I love putting mods and custom content in my game to make it even more realistic! I am definitely glad I bought this and I'm really looking forward to the expansion and stuff packs EA releases in the future!!!

rating 2.0. Time to pin our hopes on The Sims 5 The rumor is that The Sims 4 began life as a multiplayer online game a la Second Life, and it appears to be true going by the released game. We haven't had such small neighborhoods since the original Sims and the non-young adult age groups feel like an afterthought. Some of the new tools included in build mode and create-a-sim are impressive, but they quickly feel limited with the lack of create-a-style or even a color wheel, and absurd limits on the number of buildable levels. These limitations make sense when you think of the Sims 4 as an online game, where only a small area can be loaded at once, too many levels wouldn't work for the weakest of computers, and create-a-style would slow everything to a crawl as the computer would have had to download dozens of textures. Still, the Sims 4 is reasonably fun to play for a little while, though it doesn't feel like a firm base for 5+ years of expansion packs.

The Good:

The overall look of the sims is much improved over previous versions. The neighborhoods are beautiful, if frustratingly inaccessible.

New tools in build mode.

The ability to search through the game catalog is a great improvement over the cumbersome collections of Sims 2 and 3.

The Bad:

Loading screens everywhere you look. They're quick at the moment, but I doubt they'll stay that way as expansion and stuff packs are piled on.

You are required to run Origin in the background while the game is running. You don't need to be online, but it has to be open.

No pools, no toddler life stage, and infants are little more than the objects they were in the original Sims game.

The Irritating:

Careers are ridiculous rather than reality-based, and on top of that they've regressed to Sims 1 and 2 style friend-requirements for promotions. Add in that there's no aspiration reward to prevent relationship decay and you may find that you spend a lot of time meeting little goals instead of enjoying the sand box.

Neighbors like to wander into your sim's house without being invited and try to use the bathroom while your sim is showering. Then they're all stuck in such a state of embarrassment that it's difficult to make them do anything useful until it passes.

Sims can no longer eat the fruit they pick from wild plants.

Overall as someone who's played the Sims series for years, the Sims 4 is not what I had hoped it would be. The two largest draws for the Sims 3, the open neighborhood and create-a-style, are noticeably absent. Graphics are much improved, but that much is to be expected with a reboot. Gameplay is smooth, but it would have to be given how little the engine seems to be doing. I'd say that all the Sims 4 needed to make it a great game was another year or so of development so that they could have at least included the missing toddlers, fixed up the babies, made the worlds five times as large as they are, and included pools, which have been in the game since the beginning, but ultimately the problem with the Sims 4 is that they tried to use as much of the original online-only game as they could, and with such a lousy foundation the game is already locked into a style that seems very contrary to the spirit of past Sims games.

rating 5.0. Sims 4 is better than others make it out to be I normally don't write reviews, but when I do its because a product is unjustly rated. New and improved mechanics in the game are wonderful! I love that as my sim is cooking a meal, they can have a xonversation with anothersim in the room and you can control the conversation how you want. Actions of sims are fluid and seem more realistic than any of the previous instalments.

In closing, if you enjoyed playing previous Sims games then I would recomend picking 4 up. It really is a fantastic game and you can appreciate the time and love the creators put into making this a truly awesome game.

rating 3.0. A moodsetter for previous "Sims" titles I went into "The Sims 4" with the same level of excitement as I have with all of the previous "Sims" base games since the series' inception in 2000. I had been closely following the game's development since it was announced last year and I was very excited to see what it would have to offer.

I am not an "EA hater." My review is also not a grievance about the lack of pools and toddlers that I have seen widely circulating the web. No, my review is strictly about my impressions of this title. As I have seen a few other people say, "I really wanted to like you, The Sims 4."

I'm okay with getting virtually the same kinds of expansion packs for each era of "Sims" games. By this point, I have come to expect an expansion that allows them to go on vacation, plan outfits around the seasons, cast spells, and even shoot for stardom. So, I'm still holding hope that things will get better.

However, what I'm not okay with is a base game that feels stripped further down than base games of previous renditions. Otherwise, I feel like we're not making any progress. This doesn't mean that I expect all of the features from "Sims 3" + its expansions in this game. But I do expect some substantial new base feature(s) to be in a new base game. In the case of "Sims 4", I do not see anything new that is substantial.

The revitalized "Create-A-Sim" mode is nice and allows for more unique-looking Sims. In my opinion, a step-up from "Sims 3". However, I don't consider this a substantial feature alone. Personally, I do not spend the majority of my time creating Sims. Perhaps some do, but not me. I make a Sim family, move them in, and then play the game the rest of the time.

That's where the lacking of new features comes in. I'm okay with load screens. I'm okay with "closed-world". However, there's just nothing much to do with Sims after they're made. I felt like I had much more to do with Sims in the base version of "Sims 3". As others have mentioned, this title feels similar to "Sims 2" when it was at its base. However, at that time, "Sims 2" was a much more novel 3D experience - a step up from the isometric first series.
Even then, I feel like "Sims 2" had more to offer than this title does. Both are "closed-world", both have load screens, both have neighborhood community lots. However, "Sims 2" had more depth to it on a variety of spectrums - grocery shopping, more phone services, adoption, more emphasis on aspirations, a more engaging neighborhood. In "Sims 4", you can visit the neighborhood, meet neighbors, get a job, build skills, and that's about it.

In "Sims 4", the primary new feature is the emotion system. It's not a bad thought, but it stands alone in an auditorium of potential that is unfortunately not tapped into. I am hoping that things will get better from here with future expansions to give sims some more activities to do, but as of right now I am getting bored.

Visually, the game looks nice. It's similar to "Sims 3" with a few enhancements. Also, performance is much improved and gone is the very long initial neighborhood load from "Sims 3". I have a high-end machine, and can get from desktop to a household in less than 60 seconds. I'm sure lower-end machines will see the performance boost relative to their experience with "Sims 3."

Returning to the title of this review, I have found myself thinking "I wish I could adopt a baby" or "I wish I could go to the city hall to apply for a job", etc. while playing "Sims 4". I usually take a few days of playing to get back into a game of "Sims" after I have stopped for a while. So, in my few days of playing this title I have found myself getting back into playing "Sims". Only, I would rather be playing a previous title in the series. So, this title very much has set the mood for another "Sims 1", "Sims 2" or "Sims 3" binge for me.

Don't knock it completely though. Let's give the game a few more months to get patched and have some expansions made. It may feel wrong to pay $60 USD for this experience right now, but, quoting a few other reviewers, "It's an investment." Let's hope it pays off. 3 stars (for now).
